Briefe an Jeanne Lampl-de Groot 1921–1939 [Sigmund Freud—Letters to Jeanne Lampl-de Groot 1921–1939], by Sigmund Freud

Lothar Springmann

Lothar Springmann

Tübingen
Burgholzweg 40

Lothar Springmann   lospri48@aol.com

The volume edited by the Dutch psychoanalyst Gertie F. Bögels includes an additional chapter of Freud’s approximately 11,000 extant letters, which are now available in the German edition with a postscript by Joachim Danckwardt. From the correspondence with Lampl-de Groot from 1921 to 1939, only 76 letters from Freud have survived. The themes and subject matter with which Lampl-de Groot addressed Freud can therefore only be inferred from Freud’s answers. However, Bögels’ biographical details about Lampl-de Groot, and some skilfully selected This article was originally published with errors, which have now been corrected in the online version. responseSet.title: A forgotten Freudian: The passion of Karl Stern by Daniel Burston
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A forgotten Freudian: The passion of Karl Stern by Daniel Burston

Kevin Kelly

Kevin Kelly

New York
NY
USA

Kevin Kelly   kevinvkellymd@gmail.com

As a subject for biography, the psychoanalyst Karl Stern (1906–1975) presents some intriguing possibilities, and some daunting challenges. He converted *This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher.1240 from his family's Judaism to a fervent embrace of Catholicism, and announced this conversion in a published spiritual autobiography, while insisting on his continued identity as both a Jew and a psychoanalyst, and he argued that, when properly understood, psychoanalysis and Catholicism were perfectly compatible. Furthermore, despite being widely hailed by some and sharply criticized by others in the period from the publication of his first book in 1950 to his death in 1975, he is largely unknown today. He thus offers some rich paradoxes to explore, and provides abundant material about his own psychic state to help with this exploration. In this volume, Daniel Burston tries to explain both how Stern arrived at his idiosyncratic views on psychoanalysis and religion, and how he came to be forgotten so quickly. These efforts are usually thought‐provoking, though not always successful.

Burston's work pursues several agendas simultaneously, and therein lie some difficulties. It can be read as a psychological biography, as a treatise on religious identity in general and on Jewish‐Catholic relations in particular, or as an endorsement of a particular strain in psychiatric and psychoanalytic theory. These efforts run concurrently for the most part, and the text does not integrate them as much as it simply alternates among them.

As a biography, this work might be expected to fit neatly in the genre of psychohistory; the author is a psychologist, the subject is a psychoanalyst who had two analyses of his own and who left extensive written accounts of his own psychology, and the theoretical sections draw heavily on the works of Erik Erikson. However, apart from the effort to explain Stern's religious conversion, the biography offers surprisingly little in the way of psychological insight. The most striking example of this omission concerns Stern's relationship with his mother. The discussion of his early life focuses almost entirely on his relationship with his father and his father's family; the only references to his mother concern her distance from her own Jewish origins (evidenced by her decorating a Christmas tree every year and by her unease with his temporary embrace of orthodox Judaism as an adolescent), her chronic ill health, and her death when Karl was 19. This omission appears particularly striking later in the story, when we learn that Stern's effort to join psychoanalysis to religion rested on his assertion that the essence of each lay in a maternal dimension, which he argued that both psychoanalytic and religious leaders undervalued.

responseSet.title: Reading Klein by Margaret Rustin and Michael Rustin
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Klein by Margaret Rustin and Michael Rustin

Kay Long

Kay Long

New Haven
CT

Kay Long   kay.long@yale.edu

Margaret Rustin and Michael Rustin have written a gem of a book. Reading Klein is an erudite and lucid guide through the collected works of one of the great original minds in psychoanalysis. Through the use of extended quotations, much of the text is in Melanie Klein’s own words. Rustin and Rustin accompany these carefully chosen quotations with interpretive commentary that orients us to each exerpt’s place in Klein’s overall body of work; elaborates her extension of, and at times departure from, Freud; elucidates the cultural and historical context in which she developed her ideas; and indicates how and by whom her concepts have been developed over time. All in fewer than 200 pages. Anyone interested in an in-depth reading of the body of Klein’s work from her first published paper to her last need look no further. Reading Klein is a work of serious scholarship that offers immense clinical utility, and is a pleasure to read.

While interest in Kleinian theory and clinical technique has burgeoned in many parts of the world in recent years, I suspect that most analysts interested in a Kleinian approach have educated themselves primarily through the works of her successors—the first generation of Bion, Winnicott, Rosenfeld, Segal, Joseph, and Meltzer, or contemporary Kleinians such as O'Shaughnessy, Britton, Steiner, and Feldman, to name only a few. Not many will have spent much time with Klein’s actual writings, beyond a handful of her better-known papers. And fewer still will have taken the trouble to read all four volumes of her collected works. Reading Rustin and Rustin’s book will convince you—whether you are coming to Klein for the first time or are steeped in her work—that reading Klein is worth the trouble, and reading her with their lively and scholarly accompaniment is even better.

Some analysts may have been dissuaded from reading Klein because she has a reputation as a dry, dense writer. But Rustin and Rustin have done a masterful job of choosing selections from Klein’s large body of work that convey her thinking at its clearest and most coherent. At one point they quote Klein’s description of the child’s efforts in the depressive position to overcome splitting. According to Klein, the child struggles to figure out how to

… put the bits together in the right way and at the right time; how to pick out the good bits and do away with the bad ones; how to bring the object to life when it has been put together. (p. 58)

responseSet.title: An analysis of the evolution of delusion in a Chinese case of paraphrenia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An analysis of the evolution of delusion in a Chinese case of paraphrenia

Tao Zhang

Tao Zhang

Paris
France
Department of Psychoanalysis

Tao Zhang   CONTACT Tao Zhang pollus@sina.com Paris 8 University, 2 rue de la Liberté, Paris 93526, France

Paraphrenia, mnemic trace, writing, ego defect

This article proposes a psychoanalytic approach to interpret the work of Song Shipeng, especially starting from an interrogation on the function of the nomination in the evolution of his delusion. Song was an author suffering from paraphrenia; he published some books writing about his conversations with extraterrestrials and their theory of the Universe. Furthermore, in the last years of his life, he practised a pseudo-medicine, as part of his practice of cosmic energy. His works are full of traces of his personal experience, which open the door to the understanding of the evolution of his delusion. The unifying factor of this examination will be the following hypothesis: Song attempted to compensate for his psychotic crisis, which he regarded as a way to install his “Pseudo-Ego Ideal,” to invent all the delusion by the mnemic traces and to rebuild an identity. Within this perspective, writing could be viewed as enabling the patient to fight against psychotic anxiety, which could take the form of delusion of imagination, of naming oneself, of making a link between his psychotic enigmatic experience, his writing and his thought, and finally, of compensating his ego defect.

responseSet.title: I sensi e l'inconscio [Truth and the unconscious in psychoanalysis] by Giuseppe Civitarese
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

I sensi e l'inconscio [Truth and the unconscious in psychoanalysis] by Giuseppe Civitarese

Fulvio Mazzacane

Fulvio Mazzacane

Pavia
Italy

Fulvio Mazzacane   fulvio.mazzacane@gmail.com

It is appropriate and at the same time reductive to say that Giuseppe Civitarese's latest book is about psychoanalysis. True on the one hand, since it is a book which deals with psychoanalysis in that – both from a theoretical *This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher.1231 viewpoint and in the consulting room – it presents the field model in a fertile coupling with the thinking of Bion; reductive on the other hand, in that artistic and philosophical thoughts and images are frequently grafted into its fabric, constantly broadening the discussion. So this is a book which shows ‘in action’ Bion's idea that there is a transference between ideas and not just between human beings.

The fundamental characteristic of man as protagonist in Civitarese's reflections is that of possessing a mind and being self‐aware, which obliges him to equip himself with an instrument that will enable him to absorb, metabolize and transform instincts and external stimuli into thought. The analytic situation is the place where the small or large holes in this function are probed in vivo by the analytic couple in an attempt to create a narrative with meaning.

There is always a precarious equilibrium in the mind of man between a vital impulse to search, which constantly leads him to call himself into question, and a need for the continuity that is given by fixed points of reference.

The search for this difficult equilibrium has an inevitable impact on the way in which Civitarese understands the analytic journey and its goal. The objective of the analytic journey is no longer that of deciphering the unconscious, but of expanding it, increasing the capacity of the couple to tolerate ambiguity and its creative richness, and of dreaming together.

Slash

The symbol which represents the book is the slash, which evokes Bion's idea of transcending the caesura. The binary pair of concepts which structure traditional psychoanalytic knowledge are placed by Civitarese in constant dialectical tension. In his work, this becomes a style that never leads to sharp contradistinctions, but to the placing of semi‐permeable barriers between concepts, allowing some classical concepts of psychoanalysis to be revitalized.

responseSet.title: “The Unconscious in Translation” (Jonathan House general editor)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Unconscious in Translation” (Jonathan House general editor)

Claire Nouvet

Claire Nouvet

Atlanta
GA
USA
Emory University, Department of French and Italian

Claire Nouvet   cnouvet@emory.edu

The ‘Unconscious in Translation’ (UIT), the new book series expertly guided by general editor Jonathan House, fills several significant lacunas within Anglophone psychoanalytic literature. It was initially created to make available English translations of Jean Laplanche's writings. Since Laplanche (who studied with Lacan but distanced himself from him) is a major figure of Francophone psychoanalysis, UIT's commitment to the translation of his works is an important and most welcome undertaking. Written with great rigor and clarity, Laplanche's contribution indeed stands out as one of the most original Francophone contributions ‘after Lacan.’ UIT, however, is not limited to the translation of Laplanche's writings, but also includes the translation of “other important psychoanalytic thinkers whose work remains mostly untranslated into English.” Because of this twin mission, UIT is a crucial initiative, making available to an Anglophone audience a wide array of Francophone psychoanalytical thought.

*This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher.1245

responseSet.title: Freuds Traum von einer neuen Wissenschaft. Chemie und Bakteriologie im Traum von Irmas Injektion. Johann Georg Reicheneder
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freuds Traum von einer neuen Wissenschaft. Chemie und Bakteriologie im Traum von Irmas Injektion. Johann Georg Reicheneder

Wolfgang Hegener

Wolfgang Hegener

Wolfgang Hegener   w.hegener@t-online.de

The dream of Irma's injection, that first prototypical psychoanalytic dream that Freud had in the night of 23 to 24 July 1895 and that he accorded a central place, with a paradigmatic interpretation, in his Interpretations of Dreams, is probably the most debated dream in the entire history of psychoanalysis. It seems that this dream has such a distinctive and ultimately indefinable quota of condensation that the interpretive possibilities are almost endless. The publication of Reicheneder's book presented here represents a special moment in the course of these interpretations: never yet, in my opinion, has Freud's dream been so thoroughly and carefully examined and interpreted in terms of both the history of science and psychoanalysis. Reicheneder is therefore fulfilling for the time being a desideratum of research dating right back to the early 1950s, formulated by Erik H. Erikson, which is that the dream's numerous striking references to specific diseases, somatic configurations and medical methods remain to be understood. Yet Reicheneder's medical-historical reconstruction is not pursuing a merely antiquarian interest; it is embedded in the precise analysis of Freud's specific relational constellations and psychic situation at the time of the dream—and on the way Reicheneder sheds a surprising light on the preconditions for the creative founding of a science in general. His method draws closely on the procedure of dream interpretation developed by Freud: he interprets and contextualises the individual elements of the manifest dream and its associations, which refer to medical research—these are the concepts of injection, infection and dysentery, as well as tuberculosis, diphtheria and pyaemia—not in a general way but in detail.

Reicheneder begins by explaining the kind of progress that is connected with the interpretation of the dream of Irma's injection. Freud was then able to show that with the method of free association in principle every dream can be systematically interpreted. The evidence for the validity of such a strictly methodically conducted procedure was Freud's entry ticket for his therapeutic procedure, henceforth called psychoanalysis, into the usual canon of scientific medical research. He had succeeded in claiming his hitherto tentatively developed therapeutic procedure also as a general research method accessible to every interpreter (not only scientific experts). Whereas to that date the methodical procedure had not been learnable and more intensive instruction in it had only been obtainable from Freud, now he was able to envisage a procedure that could be understood and replicated. This enormous progress was associated 1473

Sara de Arbiser

Sara de Arbiser   Email: arbisersara@hotmail.com

Drives and Cultural Development, by Yako Román Adissi, with a prologue by Víctor Korman, is divided into ten chapters: I Introduction; II The Oedipus Complex, a Driver of Human Development; III What is a Representation, and How Does It Materialize?; IV Freud and Culture; V Why Sexual Morality and not Sexual Ethics?; VI Thinking with Freud; VII How is Thought Generated?; VII Violence: A Manifestation of Perversion; IX Transference Neurosis: One of the Vicissitudes of the Death Drive; and X Narcissus and Oedipus in the Psychosexual Evolution of the Libido.

We recognize in this valuable text a very thorough reading of Freudian and post-Freudian works, as well as numerous observations drawn from other scientific and literary sources. Along with Laplanche, Pontalis, Ferenczi, Piaget, Klein, and Lacan, many Argentine psychoanalysts have a strong presence: Garma, Cesio, Abuchaem, Racker, Zukerfeld, Hagelin, Braier, Dio Bleichmar, Nasio, and Faimberg. We also find a great number of renowned literary authors and philosophers. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La realidad, el sujeto y el objeto [Reality, the subject and the object], by Leonardo Peskin

Jaime Szpilka

Jaime Szpilka

Madrid
Spain

Jaime Szpilka   jszpilka@inicia.es

Leonardo Peskin's book is a commendable, intelligent, thorough, and generous attempt to bring together and clarify—in a manner both simple and profound—such thorny, complex issues as reality, the subject, and the object. He addresses such a large number of issues that it is nearly impossible to dwell on all of them. Also, what is so strikingly interesting is the collection of highly complex and refined Lacanian developments, narrated in his own language and from his own perspective, and always informed by the infallible compass of Freudian thought. As such, I would define Peskin's text as a model of Freudo-Lacanianism, in the best sense of the term. Perhaps this is why the title itself does not mention the Real, but speaks instead of reality, the subject, and the object. In a way, he draws on a particular moment and reading of Lacan himself, wherein the Real is not taken as a particular Point de capiton, but as one of the dit-mensions of the signifier in its particular relation to lack. It is in this sense that I would like to emphasise that the real is not what cannot be spoken, but rather what cannot be spoken because it is spoken, with all of the implications this entails.

In the introduction, the author states that reality, whatever it may be, is fantasmatic, and that, from a Lacanian perspective, the fantasm is what helps to conceptualise and sustain Freudian reality, basically considered in its development as psychic reality. This, Peskin adds, brings us to discuss if multiple realities, such as historical and material reality, are also subject to the same fantasmatic configuration, although he notes that, in order to organise these multiple realities, the work of subjectivity is required, which is basically constituted as the symbolic fulfils its function of separating the imaginary from the real. Peskin is clearly committed to abolishing any flat or plain dimensions of objectivity.

As we are dealing with a homonym, it is important to clarify what type of reality we are referring to, what differentiates the two terms, and what they have in common. Peskin expressly 546

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La formulación psicodinámica de caso. Su valor para la práctica clínica [Psychodynamic case formulation: Its value for clinical practice]**

Andrea Rodríguez Quiroga

Andrea Rodríguez Quiroga

Buenos Aires
Argentina

Andrea Rodríguez Quiroga   Email: andrearodriguezquiroga@gmail.com

The book includes contributions from Luján Alsina, Juan José Dapueto, Ana Laura Hernández, Alejandra Laxague, Rodolfo Moguillansky, Ana *This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher and hence differs in appearance to other papers in this issue.266 Mokszanski, Gabriela Montado, Silvia Nussbaum, Elena Turim, and Luis Villalba.

One of this book's key achievements is that it has filled a gap in the psychodynamic literature in Spanish. As its title suggests, it introduces the psychodynamic case formulation (PCF) and its uses in clinical psychotherapy. In the last chapter, Miller and Altmann de Litvan (p. 384) discuss the meaning and appropriateness of this term when they depict the current status of the field of psychotherapy in the River Plate. More specifically, they outline the criticisms directed against psychoanalysis, which have questioned its rigidity, duration, and even effectiveness. These authors argue that such criticisms are tied to social changes that require us to rethink therapeutic theories and techniques. La formulación psicodinámica de caso addresses this challenge by presenting a perspective on psychodynamic clinical practice that is informed by research, patient-centered, and clear about therapists’ responsibility. The goal is to benefit the patient and, more broadly, to further develop the discipline and its practice.

In the same line, it should be stressed, as Bernardi does in the introduction (p. 17), that the book's approach places psychoanalysts among psychotherapists of all schools, for while the focus here is on the formulation of the psychodynamic case, there are other theoretical perspectives that also use case formulation. The latter is a way of conceptualizing and presenting clinical cases. Its basic goal is to increase the probability that a therapeutic intervention will be useful for the patient by choosing the most suitable approach for this particular case. It can, therefore, arouse any practitioner's interest (McWilliams, 1999).

responseSet.title: Questions and worries: On the correspondence between Grete Bibring and Anna Freud 1949–1975
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Questions and worries: On the correspondence between Grete Bibring and Anna Freud 1949–1975**

Nina Bakman

Nina Bakman

Nina Bakman   CONTACT Nina Bakman banina@bluewin.ch

Grete Bibring (1899–1977) was a representative of the second generation of analysts. Having emigrated from Vienna to London in 1938, she left for Boston in 1941 where she made a remarkable career. In 1946, she became head of the department of psychiatry at the Beth Israel Hospital at Harvard and, from 1961, the first woman professor of medicine there. She maintained a connection with European psychoanalysis in the person of Anna Freud, with whom she corresponded regularly. Their letters contain an interesting exchange of ideas about psychoanalytic institutions (e.g. the American Psychoanalytical Association) and papers (e.g. on pregnancy). It is also the testimony of an exceptional friendship.

Grete Bibring (1899–1977) est l’une des représentantes de la deuxième génération d’analystes. Ayant émigré de Vienne à Londres en 1938, en 1942, elle partit à Boston où elle mena une carrière remarquable. En 1946, elle devint directrice du service de psychiatrie du Beth Israel Hospital de Harvard et, à partir de 1961, la première femme à y devenir professeur de médecine. Elle maintint des liens avec la psychanalyse européenne en la personne d’Anna Freud, avec laquelle elle entretint une correspondance régulière. Leurs lettres renferment un échange d’idées intéressant au sujet des institutions psychanalytiques (par exemple, l’Association américaine de psychanalyse) et d’articles (par exemple, sur la grossesse). Cette correspondance témoigne également d’une amitié exceptionnelle.

Zusammenfassung: Grete Bibring (1899–1977) ist eine Repräsentantin der zweiten Analytiker Generation. 1938 von Wien nach London emigriert, ging sie 1942 nach Boston, wo sie eine bemerkenswerte Karriere machte. 1946 wurde sie Leiterin der Psychiatrischen Abteilung des Beth Israel Krankenhauses in Harvard und 1961 ebendort die erste Professorin für Medizin. Sie hielt eine Beziehung zur europäischen Psychoanalyse in der Person von Anna Freud aufrecht, mit der sie regelmäßig korrespondierte. Der Briefwechsel enthält einen interessanten Gedankenaustausch über psychoanalytische Institutionen (z. B. die American Psychoanalytic Association) und Arbeiten (z. B. zur Schwangerschaft). Er ist auch Zeugnis einer außergewöhnlichen Freundschaft.

Grete Bibring (1899–1977) appartenne alla seconda generazione di analisti. Emigrata da Vienna a Londra nel 1938, si trasferì dopo quattro anni a Boston, dove fece una brillante carriera: nel 1946 fu infatti nominata direttrice del dipartimento di psichiatria al Beth Israel Hospital di Harvard e successivamente, nel 1961, divenne la prima donna ad ottenere una cattedra di medicina in quella stessa sede. Mantenne un legame con la psicoanalisi europea nella persona di Anna Freud, con cui ebbe una regolare corrispondenza. Il loro epistolario contiene un interessante scambio di idee sulle istituzioni psicoanalitiche (ad esempio sulla American Psychoanalytic Association) oltre che su paper psicoanalitici (ad es. sulla gravidanza). Questo corpus di lettere costituisce inoltre la testimonianza concreta di un’eccezionale amicizia.

Grete Bibring (1899–1977) fue una de las representantes de la segunda generación de psicoanalistas. Luego de emigrar de Viena a Londres en 1938, partió a Boston en 1942, donde tuvo una destacada carrera. En 1946 se convirtió en jefe del departamento de psiquiatría del Hospital Beth Israel en Harvard y, a partir de 1961, fue la primera mujer profesora de medicina en dicha institución. Mantuvo una conexión con el psicoanálisis europeo mediante una correspondencia regular con Anna Freud. Sus cartas contienen un interesante intercambio de ideas sobre instituciones psicoanalíticas (por ejemplo, la Asociación Psicoanalítica Americana) y artículos (por ejemplo, sobre la gestación). También es testimonio de una amistad excepcional.

responseSet.title: Psicologia Dinamica, by G. Amadei, D. Cavanna, and G.C. Zavattini
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psicologia Dinamica, by G. Amadei, D. Cavanna, and G.C. Zavattini

Paola Marion and Adam Elgar

Adam Elgar

Roma

Adam Elgar  

The volume Psicologia Dinamica [Dynamic Psychology] by Amadei, Cavanna and Zavattini, published in printed form by Il Mulino Strumenti (2015) and also via the digital platform Pandora Campus, constitutes a publishing undertaking that is not common in the psychoanalytic literary panorama. It is, in fact, a handbook addressed to students of the Psychology Degree Course at the University of Rome—the authors, besides being psychoanalysts, are all university professors, the first two of Dynamic Psychology, Zavattini of the Psychopathology of Couple and Parental Relations—a handbook conceived along particularly innovative lines compared with traditional manuals, and for this reason alone worthy of special attention.

The text, which has contributions from other figures coordinated by the authors, not only offers a broad and detailed panorama of psychoanalytic theory and its developments, but does so by adopting a specific theoretical and clinical approach which reflects the authors’ scientific position and orients the reading of the various chapters which address the broad range of dynamic psychology and its history. Indeed, right from the introduction the authors declare their “positioning within the intersubjective perspective which emphasises the theme of a two-way exchange between processes of internalisation and the external world as a general frame of reference” (p. 11). This “vertex” runs through the text like a fil rouge and defines its configuration, a configuration which does not re-tread the expository model of the traditional handbook, but rather responds to an “experimental” approach in which the scientific angle is strong and governs both the authors’ choices and the form in which they address the various topics. The authors make this clear in declaring that “this handbook is also an experiment” (p. 11), placing a continual emphasis on the scientific framework they have chosen. The very use of the term “dynamic psychology” tends to underline the less than complete consonance between the classical psychoanalytic paradigm, which nevertheless remains the solid core on which their model is based, and the developments and expansions which this paradigm has undergone thanks to the growth of clinical research (attachment theory, infant research) and in the light of contributions from the neurosciences.

responseSet.title: England–France–South America: Melanie Klein's correspondence (1951–1960)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

England–France–South America: Melanie Klein's correspondence (1951–1960)

Alejandro Dagfal

Alejandro Dagfal

Buenos Aires
Argentina
Historian, Psychologist, Professor of History of Psychology, Universidad de Buenos Aires (UBA), Director of the Centre for the History of Psychoanalysis (CHψ), National Library, Argentina, 68 No. 720, La Plata (1900)

Alejandro Dagfal   CONTACT Alejandro Dagfal adagfal@gmail.com

The history of psychoanalysis has paid much attention to Melanie Klein's personal trajectory—from Vienna to Budapest, Berlin and London—and to her “controversial discussions” with Anna Freud, in the 1940s (Grosskurth 1986; King and Steiner 1991; Kristeva 2001). In contrast, relatively little attention has been paid to the circulation and the uneven reception of her ideas in other regions—although some works have studied what happened in countries such as Switzerland (Quinodoz 2013), France (Grosskurth 1986; Roudinesco 1997), and Argentina (Etchegoyen and Zysman 2005; Dagfal 2009b).

The 12 letters written by Melanie Klein that are reproduced in this issue are a resource that can be very helpful to expanding the historiography of her reception in both the French-speaking world and in South America. These historical processes were interconnected: the striking reception of Klein in Argentina boosted her expansion not only in Brazil, Uruguay, Colombia, and Mexico, but also, in turn, in France (Dagfal 2009b, in press). The developments which occurred in Buenos Aires in the mid-1940s constitute a unique case, as most of the founding members of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association (APA)—and some of their spouses—very rapidly became Kleinian. Significantly, some of these analysts were the first to publish texts from a Kleinian orientation in the French language (e.g. Ángel Garma, Enrique Pichon-Rivière, Arminda Aberastury, and Marie Langer) in the early 1950s. However, Klein herself would still wait until 1959 (a year before her death) to see the translation of her first book The Psycho-Analysis of Children (1932) in print. Even then, it is remarkable that the translation of Klein's first two books into French (Klein 1959a; Klein et al. 1966) was partly made by European analysts who had trained in Buenos Aires: Marcelle Spira and Willy Baranger. In short, Klein's reception in France was as late and difficult as her implantation in Argentina had been unexpectedly easy and fruitful. Notwithstanding this, the latter facilitated the former. Most of those mentioned above are among the recipients of the first set of six letters featured here, which address Klein's reception in South America, particularly in Argentina and Uruguay. The second group of letters—another set of six—discuss the “odyssey” of the French translation of Klein's “fundamental book.” They were sent to Jacques Lacan, who was given the translations rights in 1948, to Françoise Girard and Jean-Baptiste Boulanger—a young couple of analysts who obtained those rights in 1952—and to Daniel Lagache, the renowned psychoanalyst who was in charge of the series in which the book finally appeared. To my knowledge, none of these letters have been published in their originalform, even though some have been previously quoted or translated. The letters were generally dictated by Klein and typewritten by her secretary, and then manually corrected by Klein before sending them by air mail. Only in a few special cases were her letters entirely handwritten, but that did not happen with any of the 12 that are featured.

responseSet.title: Hallucinosis and reverie: Alices pain and its transformations in the consulting room
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hallucinosis and reverie: Alices pain and its transformations in the consulting room

Maria Oldoini

Maria Oldoini

Maria Oldoini   CONTACT Maria Grazia Oldoini oldoini@virgilio.it
Hallucinosis, reverie, transformations

This article seeks to explore, from a Bionian and post-Bionian perspective, the field of hallucinosis and its transformations, and to show how a certain kind of analytic work can take the patient from hallucinosic functioning to symbolic competence. The author refers to clinical situations where symbolic competence is markedly defective, and uses the term Munch-patients to refer to such persons, drawing on the metaphor suggested by the biography of the painter for its affinities with the suffering experienced by such patients.

The case of Alice is used to develop these ideas. The patient, faced with her inability to cope with a certain psychic pain, transforms this pain in hallucinosis. Having to face the emotional turbulences present in the field, the analyst becomes involved in an enactment, culminating in a state of somato-psychic hallucinosis during a session. Alices pain enters the relationship through the illness of the field, brought on stage, as it were, by the hallucinosis of the analyst, who manages to set in motion a metabolic cycle by means of her oneiric functions.

The paper suggests regarding hallucinosis as a bridge between psychotic and oneiric functioning and emphasizes the importance of oneiric functions in enabling the transition from concreteness and raw facts to real emotions.

responseSet.title: Making room for madness in mental health, by Marcus Evans
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making room for madness in mental health, by Marcus Evans

Ed Beveridge

Ed Beveridge

London

Ed Beveridge   ebeveridge1@nhs.net

Marcus Evans’ book is aimed at engaging a general audience of mental health professionals in thinking psychoanalytically about their day to day work with severe mental illness. In a way, his title sets out his agenda. He uses the word “madness” deliberately and provocatively, since he wishes practitioners to listen to and engage with their patients’ psychotic thinking, rather than deny or collude with it. In his introduction, he comments, with some regret, that he sometimes finds himself wondering “where we thought the ‘illness’ had gone when we changed the term from ‘mental illness’ to ‘mental health’.”

Evans is a nurse and psychoanalyst by background who for a number of years has worked in a broad range of mental health and psychotherapy settings within the National Health Service (NHS) in the UK. He trained in psychoanalytic psychotherapy at the Tavistock Clinic and he later held a number of clinical lead and managerial roles there. Of particular note, he was involved in founding the Fitzjohns Service, which offers psychoanalytically informed treatment to patients with complex mental health needs (including psychosis) who may not ordinarily have been considered by generic NHS psychotherapy services. Much of his detailed clinical material seems to be from this area of his work. He also has extensive experience of providing supervision to frontline staff from various settings—including secure (forensic) units—and he derives further interesting material from this area of his work.

In her brief foreword, Edna O'Shaughnessy—who had worked with Evans at the Fitzjohns Service—notes that, according to Money-Kyrle, “most people fear contact with the insane,” which in her view (and Evans’), is why people tend to want to “keep insanity out of view.” O'Shaughnessy herself cites Klein and Bion as key influences but mainly highlights the value, in her view, of Evans’ application of psychoanalytic understanding to everyday practice, especially through the supervision of frontline staff. “[Evans] shows how by not doing something […] we may reach the patient, who may then be able to make of it a healing experience of being understood.

responseSet.title: Towards psychoanalytic contribution to linguistic metaphor theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Towards psychoanalytic contribution to linguistic metaphor theory

Tair Caspi

Tair Caspi

Kfar Saba
Israel

Tair Caspi   taircaspi@gmail.com
Klein, Lakoff and Johnson, linguistic metaphor theory, metaphor, novel metaphor, Ogden, philosophy of language, symbol, symbolization, Winnicott

This paper lays out a formulation of the psychoanalytical contribution to linguistic metaphor theory. The author's main argument is that psychoanalysis can help enrich and shed light on linguistic metaphor theories, since these have focused on the cognitive aspect, to the exclusion of the role played by affect. Based on the tight link between metaphor and symbol – both configurations of figurative language – the author shall apply ideas sourced from some of the key psychoanalytic symbolization theories, focusing in particular on Klein, Winnicott, and Ogden. The course of exploration will serve to trace the unconscious emotional aspects that participate in the metaphor's mechanism, just as they participate in the symbol's workings. The study leads to the main conclusion that the intersubjective transitional space is of substantial importance to metaphor's constitution, particularly in regard to novel metaphors. Expanding the understanding of metaphor's modus operandi has important implications in conceptual clarification and for an in‐depth analytical work, and is of immense significance when it comes to analytical work with patients who suffer impairment of their metaphoric ability.

responseSet.title: “Psyche is extended”: from Kant to Freud
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Psyche is extended”: from Kant to Freud

Paolo Carignani

Paolo Carignani

Roma
Italy

Paolo Carignani   CONTACT Paolo Carignani paolocarignani@gmail.com
Mind-body relationship, space, time, philosophy, Freud, Kant, Bonaparte

This paper is inspired by one of Freud's last notes, which is famous for its astonishing conclusion: “Psyche is extended; knows nothing about it,” which describes the perception of space as a product of the extension of the psychic apparatus, and compares it with Kant's a priori categories. The Author reconstructs the historical background of this idea as part of a long discussion between Freud and his pupil Marie Bonaparte in the second half of 1938, and shows how the relationship between body and psychic functions in Freud's thought was influenced by his intellectual debt to Kant. Through an outline of the influence of Kantian philosophy on the emerging Naturphilosophie in fin de siècle Vienna, the Author aims to show the profound influence of Kantian philosophy on the development of Freud's thought. By committing to a modern and non-dualistic conception of the relationship between body and mind, Freud conceives an indissoluble bond between the mind and, not only the brain, but the whole body and its spatial extension. The Author concludes by identifying a close link between the aphorism of 1938 and Kantian pre-Critique thought, and, more generally, highlighting the influence of the Königsberg philosopher on the relationship between mind and body and on the perception of time and space in Freudian thought.

responseSet.title: Paths of the countertransference in the analyst—clinical examples of working through
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paths of the countertransference in the analyst—clinical examples of working through

Delaram Habibi-Kohlen

Delaram Habibi-Kohlen

Bergisch Gladbach
Germany

Delaram Habibi-Kohlen   CONTACT Delaram Habibi-Kohlen d.habibi-kohlen@netcologne.de
Intersubjectivity, relational psychoanalysis, technique, history of psychoanalysis

Today the importance of working through the analyst’s countertransference is stressed throughout all different schools of psychoanalysis. This article aims to make the microprocesses more transparent regarding the transformations of transference and countertransference, from the beginning of the anchoring of a projection in the analyst up to the re-internalization of a transformed projection in the patient. This is introduced by a survey of the history of two central terms: Projective Identification (on the side of the patient) and Countertransference (coming from the side of the analyst). How the analyst is also a “do-er” and how this fits in with the projective identifications of the patient is a newer development in psychoanalysis and is the main subject of this paper. Alongside the clinical material shown is described what the patient triggers in the analyst with his projections, and how the analyst manages to work through his countertransference where the patient becomes a temporary object, connecting with objects of the analyst until—if all goes well—the analyst manages to transform the projection of the patient while “triangulating with his inner understanding parents”.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The relevance of Evelyne Kestemberg today**

Antoine Nastasi

Antoine Nastasi

Paris

Antoine Nastasi   CONTACT Antoine Nastasi anrod@wanadoo.fr
Fetishistic relation to the object, ambisexedimago, the Self, cold psychosis, the pleasure of mental functioning, the phobia of mental functioning, the third character, the fragmented transference

Evelyne Kestemberg bequeathed rigorous metapsychological claims and she mapped out a way to approach the psychotic modes of being and formalise the specificities of the transference when delusion is at work, whether visibly or latently. The prime goal of the treatment is an attempt to restore the pleasure in mental functioning, a pleasure which psychotic suffering precisely obstructs.

responseSet.title: Looking back and moving forward
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Looking back and moving forward

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen   dana.birksted-breen@dib.me.uk

If anyone were inclined to put forward the paradoxical proposition that the normal man is not only far more immoral than he believes but also far more moral than he knows, psycho-analysis, on whose findings the first half of the assertion rests, would have no objection to raise against the second half. (Freud, 1923)

One hundred years ago, on 7 December 1918, shortly after the end of the First World War, Ernest Jones wrote to Sigmund Freud: “I think the time is ripe for an English journal, or edition of the Zeitschrift (not identical, of course) owned by the Vereinigung.” The Letters from Warsaw in this volume’s opening issue remind us again about the interconnectedness between the development of psychoanalysis and of The International Journal with the two world wars, their context of social upheaval and forced migrations, massive loss but also passion. As we move closer to marking the 100th volume of the IJP in 2019, we remember the significance of, and the contribution that psychoanalysis has offered at times of upheaval and change, as well as its role in bringing inter-cultural exchange and dialogue to the fore as we continue to navigate our contemporary times.

2018, the year of the 99th volume of The International Journal of Psychoanalysis, will see the first of our centenary celebration conferences, starting off our encounter with the general theme of the conferences: The Unconscious Core: Encountering and Speaking to the Unconscious. Taking place in October 2018 in a beautiful countryside setting in Rye Brook, a short drive from New York City, the conference will be in the form of a retreat with an emphasis on small group discussions of pre-circulated papers. Registration is now open for this conference. Two further conferences will follow in 2019, the anniversary year, with different speakers and in different formats. The celebration conference in Buenos Aires will take place in the historic National Library and will be conducted in Spanish only. The finale will be held in London in July that year at the Tower Bridge hotel next to St Katherine’s docks and overlooking Tower Bridge, a combined bascule and suspension bridge built on the Thames in the 1880s.

responseSet.title: Metà prigioniero, metà alato. La disssociazione corpo‐mente in psicoanalisi [Half prisoner, half winged: The body‐mind dissociation in psychoanalysis] by Riccardo Lombardi
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Metà prigioniero, metà alato. La disssociazione corpo‐mente in psicoanalisi [Half prisoner, half winged: The body‐mind dissociation in psychoanalysis] by Riccardo Lombardi

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

London
UK

Andrea Sabbadini   a.sabbadini@gmail.com

The cryptic title of this important book – Half Prisoner, Half Winged – comes from painter Paul Klee: “Man is half prisoner, half winged; each of the two parts in which his being is torn, realizing the existence of the other one, becomes aware of its own tragic incompleteness” (1925).

This quotation describes in beautifully poetic words the Body‐Mind Dissociation in Psychoanalysis of the book's clarifying subtitle. Its author is the Italian psychoanalyst and psychiatrist Riccardo Lombardi, already known to English readers for his book Formless Infinity (Routledge, 2015) on Matte Blanco and Bion, as well as for his numerous articles (many on similar topics to those presented in this latest publication) to be found in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis and in other psychoanalytic journals.

When mistakes, fault lines, blind spots, or limitations are identified in psychoanalytic theory or technique, there is a risk of over‐compensating for them by exaggerating their importance and promoting extreme solutions to the problem: babies will then risk being thrown out with the bathwater. For instance, Klein may have overstated the importance of envy in early childhood to compensate for the view that infants are still in a symbiotic relationships with their mothers; instead of searching for integration some object relational analysts may have been too quick to dismiss drive theory, and feminist ones to dismiss observable evidence of castration anxiety and penis envy; and some authors may have overstressed the centrality of sibling relationships by suggesting that they should take priority over Oedipal constellations. All of these colleagues were undoubtedly well intentioned, but their enthusiasm may have prevented them from reaching a balanced compromise between the tradition they had good reasons to challenge and the innovations they had equally good reasons to put forward.

In view of the above considerations, and being aware that the issue of body‐mind relationships is a prominent example of a largely neglected area of psychoanalytic inquiry, I was concerned that Lombardi's theorizations might fall into a similar predicament: that he would place excessive weight on the patient's, and therefore also on the analyst's, bodies and their conflictual relationship with their minds, with the result that the importance of, say, instinctual drives, interpersonal dynamics or the analytic field would end up being underestimated. The outcome would have then amounted to what I could describe as a sort of ‘metapsychological hypochondria’ – a misjudged over‐investment on bodies (healthy or sick, as the case may be) at the expense of whatever else may be relevant to our understanding of the This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher.

responseSet.title: Reading Italian Psychoanalysis edited by Franco Borgogno Alberto Luchetti and Luisa Marino Coe
responseSet.authorMast: Benedetta Guerrini Degl’Innocenti and  Adam Elgar
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Italian Psychoanalysis edited by Franco Borgogno Alberto Luchetti and Luisa Marino Coe

Benedetta Degl’Innocenti and Adam Elgar

Adam Elgar

Adam Elgar  

Reading Italian Psychoanalysis is an important publishing initiative aimed at offering English-speaking psychoanalytic practitioners the possibility of getting to know Italian psychoanalysis for the first time in an extended, in-depth way. The interest of the book and of the selections made by the editors consists in the fact that writers who are producing work of very great interest are finally accessible to readers of the English language.

The structure of this project, as its editors Franco Borgogno, Alberto Luchetti and Luisa Marino Coe write in the general introduction, has strong roots going back a long time:

It is not only the project of this book but also its content that ‘come from afar’: it comes from an extensive network of a huge number of psychoanalysts in Italy, who, for almost a century and especially over the last 50 years, have passionately dedicated their energies to psychoanalysis, and from the equally vast network of their patients who have fed that work with their heartfelt urge towards transformation. (p. 2)

Forty-eight seminal and representative papers out of such an extensive network have been chosen to illuminate what is distinctive about Italian theoretical and clinical thinking and to highlight the specific character of its psychoanalytic discourse.

To make it easier for the reader to build up a map of this extensive network, the editors decided to gather the selected papers into six thematic parts, highlighting a prevalent viewpoint from which the thematic parts were to be considered, including:

History of psychoanalysis in Italy

Metapsychology

Clinical practice, theory and technique, therapeutic factors

The person of the analyst, countertransference and the analytic relationship/field

Trauma, psychic pain, mourning and working through

Preverbal, precocious, fusional, primitive states of the mind

The first, introductory part contains two historical contributions, fundamental for understanding the cultural and conceptual roots of Italian psychoanalysis. In the first article, Psychoanalysis in Italy, Giuseppe Di Chiara outlines a history of Italian psychoanalysis, starting from the long relationship which Freud himself had with Italy, and with the first Italian professionals who came into contact with his thought. The second historical contribution, edited by Anna Ferruta, is a remarkable conceptual map for exploring the Italian psychoanalytic landscape, so as to gain an idea of the themes around which psychoanalytic thought has developed in Italy, and to understand the historical and cultural conditions which have characterized its development and given it its specific character. These conditions can be summed up in the oxymoronic pairing of two key expressions: linguistic isolation and openness to other psychoanalytic traditions. Linguistic isolation is the condition in which Italian psychoanalysts found themselves taking their first steps, for two reasons: because the psychoanalysis which has developed in Italy remained unknown for a long time by the international scientific community, since 1253

responseSet.title: Ending analysis: The case of Karl
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ending analysis: The case of Karl

Bernard Feinberg

Bernard Feinberg

St. Louis
MO
USA

Bernard Feinberg   CONTACT Bernard Feinberg feinbergbernard@gmail.com American Psychoanalytic Association, 8820 Ladue Rd Ste 311, St. Louis, MO, 63124, USA

Karl is a tall, attractive man, a designer, who was in his early fifties when we met. He was in a state of turmoil. His wife and he had been in a roiling crisis for at least a year. Now, she was about to move out. She had told him that she needed a break from being a wife and mother. However, she would stay if Karl would only say he loved and wanted her. He felt terrible about the possibility of a divorce and feared he would “starve without her.” However, he complained that if he were to say he loved her, she would have power over him that she would turn to her advantage in the future to threaten him with leaving. In the first few sessions, Karl had a striking tic that involved his right shoulder and head. It disappeared after a few meetings.

Karl felt himself to be caught between feeling dependent on his wife and resenting her criticisms. If he were single again, he imagined finding a more physically attractive woman, a calm woman who wouldn’t scream at him and their two children. The problem was that, among other things, he relied on her to tell him which of his design projects were well conceived and which wouldn’t work. Though she had no training in his field, he relied on her stamp of approval. To the degree that he relied on her, he was extremely sensitive to her disapproval. On the other hand, where he tried to convince me that he was an inadequate lover, she reassured him the opposite was true.

While Karl and his wife slept under separate rooves for about the first month of treatment, they communicated regularly. He suspected she might be unfaithful and she later confessed to having had the same worry about him. When she returned, he felt for a time that she was more loving. However, they continued to clash over the children and he now added her having moved out to the items he held against her.

Karl had married in his mid-thirties. Following two miscarriages, his wife had conceived their son in the midst of arrangements to adopt an infant daughter. Consequently, the children were less than a year apart.

responseSet.title: Lectures on technique by Melanie Klein: The return of feelings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lectures on technique by Melanie Klein: The return of feelings

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Geneva
Switzerland

Jean-Michel Quinodoz   CONTACT Jean-Michel Quinodoz quinodoz.jm@gmail.com

In reading these unpublished texts by Melanie Klein, especially the first lecture from 1936, I said to myself: “Here is an excellent introduction to the work of Melanie Klein.” Indeed, we discover how she brings innovative solutions to the dilemma posed by the transformation of a situation where hate is stronger than love, as we observe in many psychopathological disorders, to a situation where love is stronger than hate. Melanie Klein approaches this question (left open by Freud theoretically and clinically) from the angle of technique, and applies it to the management of positive and negative transference in clinical practice.

Written in 1936, these lectures are not only interesting for the history of ideas in psychoanalysis, but also for its teaching. In fact, her innovative ideas are linked to Freud’s questions, particularly regarding the place of feelings of love and hate in relation to positive and negative transference. We may also note the clarity and freshness of her writing and style, which takes nothing away from the complexity of the subject.

Melanie Klein was engaged in a delicate balancing act here. On the one hand, she was concerned to convey innovative ideas that were liable to be disturbing; on the other, she wanted to follow in Freud’s footsteps. As our reading progresses, however, her references to Freudian concepts are more implicit than explicit. For psychoanalysts familiar with Kleinian thought, her affiliation to Freud no doubt seems obvious, especially for those who have been trained in the British Society. On the other hand, for colleagues less aware of Kleinian concepts, her affiliation with Freud is less easy to detect, especially since certain Freudian notions have not always acquired the place they deserve, even today, particularly in the field of affects.

That is why I would like to recall Freud’s dilemma over the relationship between love and hate, and the positive and negative transference, because his hesitations as to how to solve this dilemma were shared by psychoanalysts of his time and sometimes remain so today.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The new Klein–Lacan dialogues, edited by Julia Borossa, Catalina Bronstein, Clare Pajaczkowska

Neil Morgan

Neil Morgan

London
British Psychoanalytical Society

Neil Morgan   neil_morgan@lineone.net

Words are the currency of psychoanalysis. In another context, D.Z. Phillips (Phillips 1991), the Welsh philosopher of Religion, wrote that: “the words in our lives, and the life in our words, reveal where, and who, we are.”

What are we to make then of the attempt, as here, to compare and contrast two quite distinct vocabularies—the Kleinian and the Lacanian? At times they can seem like two quite different forms of life, to use Wittgenstein’s radical phrase, creating different landscapes of psychoanalysis, two separate continents, speaking different languages to each other.

What does it mean for a truly realist vision of psychoanalysis to have two such different paradigms?—I think questions of this type are bound to emerge when reading this important and thought provoking collection of Essays.

1502

Giuseppe Civitarese   Email: gcivitarese@venus.it

More Than Just a Job could be the subtitle of these notes on reading Andrea Sabbadini's book, Boundaries and Bridges: Perspectives on Time and Space in Psychoanalysis (henceforth BB). The expression, which the author uses to explain what psychoanalysis represents for him, echoes Bion's famous ‘Making the best of a bad job’. There is something that unites the two expressions at a deep level, and which I would certainly suggest has the features of a profound ethical position. What do I mean by this? That both authors feel that they are engaged in something very serious and very important. All work is noble, of course, but as analysts we are concerned with human suffering. Stefano Lussana

Stefano Lussana

Roma
Italy

Stefano Lussana   CONTACT Stefano Lussana stefano.lussana@virgilio.it
termination of analysis, separation, separateness, detachment, catastrophic change, caesura, non psychotic and psychotic personalites, oscillations

If, as Bion states, It [analysis] does not end; the relationship between a particular doctor and a particular analysand does (Brazilian Lectures), this brings to mind both Freuds Analysis Terminable (the analystanalysand relationship) and Interminable (the analysis) and the heated debate between an essentially weak general theory and a decidedly strong specific model for the termination of analysis. According to the indications of Bion and Ferro, the theory presents two modes of mental functioning at the end of analysis based on the oscillations PSD and . As proposed in this paper, this can be supplemented as PS/D and . The specific model for the end of analysis, strongly supported by Bion and Ferro, is only concerned with the analysandanalyst dyad. In this case, it is portrayed by The Little Dummy Man, a clinical model jointly constructed by the analysand and the analyst during the last two years of the analysis, and is retold here by both. An archaeological metaphor is employed at the end of the paper. It is believed that an analysis thrives on the fluctuations between moments of unity and intimacy, and moments of separation and detachment, with the end of the analysis being at once an act that is the most painful and the most transformative.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The work of psychoanalysis: Sexuality, time and the psychoanalytic mind**

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

London
UK
Consulting rooms, 24 Hillfield Road

Ronald Britton   Email: rbpsych@aol.com

The Work of Psychoanalysis, as its subtitle suggests, addresses the interconnections of sexuality, time and the psychoanalytic mind. It especially *This paper has been typeset and published by the previous publisher and hence differs in appearance to other papers in this issue.286 expresses one particular psychoanalytic mind: that of the author. The work has two faces; reflecting two aspects of the professional mind of Dana Birksted-Breen. Firstly it involves that of the psychoanalytic scholar and secondly that of the analytic practitioner. In her first role Birksted-Breen is discriminating, comprehensive and contemporary, as befits one who has been Editor of The International Journal for many years and who also previously acted as Editor of the New Library of Psychoanalysis. She is also determined to make clear what she believes are the most significant discoveries that have arisen from practice and that have in turn, an effect on practice. The closer she comes to describing her own work the clearer it becomes which of the ideas in contemporary psychoanalysis are relevant to her views, most notably on the working of the analyst's mind in clinical practice.

Birksted-Breen has made her own original contributions to analytic theory: notably ‘Phallus, Penis and Mental Space’ (1996) a famous paper which she re-tells with more elaboration in Chapter 6. Here, in this new work she also gives her own original thought on ‘Psychoanalytic Time’: the relationship of ‘there and then’ to ‘here and now’; après coup; continuity and/or transformation. She also introduces her ideas on mental space as an aspect of time in analysis in her concept of Bi-ocularity in the mind of the analyst at work. One could apply the notion as she describes it in Chapter 11, to this book itself in the sense that she maintains an objective awareness of psychoanalytic theory (informed by her own thinking and wide reading) alongside which lies her direct day-to-day clinical experience while between these two lines lies what she regards as a creative space.

responseSet.title: From seriousness to reality in play: Some considerations on the psychic mechanisms involved in play and their applications in clinical practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From seriousness to reality in play: Some considerations on the psychic mechanisms involved in play and their applications in clinical practice

Sébastien Bogajewski

Sébastien Bogajewski

France

Sébastien Bogajewski   CONTACT Sébastien Bogajewski sebastien.bogajewski@univ-paris13.fr Associate Researcher, Paris University 13, 99, av Jean-Baptiste Clément 93430 Villetaneuse Centre Médical Croix de Chavaux, 11, av de la Résistance, 93100 Montreuil

Play, transitional phenomena, reality, seriousness, psychic reality, clinical practice

The idea that play is a serious matter seems agreed. However, it seems necessary to ask ourselves if this concerns the seriousness with which the activity is performed or the serious character of the activity itself. Is a game that evokes psychic or physical death still playful? Can a game whose rules are not respected scrupulously continue to be regarded as a game? This first consideration will lead me to present the relationship between play and reality with a view to including it in the framework of Winnicott’s transitional phenomena. I put forward the idea that play is first and foremost to do with the relationship between a subject and reality. This leads on to a discussion of the idea, attributed wrongly to Freud, that play can be opposed to reality. I believe, on the contrary, that it is fully grounded in reality, but that it develops in a closed and structured space within it. The notion of play is thus not only related to the creation by the subject of this closed and structured space, but also by the way in which he uses it. Finally, I will examine how play is utilized in clinical practice. We will look at what, in my view, may constitute an impasse, and even a danger, in its utilization, as well as its potentialities as a whole, which are linked to the issue of veiling or concealment and to the introduction of a certain frivolity that is suited to protecting the subject from certain elements that are too difficult to deal with head-on.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

Los Angeles
CA
USA

Joseph Aguayo   joseph.aguayo@gmail.com

Dear Editor,

I have read with appreciation the recent paper by J. and D. Scharff in the December 2017 issue (IJP: 98: 1619–1639) on the “Group Affective Model,” (GAM) in the training of psychotherapists and analytic candidates. Their methodology seems like a supple response to Bolognini’s (2014) entreaty that psychoanalysts consider a “4th pillar” to their tri-partite Eitingon model of training analysis, supervision and didactic courses.

By having student therapists and analytic candidates alike undergo “group affective” experiences in groups where they emotionally process their responses to analytic readings, there seems to be new possibilities of enlivening the overall trainee experience in didactic courses—as the Scharffs’ small group examples demonstrate.

As it turns out, their methodology (honed after many years of experience in Washington, DC and abroad) also turns out to be a felicitous match for one deployed by a group of IPA-affiliated graduate analysts who have gathered in Los Angeles for annual meetings over the past 3 years. A group of 70 (primarily American) analysts get together to discuss the clinical work of Wilfred Bion, striking an inter-regional dimension by inviting UK analysts to discuss his work with our group. In the last 2 years, we have also added a comparative aspect, where we have examined the work of Bion in connection with the clinical ideas of D.W. Winnicott.

Just last year, we also experimented by having a Tavistock-based, Group Relations, all day conference just prior to a traditional academic 3-day conference weekend. We have continued this experiment into a second year and are pleased that the results now appear in the May issue of the British Journal of Psychotherapy (Aguayo et al., 2018).

While our methods developed independently of the Scharffs' GAM, we are pleased to report some intriguing similarities, a case of what Thomas Kuhn has termed “simultaneous discovery,” where researchers light upon similar findings in two separate geographical locations. Even the seasoned analysts in our group (on the average some 15 years post-graduation) can and do have a different sense of their “group selves” in a rather emotionally intense way in small group experiences.

responseSet.title: Embodying disillusionment: Poussin’s blinded giants
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Embodying disillusionment: Poussin’s blinded giants

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

New York
NY

Adele Tutter   CONTACT Adele Tutter atutter@mac.com 124 W. 79th Street, Suite 1C, New York, NY 10024 USA

Illusion, disillusionment, Ovid, Nicolas Poussin, giant, myth, Polyphemus, Cacus, Hercules, Orion, embodiment, the Metamorphoses

Extending the traditional view of the giant of ancient myth as the personification of the father, the giant also affords the metaphorical elaboration of infantile fantasies of grandiosity derived from identification with the omnipotent parent. Conversely, the fallen and typically blinded giant embodies disillusionment: with the idealised parental imago, and also with one’s own illusions of omnipotence. Nicolas Poussin’s successive aesthetic interpretations of the giants of Greek legend highlight the symbolic dialectics of size, and offer a window onto the illusions and disillusionments that are intrinsic to generational succession, and accompany the confrontation of various realities—of dependency, vulnerability, maturation, achievement, aging, loss and death.

Dans le prolongement de la vision traditionnelle et mythique du géant comme personnification du père, le géant est aussi le support d’une élaboration métaphorique des fantasmes infantiles de grandeur, issus de l’identification au parent omnipotent. A l’inverse, le géant tombé et aveugle incarne le désillusionnement par rapport à l’imago parentale idéalisée, ainsi que par rapport à l’illusion d’omnipotence propre au sujet lui-même. Les interprétations esthétiques successives des géants de la mythologie grecque par Nicolas Poussin soulignent le symbolisme de la dialectique de la stature et font entrevoir les illusions et les désillusionnements qui, inhérents à la succession des générations, accompagnent la confrontation avec la réalité dans ses différents aspects—dépendance, vulnérabilité, maturation, succès, vieillissement, perte et mort.

Nach traditionellem Verständnis sind die Giganten des antiken Mythos personifizierte Vaterfiguren. In Erweiterung dieser Sicht stellen sie auch die metaphorische Ausgestaltung infantiler Größenphantasien dar, die aus der Identifizierung mit dem omnipotenten Elternteil hervorgehen. Umgekehrt verkörpert der gefallene und typischerweise geblendete Riese die Desillusionierung bezüglich der idealisierten Elternimago, aber auch der eigenen Allmachtsillusionen. Nicolas Poussins ästhetische Interpretationen der Giganten der griechischen Sagenwelt wirft Licht auf die Symbolik der Dialektik von Größe und gewährt Einblick in die Illusionen und Desillusionierungen, die der Abfolge der Generationen inhärent sind und das Aufeinanderprallen verschiedener Wirklichkeiten—Abhängigkeit und Verletzlichkeit, Reife und Leistungsfähigkeit, Älterwerden, Verluste und Tod—begleiten.

Se ci si spinge un po’ oltre la tradizionale interpretazione del gigante dei miti antichi come personificazione del padre, si potrà constatare come le figure dei giganti consentano anche di elaborare metaforicamente fantasie di grandiosità derivate dall’identificazione con un genitore onnipotente. I personaggi di giganti caduti in disgrazia (e solitamente accecati) incarnano invece vissuti di disillusione, sia rispetto all’imago genitoriale idealizzata sia rispetto alle proprie illusioni di onnipotenza. La sequenza di interpretazioni estetiche dei giganti del mito greco offerte da Nicolas Poussin pongono in evidenza il simbolismo intrinseco alla dialettica delle dimensioni, gettando luce sulle illusioni e disillusioni che punteggiano l’avvicendarsi delle generazioni e accompagnano ciascun individuo nel suo confrontarsi con diverse realtà che vanno dalla dipendenza alla vulnerabilità, alla maturazione, al successo, all’invecchiamento, alla perdita e infine alla morte.

Extendiendo la visión tradicional del gigante de los mitos antiguos como la personificación del padre, el gigante también permite la elaboración metafórica de las fantasías infantiles de grandiosidad derivadas de la identificación con el padre omnipotente. Por lo contrario, el gigante caído y típicamente enceguecido encarna el desilusionamiento con la imago parental idealizada y también con las propias ilusiones de omnipotencia. Las interpretaciones estética sucesivas de Nicolas Poussin de los gigantes de la leyenda griega ponen de relieve el simbolismo de la dialéctica del tamaño y ofrece una ventana hacia las ilusiones y los desilusionamientos que son intrínsecos a la sucesión generacional y que acompañan la confrontación de diferentes realidades: de dependencia, vulnerabilidad, madurez, logro, envejecimiento, pérdida y muerte.

responseSet.title: Lectures on technique by Melanie Klein: A commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lectures on technique by Melanie Klein: A commentary

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman

London
Distinguished Fellow and Training Analyst

Michael Feldman   CONTACT Michael Feldman mmf@mmfeldman.co.uk

This book is an important contribution to our understanding of Klein’s views on psychoanalytic technique and theory. As well as being of historical interest, it remains highly relevant to the contemporary working analyst who wishes to clarify aspects of his clinical work with patients. How much does he interpret what is going on in the “here and now” of the sessions and how much does he relate this to the patient’s account of his history? What weight does he give to unconscious phantasy, and how does he use these theories? How does he understand the feelings and thoughts that arise in his own mind, usually thought of as counter-transference? And how do these link with the thoughts induced in him by the patient’s phantasies, anxieties and behaviour?

All this and more are addressed in Klein’s Lectures on Technique, which are published here for the first time. These six lectures were given to students at the British Psychoanalytical Society in 1936 and probably repeated several times in subsequent years. The text was discovered in the Melanie Klein Archives in the Wellcome Medical Library in London and studied by Claudia Frank and Elizabeth Spillius, who was the Honorary Archivist to the Melanie Klein Trust.

These lectures were given during a period in which Klein was formulating her ideas on psychic development and functioning, and reflect her evolving theoretical and clinical model. She had published Psychoanalysis of Children in 1932, and a few years later her major paper on the depressive position (1935). Her conceptualization of the “Paranoid-schizoid position” was not published until 1946. Fundamentally, she suggested that the constant operation in phantasy of projection, introjection and identification contributed to the construction of an inner world of objects and self, which determines the individual’s relation to themselves and to the external world.

The Kleinian approach to the technique of psychoanalysis which she articulates in these lectures has a special significance because of the importance she places on what she calls the “Analytic Attitude.” In this lively and highly readable account Klein emphasizes the need for the psychoanalyst to give the patient his undivided attention: his “whole interest is focused on one aim, namely on the exploration of the mind of this one person who for the time being has become the centre of our attention,” and to give priority to the task of understanding what is being communicated.

responseSet.title: Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina's Diccionario de Psicoanalisis Argentino [Dictionary of Argentine Psychoanalysis] edited by Claudia Lucia Borensztejn, Andrés Rascovsky, Gilda Sabsay de Foks, Eduardo Safdie and Beatriz Zelcer
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina's Diccionario de Psicoanalisis Argentino [Dictionary of Argentine Psychoanalysis] edited by Claudia Lucia Borensztejn, Andrés Rascovsky, Gilda Sabsay de Foks, Eduardo Safdie and Beatriz Zelcer

Alfonso Pola

Alfonso Pola

Santiago de Chile
Chile

Alfonso Pola   E-mail:alfonsopola@vtr.net

This dictionary provides readers with a wealth of information on psychoanalytic ideas developed in Argentina. Given the large number of concepts of inestimable clinical and heuristic value contained in it, consulting it will provide an illuminating experience to anyone seeking to learn about the contributions of Argentine psychoanalysis.

Formally, it presents the alphabetically organized entries typical of dictionaries. There are 175 articles (91 in Volume 1 and 84 in Volume 2) written by 94 psychoanalysts who specialize in the various topics. The articles are relatively brief but go deep into the issues discussed. The authors have been able to describe each subject in depth in just a few pages. The clarity and thoroughness of the contributions show that the authors chosen are the most knowledgeable in each area; they are true authoritative voices in the field.

Every article includes a brief literature review and a few terms in bold, which are also dictionary entries. In addition, the works cited that are followed by the Greek letter Ψ can be found in the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association's database (APAPsiBase). Thanks to these features, the dictionary offers a good introduction to the ideas included in it and, at the same time, facilitates further research when an article arouses the reader's interest in a specific topic.

I would now like to dwell for a moment on an aspect of this Dictionary of Argentine Psychoanalysis that is unusual for this kind of publication. I am referring to the fact that it does more than just elucidate the meaning of certain terms, in this case, psychoanalytic concepts developed in Argentina. The entire book seems to have been conceived to help the reader understand ‘Argentine psychoanalysis’ not only in its theoretical aspects, but also with regard to the personal circumstances of the authors who formulated them. This editorial decision fully justifies the incorporation of brief biographies of the main figures that contributed to the development of these ideas and concepts. The editors state that a policy decision was made to include only deceased thinkers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unzustellbar. Psychoanalytische Studien zu Philosophie, Trieb und Kultur [Undeliverable: Psychoanalytic studies on philosophy, instinct and culture] by Wolfgang Hegener

Friedrich‐Wilhelm Eickhoff

Friedrich‐Wilhelm Eickhoff

Tübingen
Germany

Friedrich‐Wilhelm Eickhoff   FWEickhoff@t-online.de

Wolfgang Hegener borrows the evocative title of his book ‘Undeliverable’ from the topos of undeliverable letters in Hermann Melville's short story Bartleby the Scrivener. In this moving story from 1853 the enigmatic protagonist Bartleby captivates the narrator, a New York attorney, who has hired him as a clerk in his law office, with his repeated and mild: “I would prefer not to”. The reader learns, after his sad death from starvation, of his former job in a Dead Letter Office. There he sorted undeliverable letters, the messages of which did not reach depressed people. The author focuses particularly on the image of the ‘dead’ letters because he sees in this metaphor an effective illustration of the circumscription of certain autistic emotional states, an expression of the silent workings of the death instinct, unsuccessful attachment and objectalizing functions. As in a dead script the work of translation fails when the memory cannot renew itself in what Freud called ‘retranscription’. With his speculations in ‘Beyond the Pleasure Principle’ Freud marked the point at which there is a remnant, precisely, of these undeliverable remainders. Mindful of this constitutional limitation, Hegener naturally advocates the chance of an articulation and transformation, extending as far as possible, of that which gives the impression of being undeliverable.

In the chapter: ‘Between Counter‐science and the Power of Subjugation, Foucault, the Unconscious and the Place of the Psychoanalyst’ the author struggles hard with Michel Foucault's shadow and his inner contradictions. Before Foucault's ambivalent attitude led him to discard psychoanalysis, the psychoanalytical project played a major role in his work. It is true – the author writes – that Foucault allows Freud a special status, but then repeatedly withdraws it, similar to the ‘fort‐da’ game. On the one hand, Freud is called as a witness for a possible dialogue with unreason; on the other hand he is presented as the heir of psychiatry, in an all‐out attack, so that the Freudian model of the doctor‐patient relationship appears to be an external extension of the psychiatric asylum. Hegener forcefully confronts these polemic arguments by emphasizing the psychoanalyst's relinquishment of safety based on power. Hegener sees Derrida's comparatively psychoanalytical 1043
