responseSet.title: Achilles: A Homeric hero enamoured with the absolute
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Achilles: A Homeric hero enamoured with the absolute11

Nicolas Evzonas

Nicolas Evzonas

Paris
France

Nicolas Evzonas   taircaspi@gmail.com
Homer, Iliad, Achilles, psychological myth, Eros of the absolute, paranoid‐schizoid position, splitting, idealization, depressive position, mourning, mania, sublimation

This article explores through a psychoanalytical lens the character of Achilles in Homer's Iliad, the matrix behind the Western conception of heroism. The contribution reveals the psychological link binding the words and acts of the most valiant of warriors in Antiquity, which is situated in myth and termed “the Eros of the absolute.” The paroxystic ideality underlying the aforementioned myth, which is rooted in the anthropological need to believe, is at the origin of Achilles’ legendary μῆνις, that is, the flood of rage triggered by contests for supremacy, aggravated by the loss of his war comrade, aroused by the drama of aging and death, and then transfigured through song and memory. The main claim of the author is that Iliad, despite its seeming lack of attention to interiority, is launched by the archetypal emotion of wrath and owes its appeal to its hero's embrace of heroic idealism in an excessive, radical and absolute way that results in a captivating narcissism and sadomasochistic antithesis of ideality. This argument leads to the conclusion that Homer is the Father of the “primitive horde” of affects.

responseSet.title: Le Narcissisme: Que sais-je? by Paul Denis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le Narcissisme: Que sais-je? by Paul Denis

Françoise Coblence

Françoise Coblence

Paris
236, rue de Tolbiac

Françoise Coblence   francoise.coblence@wanadoo.fr

Any contextualization or appreciation of Paul Denis’ exposition of Freud’s notion of narcissism and its current usage must take into account the originality of Denis’ drive theory. Following in the footsteps of “Drives and Drive Destinies” or Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality, Paul Denis defines the drive as the “the measure of work demanded of psychic life as a result of its union with the corporal”. But we know that he posits the breaking-down of the psychic force that is the drive into two components – the two formants of the drive – a cathexis through mastery stream and a libidinal force of satisfaction cathexis (Denis 1997). To understand the concept of narcissism, we must connect these two formants with the love objects of the drive: oneself and the other. Before 1914, Paul Denis reminds us, Freud had introduced narcissism in the Schreber case as an intermediate stage between auto-eroticism (the cathexis of the erogenous zones and the partial drives) and object love, the stage where one’s own body is taken as a love object. Parallel to this unification of the body, it is also the self which constitutes itself. With the 1914 text, “a sea change in theory” (p. 19), emphasis is now placed upon the original psychic conflict: ego-libido/object-libido, with Freud holding to the hypothesis of the “intercommunicating vessels” (a constant quantity of energy would mean that, as one of the components increases, so the other decreases). Narcissism is a correlate of the libidinal cathexis of the self. Freud, however, means to distance himself from the Jungian unification of libido and must therefore maintain – even accentuate – the opposition between the two libidinal currents. The other aspect of the revolution precipitated by the introduction of narcissism is the central place it occupies in the psychic development and constitution of every individual. As a result, narcissism has little in common with the perversion described by Havelock Ellis in 1898 – even if this necessitates a revisiting of perversion. Above all, narcissism is no longer a stage in the individual’s development; it accompanies every facet of one’s life and the entirety of one’s activities – including the most object-related. In this respect, the theory of the communicating vessels between ego- and object-libido does not hold; even less so, as Paul Denis continually demonstrates, if we consider that any object satisfaction (for example, in sexual life, in love for one’s children) may come to strengthen self-worth and narcissistic satisfaction. Sexuality and narcissistic affects all attest to the manifold combinations between satisfaction and mastery drive currents (streams), with analysis according to these *

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L’analyse avec fin [Analysing to an end] by Jean-Louis Baldacci

Bertrand Colin

Bertrand Colin

Bertrand Colin   bcolin002@gmail.com

Jean-Louis Baldacci is known for his frequently quoted paper on “Sublimation, from the outset” given at the Congress for French-speaking analysts. This explored in detail a note by Freud concerning a sublimation that begins with the drive, from the beginning of life. But he is equally well known for his work on the consultative process, or consultative endeavour, of psychoanalytic treatment, starting with the inter-analytic research and work carried out at the Centre Jean Favreau that he directed from 2000 to 2015.

“Analysing to an end” traces the common theme of these two dimensions: “sublimation,” as what has to be grasped from the outset of any treatment, and the “consultative process,” in its connection with the inter-analytic work that is a precondition for psychoanalysis.

Accordingly, the book is entitled “Analysing to an end.” The original French title harbours a welcome ambiguity because of the double meaning of the word “fin.” The translator into English may struggle to find a translation that can render this ambiguity. First of all, there is the reference to Freud’s essay “Analysis terminable and interminable.” But also, more significantly, “fin” in French also refers to “end” (in its main sense) and “purpose” (a less common sense of “end”) in English. Just as it is appropriate with “sublimation” to identify from the outset something that is usually referred to as an accomplishment, will the “end” also be an issue for the author from the outset of every “consultative process”? Ambiguity is also a necessity in the art of interpretation, and from the outset. It must be able to hold the equilibrium and sustain the movement required by the “two-sided” nature of the transference: the attachment to the object (its hypnotic pole); the displacement (its anti-hypnotic part by which the play of digression can transform mnemic traces into memories). From the outset, the author humorously illustrates this necessary ambiguity—by quoting the great French psychiatrist of the 1960s, Henri Ey: “Yes … do an analysis, but get out of it!.” And by quoting the equally famous German psychiatrist and philosopher, Ludwig Binswanger: “Someone who is once seized by analysis is never released … .” Both these quotations helpfully trigger an “anxiety signal”: a testimony to the “love” in play and an appeal to the anticipated “work.” To love and to work: to quote that “end” and “purpose” often given to analysis, in Freud’s footsteps. So we find this conclusion, as an epilogue of the book and a commentary on an end of analysis: “Humour and tenderness during the day in the session and dreams and sleep at night, as forms of the balanced play between sexualisation and desexualisation, carve out the space for the sexed other and a shareable ideal. [[RunningHead]]

Death and fallibility in the psychoanalytic encounter: Mortal gifts, by Ellen Pinsky

Sharone Bergner

Sharone Bergner

New York
NY
USA

Sharone Bergner   sbergnerphd@gmail.com

Ellen Pinsky’s gem of a book, a collection of finely wrought, poignant, sometimes angry essays, is unusually beautiful, and yet it leaves us with disturbing discomfort. Pinsky demands that we acknowledge the analyst’s mortality – especially his actual death and also his fallibility, and the powerful effect this has on his patients. The book proceeds, like Gabriel Garcia Marquez’ (1981) so-named novella, as a “chronicle of a death foretold”. It is Pinsky’s experience of the death of her analyst that impels her writing. This death is declared at the beginning, specifically and with full humanizing detail, thus establishing an arc of tension, a disturbance, which must be borne by the reader for the entirety of the narrative. I think some of the book’s considerable power comes from this: despite stating at the outset that her analyst’s death forms the basis of her writing, despite interleaving the investigation of scant considerations of analysts’ mortality in our literature along with her personal experience of this event from all corners of the essays, the reader is nevertheless startled by the epilogue’s recounting of the tender words of affection she offered him as he was leaving for a conference: “[h]ave a good time, learn something, and 1509

responseSet.title: Pensamiento vivo. En la obra de Carlos Sopena [Living thought in the work of Carlos Sopena] edited by Asociación Psicoanalítica de Madrid (APM)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pensamiento vivo. En la obra de Carlos Sopena [Living thought in the work of Carlos Sopena] edited by Asociación Psicoanalítica de Madrid (APM)

Milagros Cid Sanz

Milagros Cid Sanz

Madrid

Milagros Cid Sanz   E-mail: Dra.cidsanz@outlook.es

This book is a compilation of articles by Carlos Sopena, a psychoanalyst of Uruguayan origin who lived and worked in Madrid from 1975 until his death in April 2012. His dual membership of the Uruguayan and Madrid Societies provided the freshness and vitality of Uruguayan psychoanalysis and his culture and openness to different psychoanalytic currents, together with his commitment to clinical work and teaching, represented a significant contribution to the development of analytic thought at the Madrid Psychoanalytic Association. This book, published posthumously, offers a modest homage to his memory.

The choice of the chapters, by not following a chronological order, allows a gradual unfolding and harmonious interweaving of the themes studied by the author. I shall focus on ‘Freudian Heritage and Psychoanalytic Babel’, ‘On Hamlet’, ‘Amadeus: Reflections on Envy’, ‘To Love: Between the Same and the Other’, the author's work on the themes of ‘Sublimation’, ‘Mourning’, ‘Construction and Reconstruction’, ‘The Body in Hysteria’ and his last paper on the subject of ‘The Dynamic Field of Psychoanalysis’, for which he was awarded the Carolina Zamora Prize.

The work of sublimation, the work of mourning and shared mourning through the word are some of the themes the book explores at different points and in reference to different processes, such as love, passion, transference and countertransference. The always‐incomplete drive satisfaction and the void of the unrepresentable will provide the impetus for the creative and scientific act, the author maintains. These are processes present in analytic treatment, which takes part in the creative act – there is no analysis without creation, Sopena reminds us – emphasizing the importance of asymmetry in the analytic relation, which must leave a third space making possible the encounter with the hidden, with the unsymbolized, which is articulated through language – a dimension that would be present throughout his work.

One of the characteristics noticeable throughout the chapters is a love for culture. Carlos Sopena was a man of culture, both in the Freudian as well as in the traditional sense of the term. This culture is present in the reference to literary characters, to philosophers and writers, to those whom very creatively he invites into dialogue with psychoanalysis, while at no point losing sight of the latter's specificity: the exploration of the Unconscious.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La jeune fille et le psychanalyste [The girl and the psychoanalyst]**

Françoise Neau

Françoise Neau

Françoise Neau   Email: francoise.neau@parisdescartes.fr

Having become a young clinical psychologist after an initial literary education, Catherine Chabert began her practice as a drama therapist, then as a psychotherapist of adolescents and young adults hospitalized in a psychiatric setting for severe disorders. Now a highly active training member of the French Psychoanalytical Association, she combines her work as an analyst with a long and brilliant academic career at Paris Descartes University (Paris V). As a senior lecturer and then a professor, she contributes to the development of clinical psychology and psychoanalysis at the university, at the institutional level by creating and directing the laboratory for clinical psychology and psychopathology, and at the scientific level by her own research and the research she coordinates or directs; in particular, she is updating and developing the psychoanalytic interpretation of projective methods in an approach that is now gaining an international following. Her strong interest in the transference in clinical practice and the metapsychology closely linked with it, which she refuses to separate, form the heart of Chabert's psychoanalytic practice and thought; they have permeated the university teaching she has provided to future clinical psychologists and young researchers, many of whom have received and transmitted her passion for psychoanalysis, as they have long nurtured the training of many analysts in supervision with her at the APF's training institute.

Chabert's work is especially close to the style and thought of Pontalis – a major proponent of Winnicott's work in France – with his liking for the ‐‘intervening period’ and ‘intermediate realms’, but she is also influenced by other eminent figures of French psychoanalysis such as Daniel Widlöcher, André Green, Roger Dorey, and Didier Anzieu, about whom she has written a monograph. Building on Anzieu's works on psychic envelopes, she concentrates much of her research on narcissistic and depressive sufferings, a productive field in contemporary psychoanalysis for the past few decades. On the metapsychological level, she sets out increasingly to emphasize the connection between the problematic of loss, common to these registers of narcissistic and borderline functioning despite different defensive forms, and the oedipal problematic and psychosexuality that structure the psyche and found the Freudian thought that is still a source of inspiration for her.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

Los Angeles
CA
USA
11849 West Olympic Blvd., Suite 202

Joseph Aguayo   joseph.aguayo@gmail.com

Dear Editor,

I have read with much appreciation the recent book review of Winnicott’s Collected Works by Rachel Chaplin, (IJP, 2018, 99: 765–769). His many papers and letters, published and unpublished, have found safe harbour in this act of international scholarship, and the reviewer is to be congratulated for a marvellous compression of its many features, not the least of which is a completely digitized reference volume, where analysts and scholars can look up any topic or colleague whose work Winnicott ever wrote about.

Of course, and of necessity, much material has received scant attention in Chaplin’s review; and I here call particular attention to the many “Introductions” that begin each of the 12 volumes, which in themselves, give us an up-to-date opportunity to both review and debate various facets of current Winnicott scholarship. He appeared to be on a truly ecumenical search for psychoanalytic truth aptly represented by the many contemporary colleagues with whose work he interacted. Caldwell and Joyce (2011) have called attention to the many analytic theorists with whom Winnicott sought generative dialogue—from Jacques Lacan, Michael Fordham, (the London Jungian), Michael Balint, and of course, Melanie Klein herself and members of her London group.

In many ways, Winnicott’s multi-layered relationship to Klein—for many years as supporter before he turned away from her views—continues to be a controversial area for debate. And as a result, I found it interesting to read what the various analytic commentators had to say about this relationship; so, I set out to read what might be new, additive or debatable about what we already know about his long-term collaboration with Klein, (which lasted from 1935 to 1951) and how we might now understand what new light the new documents in these Collected Works might shed.

Right from the start, when his analyst of the 1920s, James Strachey relayed that if Winnicott wanted to learn something of child analysis, he needed to consult with Klein herself, Winnicott read Klein’s (1932) Psychoanalysis of Children. He was so enthusiastic about her approach to analyzing young children that he read the book twice. To the end of Klein’s life, Winnicott never ceased to be fascinated by her work. And we now learn that when Winnicott read Klein’s (1961) posthumously published Narrative of a Child Analysis, he immediately read it a second time round. That Klein exerted a tremendous and shaping influence on Winnicott’s own work as a child analyst beginning in the 1930s is not in doubt, yet there is a tendency in some of these “Introductions” to almost simultaneously admit as well as diminish her influence, sometimes within the space of one sentence. For example, of Winnicott’s (1935) “Manic Defense” membership paper for the British Society, as well as his (1936) paper, “Appetite and Emotional Disorder,” Robinson (2016, CW, 1: 14) writes: “Both <papers> speak of Klein’s influence, but both also show signs of Winnicott beginning to find his own way in working out the relation of the environment to internal world in the first stages of what Abram (2008, 1194) has termed his ‘environmental individual set-up.’ Rather than stick to contextualizing what Winnicott learned in 1935, his ideas are in a sense conflated with later developments, which tend to diminish what clearly appears to be Klein’s shaping influence at that time.

responseSet.title: On being consoled: Engaging with Michel Fain’s paper ‘Mentalization and passivity’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On being consoled: Engaging with Michel Fain’s paper ‘Mentalization and passivity’

Richard Simpson

Richard Simpson

Richard Simpson   CONTACT Richard B. Simpson richardbsimpson@me.com

My first reading of this paper reminded me of a case presentation where a patient’s symptom was that he was often “inconsolable”, particularly toward evening and bedtime. The striking thing about the patient’s mother seemed to have been her lack of awareness of her child’s states of distress. One could view Michel Fain’s discussion of passivity and mentalization as raising the question “what is the psychoanalytic nature of being consoled?” I had wondered if the patient was not simply “inconsolable” by his nature but was repeating a scene of not being consoled or repeating a connection of non-consolation with someone. I will use the human experience of “consolation” to help us interact with Fain’s paper.

For an Anglophone reader, myself included, Fain’s paper is dense with metapsychological terms which the author uses to make connections between passivity and mentalization. Fain also tends to move from one subtopic to another in a manner that is not so easy to follow. There is a sense that this paper is part of long conversations and debates within French psychoanalysis whose context is difficult for the current reader to understand. My approach to the dense metapsychological terminology of Fain’s text will be to paraphrase or unpack parts of Fain’s text in order to elaborate the continuing relevance of the ideas in the paper.

The clearest entry point into clinically applicable ideas is on page four, where Fain discusses the role of the mother in the functioning of sleep as restorative for mind and body of the child. For sleep to occur, the child must mentally decathect or disinvest the sensory-motor system which links its mind to sensor input from external reality. According to Fain, to help the infant in this disinvestment of the external world, the mother needs to create a “tender cathexis” with the child in a comforting body to body contact that tends to shield the child from stimuli which would disrupt going to sleep. The paradox here is that the mother herself is part of the external reality from which the child must decathect. Fain addresses this paradox by theorizing an interesting idea about a background hallucination on the child’s part. I will be discussing later in the paper the role hallucination plays in his theorizing. Fain says this tender cathexis of cuddling reduces tension in the child, not by discharge of instinctual tension but through actions of the mother that have the “value of an introjection”. This “introjection” is the “vector for the psychosomatic formation of the ego”. Here, he is talking on the level of structural formation of the ego and how the ego gets built from such interactions. Maternal tenderness is seen as the “sublimation of a sexual drive with a displaced aim”. The child’s ability to relax into sleep preserves 487

responseSet.title: In memoriam: Albert Mason
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In memoriam: Albert Mason

Jon Tabakin

Jon Tabakin

Los Angeles
CA
USA

Jon Tabakin   jontabakin@gmail.com

Our beloved and revered Dr Albert Mason has passed away. Curiously, in supervision the week before, I had mentioned that phrase and Dr Mason wondered why we use the expression “passed away” when we mean to say that somebody has died. It was just like him to look at everything with an analytic mind, because one never knows when or where the unconscious will show itself. In so many ways Dr Mason taught us to listen to patients very attentively, to hear the subtle nuance, the unusual turn of phrase, the intricate interweaving of emotions and their phantasies. His favourite metaphor for analytic hearing was listening to chamber music, to listen for the way a theme is passed amongst the individual instruments, whose players work together in a very intimate way to create a musical conversation. And so it was my profound privilege to have had such an extended conversation with him. He was completely unique.

Dr Mason, it was the deepest and most intimate of pleasures to have known you for so many years.

responseSet.title: Michel de M’Uzan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michel de M’Uzan

Béatrice Ithier

Béatrice Ithier

Béatrice Ithier   beatriceithier@yahoo.fr

Michel de M’Uzan took his departure from us on the morning of 7 January 2018. With the “work of dying” having been carried out as he had conceived it, a life programme was completed that he had connected more specifically in the past few years with the vital-identital, the cornerstone of his theory of the two orders at the psychic dawn of being: the vital-identital and the sexual.

Born in Paris in October 1921 of a Tunisian Jewish father and a Danish Protestant mother, de M’Uzan's first marriage was to Marthe Robert, a literary critic, and his second was to Murielle Gagnebin, a psychoanalyst and lecturer in philosophy and the psychoanalysis of art at La Sorbonne Nouvelle, Paris III. De M’Uzan was a founding member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, and from 1969 to 1971 he was Director of the Institute of Psychoanalysis in Paris. His legacy is more extensive, however, because a great many analysts remained his students until the very last years of his life. Analysed by Maurice Bouvet, and having written a doctorate on Kafka in 1948, he leaves behind a substantial and profoundly original and powerful collection of psychoanalytic works.

His journey began as a literary one before it took a psychoanalytic direction. Close in his fiction to the nouveau roman, whose authors were among his friends, with imagination as well, and a literary critic and a writer, de M’Uzan's stories that developed in connection with his later discoveries and brilliant clinical insights include Les chiens des rois [The Dogs of Kings] (1954), Le rire et la poussière [Laughter and Dust] (1962) and Celui-là [That One] (1994).

A great friend of Antonin Artaud, as well as an expert on his work, he tended him in his dying days, and could speak about him with gravity and reverence. In Artaud's writings, de M’Uzan was able to hear those early, egotic “lallations,” consisting of onomatopoeias and akin to a cry, giving way to an early language that he conceptualised as an “idiom of identity” uttered by what he called the “paraphrenic twin.” This concept of the “paraphrenic twin” has nothing in common with the subject's object but is a sort of “transitional” subject brought into the world by the ego in order to be extricated from itself, and of which the subject must free itself.

responseSet.title: Roy Schafer, 1922–2018
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Roy Schafer, 1922–2018

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute; Editor, The Psychoanalytic Quarterly

Jay Greenberg   jaygreenberg275@aol.com

Psychoanalysis lost one of its giants when Roy Schafer died on 5 August 2018 at the age of 95.

Roy was educated at the City College of New York (B.A.), the University of Kansas (M.A.) and Clark University (Ph.D.), and completed his psychoanalytic training at the Western New England Institute. He was an influential presence in psychoanalytic and psychodynamic thinking for more than seven decades. He was only 23 years old when, with David Rapaport and Merton Gill, he co-authored the seminal Diagnostic Psychological Testing, and he had published two more important books about testing by the time he was 32. His focus shifted, in its object more than in its vision, to psychoanalytic theory beginning with “Generative Empathy in the Treatment Situation” (1959), and he produced 11 more books and more than 70 papers over the course of 54 years.

Roy was intellectually restless; he constantly probed the limits—and the limitations—of received theory, including the theories within which he was working at any moment. Trained in the ego psychological tradition under the strict mentorship of Rapaport, his first psychoanalytic book, Aspects of Internalization (1968), was anchored in an emended version of mainstream theory. But eight years later, breaking decisively with the ideas of the man to whom he referred in a poem (Roy wrote poetry throughout his life) as “tyrannical critic, unstinting benefactor,” he wrote the provocative and groundbreaking A New Language for Psychoanalysis (1976), in which he argued that a metapsychology relying on concepts of force and structure was inappropriate to a discourse designed to explore meaning, intention, choice, and action.

In later years Roy was drawn to and embraced the work of contemporary Kleinian thinkers, Betty Joseph, Michael Feldman, John Steiner, and Ron Britton among others. This final turn seemed anomalous and even shocking; to many, it appeared that he had turned his back on his ego psychological roots; Rapaport, after all, had characterized Klein’s object relations theory as an “id mythology.” But Roy, narrating the story of his professional evolution as seen through his own eyes, insisted that there was continuity in his sensibility from beginning to end, despite shifts of emphasis and interest. He compared the contemporary Kleinian focus on unconscious fantasy and attempts to actualize it in the transference—and the countertransference—to his own emphasis on narration and action. Strikingly, he saw the Kleinians’ exquisite attention to the here and now in treatment as similar to his own early appreciation of interaction in the testing situation as a source of information about the patient’s psychodynamics; this modification and expansion of technique drew Rapaport’s criticism.

responseSet.title: A review of Cold War Freud: Psychoanalysis in an age of catastrophes, by Dagmar Herzog
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A review of Cold War Freud: Psychoanalysis in an age of catastrophes, by Dagmar Herzog

Daniel Greenberg

Daniel Greenberg

Ann Arbor
Michigan
USA

Daniel Greenberg   daneli@umich.edu

Many factors contributed to the decline of psychoanalysis in the United States from the height of its popularity and influence in the 1950s and 1960s to its present-day attenuated situation. None was more painful to witness and experience than the hostility directed at psychoanalysis from some on the progressive Left. Even in the popular imagination, psychoanalysis became associated with misogyny, homophobia, authoritarianism, and a myopic disregard for broader social and political issues. Psychoanalysis was seen as having little to contribute to the struggles against inequality and privilege. Activists warned friends that in a typical analysis, political activism, the struggle for women’s rights, sexual liberation, homoerotic experience, and much more were likely to be interpreted as symptoms of unresolved oedipal conflicts, narcissistic character pathology, perversions, or worse. Psychoanalysis, once daring and revolutionary, came to be seen as hostile territory for those struggling with and against conformity, accommodation, complacency, and injustice. While a more sympathetic avant-garde worked to integrate psychoanalysis with philosophy, Marxism-socialism, feminism, post-modernism, and the struggle for racial, gender, and sexual equality, these efforts were often little reflected in the US institute-based psychoanalysis of the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s. The stereotype of the older male analyst, dispensing soul-crushing interpretations from a position of unassailable and unquestioned authority—driving some to despair or madness—had enough truth to it that one had to wonder whether the psychoanalytic community in the United States had deliberately embarked on a path certain to curtail its cultural, social, and political legitimacy.

Dagmar Herzog’s Cold War Freud is an important contribution to an ongoing transformation in our appreciation of the progressive political potential of psychoanalysis. This is a sweeping, breathtaking intellectual history of that period in which the regressive political reputation of psychoanalysis appeared to become cemented. Cold War Freud joins books such as Elizabeth Danto’s Freud’s Free Clinics (2005), Eli Zaretsky’s Political Freud (2015), and Russell Jacoby’s The Repression of Psychoanalysis (1983) as work that attempts to rescue a politically engaged and progressive psychoanalysis from its previous subaltern existence. Herzog’s knowledge is encyclopedic, and her linguistic competence enables a survey of the development of psychoanalysis and its political and cultural context across the globe. Herzog’s work evinces a theoretical sophistication and a psychoanalytic sensibility that will be greatly appreciated by analytic readers. She grapples with an enormously broad range of psychoanalytic ideas, theories, innovations, and developments. There is much here that will surprise and educate even the most well-read students of psychoanalysis. Herzog’s book is animated by a love for her subject matter and a tenacious commitment to a psychoanalysis that is still engaged in the project of human emancipation.

responseSet.title: Passivity, non-expression and the Oedipus in Japan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Passivity, non-expression and the Oedipus in Japan

Kenichiro Okano

Kenichiro Okano

Japan
Department of Education

Kenichiro Okano   CONTACT Kenichiro Okano kenokano@gmail.com Yoshida-honmachi, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8501 Japan
passivity, non-expression, Oedipus in Japan

The Japanese mentality is often characterized by its secretiveness and non-expression in various social contexts. Culturally, it is regarded as a virtue not to reveal our capacity and strength. The essence and knowledge of traditional art and craftsmanship at the highest levels is kept esoteric and should not be propagated to the general public, as truly essential and valuable points could not be verbally expressed or revealed. In the past, some psychoanalysts, such as T.Doi and O.Kitayama, have dealt with the issue of secretiveness and non-expression. This secretiveness and non-expression apparently have some merits and even a social role. It inhibits envy and competitiveness that a show of one’s capacity and strength would elicit in others. Also, they protect both one’s strength and vulnerability by keeping these invisible to others as well as to oneself, so that they remain unchallenged and unharmed by others. This invites an important question about the Oedipus in Japan. Is our secretiveness a way of avoiding Oedipal confrontations with others? Is it defeat or a higher tactic? The author considers that in Japanese society, the Oedipal dynamic exists in a rather convoluted way due to the passive and secretive nature of the Japanese people.

La mentalité japonaise est souvent décrite comme cultivant le secret et la non-expressivité dans différents contextes sociaux. D'un point de vue culturel, ne pas révéler ses capacités et sa force est considéré comme une vertu. L'essence et le savoir propres aux arts et métiers traditionnels les plus accomplis demeurent cachés et ne doivent pas être rendus publics, étant donné que les aspects véritablement essentiels et précieux ne sauraient être exprimés ou révélés verbalement. A une certaine époque, des psychanalystes comme T. Doi et O. Kitayama ont traité la question de cette culture du secret et de la non-expressivité. Cette culture du secret et de la non-expressivité n'est pas sans mérite et joue même un rôle social. Elle inhibe l'envie et la compétitivité que pourrait éveiller chez autrui la démonstration des capacités et de la force propres à un individu ; de même qu'elle protège la force et la vulnérabilité d'un individu en rendant celles-ci invisibles aux autres comme à l'individu en question, si bien qu'elles demeurent tout aussi incontestables qu'inaltérables. Cela invite à réfléchir à la question de l'Œdipe au Japon. Cette culture du secret est-elle un moyen d'éviter le conflit œdipien avec les autres ? S'agit-il là d'une défaite ou d'une tactique supérieure ? L'auteur de cet article considère que la dynamique œdipienne existe bien dans la société japonaise, mais sous une forme plutôt détournée en raison de la nature passive et secrète du peuple japonais.

Die japanische Mentalität ist oft durch Verheimlichung und Ausdruckslosigkeit in verschiedenen sozialen Kontexten charakterisiert. Kulturell gilt es als Tugend, seine Fähigkeiten und Stärken nicht zu zeigen. Die Essenz und das Wissen traditioneller Kunst und Kunstfertigkeit auf höchstem Niveau werden geheim gehalten und der Öffentlichkeit nicht preisgegeben, weil die wirklich wesentlichen und wertvollen Aspekte verbal nicht ausgedrückt werden können. In der Vergangenheit haben sich Psychoanalytiker wie T. Doi und O. Kitayama mit dem Thema Ausdruckslosigkeit und Verheimlichung auseinandersetzt. Beide Eigenschaften haben offensichtlich ihre Vorzüge und erfüllen sogar eine gesellschaftliche Funktion. Sie wirken dem Neid und der Eifersucht entgegen, die durch eine Demonstration der eigenen Fähigkeiten in anderen Menschen geweckt werden könnten. Zudem schützen sie die eigene Stärke wie auch die Vulnerabilität, indem sie diese vor anderen und vor sich selbst verbergen. Dies wirft die wichtige Frage nach dem Ödipuskomplex in Japan auf. Ist die Verheimlichung eine Methode, um ödipale Konfrontationen mit anderen zu vermeiden? Der Autor erläutert, dass sich die ödipale Dynamik in der japanischen Gesellschaft aufgrund der passiven und zur Geheimhaltung neigenden Mentalität des japanischen Volkes höchst kompliziert gestaltet.

Spesso la mentalità giapponese si contraddistingue per una propensione alla riservatezza e per la tendenza a non esprimersi in vari contesti sociali. Si tratta di una cultura presso la quale il non rivelare le capacità e i punti di forza di cui si dispone è anzi ritenuto una virtù. L’essenza e la conoscenza delle arti, come pure la maestria artistica e artigianale di alto livello, rappresentano un sapere di tipo esoterico che non si ritiene debba venire divulgato al pubblico: quanto di più essenziale e prezioso è sotteso a tale sapere non può infatti venire espresso o rivelato verbalmente. In passato, alcuni analisti tra cui ad esempio T. Doi e O. Kitayama si sono occupati del tema della riservatezza e della reticenza a esprimersi. Si tratta a quanto pare di inclinazioni che, oltre a non essere prive di vantaggi, hanno anche un ruolo preciso nei rapporti sociali: esse inibiscono ad esempio quell’invidia e quel senso di competizione che tipicamente vengono sollecitati negli altri quando una persona mostra le proprie abilità e la propria forza; inoltre, le medesime tendenze proteggono tanto la forza quanto la vulnerabilità del soggetto nella misura in cui le rendono invisibili sia agli altri sia al soggetto stesso – e quest’ultimo non viene di conseguenza sfidato o danneggiato dagli altri. Questo quadro solleva un’importante domanda circa il ruolo del complesso di Edipo in Giappone: può essere che la nostra tendenza alla riservatezza rappresenti in realtà un modo per evitare confronti di natura edipica con gli altri? Abbiamo a che fare con una forma di sconfitta o con una tattica di livello superiore? L’autore ritiene che, all’interno della società giapponese, la dinamica edipica si manifesti in una forma piuttosto contorta a causa della natura passiva e oltremodo riservata che caratterizza i giapponesi.

La mentalidad japonesa suele caracterizarse por su carácter reservado e inexpresión en diversos contextos sociales. No revelar las capacidades y las fortalezas propias es considerado culturalmente como una virtud. La esencia y el conocimiento del arte y la artesanía en sus más altos niveles se mantienen esotéricamente y no deben propagarse al público en general debido a que los elementos verdaderamente esenciales y valiosos no pueden expresarse ni revelarse verbalmente. En el pasado, algunos psicoanalistas, como T. Doi y O. Kitayama, han abordado el tema de la reserva y la inexpresión japonesa. Estas tienen aparentemente algunos méritos e incluso un rol social. Inhiben la envidia y la competitividad que produciría en otros la demostración de las capacidades y fortalezas propias. Además, protegen tanto las fortalezas como las vulnerabilidades propias al mantenerlas invisibles ante los demás y ante uno mismo, de modo que permanezcan incuestionadas e incólumes. Esto suscita una importante pregunta acerca del edipo en Japón. ¿El carácter reservado es una manera que tienen los japoneses de evitar confrontaciones edípicas con otros? ¿Es una derrota o una táctica superior? El autor considera que la dinámica edípica en la sociedad japonesa existe en una forma bastante intricada debido a la naturaleza pasiva y reservada del pueblo japonés.

responseSet.title: The beginning of psychoanalysis in Poland
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The beginning of psychoanalysis in Poland

Ewa Glod

Ewa Glod

Ewa Glod   ewaglo2@poczta.onet.pl

The history of the psychoanalytic movement in Poland is marked by recurrent loss. Therefore, it can be said that there were several beginnings.

Historical and social circumstances hindered or prevented the establishment of an institutional form of this movement for a long time. What is most characteristic of the psychoanalytic movement in Poland is the enthusiasm and passion, as well as the profiles of the most significant people dedicated to the idea of psychoanalysis.

Psychoanalytic thought in Poland was present almost from the very beginning of its formulation by Sigmund Freud. (Dybel 2016; Magnone 2016) At that time, and until 1918, Poland did not exist as a state, being divided into three parts among the Austro-Hungarian Empire, Germany, and Russia. For this reason, many people studied not only in Cracow or Warsaw, but also in the major centres of scientific life in the area—in Vienna, Berlin and, less often, in Moscow.

As the authors of the exhibition on the history of Polish psychoanalysis1 wrote:

Among the members of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society until 1938, as many as 19 people were born on land recognized as Polish. Some of these people were also members of the Berlin Society. They were: Sigfried Bernfeld, Berta Bornstein, Stefania Borstein-Windholz ,Gustaw Bychowski, Bernhard Dattner, Helena Deutsch, Ludwik Eidelberg, Salomea Gutmann-Isakower, Ludwik Jekels, Salomea Kempner, Edward Krohengold, Johaness Jaroslaw Marcinkowski, Hermann Nunberg, Beata Rank, Jozef Reinhold, Izydor Isaak Sadger, Max Schur, Eugenia Sokolnicka, Jenny Wälder.

Poland was a multinational country at the time, and the above mentioned people were of different nationalities. Some of them came from assimilated Jewish families, some from mixed Polish-Jewish families or from Polish-German families. However, most of them were strongly associated with Polish culture. Several people in this group felt Polish and maintained a strong bond with Poland. They tried to instill psychoanalytic ideas among Polish psychiatrists, translating Freud into Polish. We consider them pioneers of psychoanalysis in Poland. These were Ludwik Jekels, Hermann Nunberg and Gustaw Bychowski. Their profiles illustrate the significant influence of psychoanalysts coming from this region of Europe on the development of world psychoanalysis. In the face of the onslaught of fascism, they all emigrated to the United States and continued their clinical practice in New York. All of them had a significant contribution to the development of psychoanalysis there. Ludwik Jekels being temporarily in Sweden, along with Otto Fenichel, was also a co-founder of the Swedish-Finnish Psychoanalytic Society and was a key figure there. (Wojciechowska et al. 2012, my own translation)

responseSet.title: Freuds Atheismus im Widerspruch: Freud, Weber und Wittgenstein im Konflikt zwischen säkularem Denken und Religion [Freud's atheism in contradiction: Freud, Weber and Wittgenstein in the conflict between secular thought and religion]
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freuds Atheismus im Widerspruch: Freud, Weber und Wittgenstein im Konflikt zwischen säkularem Denken und Religion [Freud's atheism in contradiction: Freud, Weber and Wittgenstein in the conflict between secular thought and religion]**

Eckhard Frick

Eckhard Frick

Munich
Kaulbachstr. 31a

Eckhard Frick   Email: Eckhard.Frick@hfph.mwn.de

The project of classical modernity shapes the world in secular terms, institutionally and – especially – in the critique of religion. Freud is a late proponent of classical modernity, and his concept of ‘discontent in civilization’ represents the acme and the crisis of modernity. Both Charles Taylor and Zygmunt Bauman address the topos of discontent to conceptualize the post‐temporality of classical modernity, which has various names: liquid modernity, postmodernity, reflexive modernity, post‐secularism, the Spiritual Turn and so on.

Herbert Will explains the basic thesis of his book in relation to the lives of Sigmund Freud and his wife Martha Bernays: Sigmund, considered by many simply as an atheist, married Martha, who was from an orthodox Jewish family in Hamburg. Sigmund has to submit to a religious wedding ceremony because of official regulations but he rejects circumcision of his sons as well as a religious burial for himself. Sigmund (patriarchally!) forbids Martha to celebrate the Sabbath on Friday evening by lighting candles; Martha (unlike his daughter Anna) resumes this custom after Sigmund's death after a gap of some decades.

Will's thesis can be summarized as follows: Freud was able to externalize the contradictory and conflicted nature of his atheistic world view by ambivalent splitting, and to deal with it in relation to (external) religious institutions. Many psychoanalysts have adopted this pre‐analytic world view in misconceived obedience towards the ‘father’ of psychoanalysis. In post‐modernity, the basic religious conflict is (re‐)internalized. Anyone who confronts his own spirituality today does so much less often by engaging with churches and other religious communities than people did in the nineteenth or twentieth centuries. The unbelieving sons and daughters of modernity, as we can say with Habermas, are looking for post‐secular translations of religious language to exhaust the ‘semantic potentials’ of what is traditionally called creation, sin, redemption, belief and so on. Post‐secular people, including analysands and analysts, have in a sense already completed their emancipation from external religious oppressors. If despite everything they ‘are religiously inclined’, they are

exposed to the tension that arises between their religious belief and the secular way of thinking and must somehow negotiate the chasm between religious knowledge and worldly knowledge. The contradiction that Freud tried to resolve for himself externally re‐emerges for these contemporaries in an internalized and ‘modernized’ way. They must come to terms with being secular and religious as one person. In a sense they carry inside them the secular Freud and his religious wife, Sigmund and Martha at the same time. The major external and exclusive confrontation of the nineteenth century has at least in the European sphere become an internal conflict. It is part of individualization, which increasingly characterizes religion … The contemporary forms of religion now have little to do with the closed religious world view with which Freud had battled, at least in its orthodox and fundamentalist variants.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in China
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in China

Dorinda Welle

Dorinda Welle

Albuquerque
New Mexico
USA

Dorinda Welle   dwelle@salud.unm.edu

This collection offers instructive glimpses into the founding foreign and local psychoanalytic and psychodynamic training initiatives in China. Essays are presented predominantly by psychoanalysts, psychiatrists and psychologists from Austria, China, Norway, Taiwan, the USA and other locales who participated in the foreign trainings as teachers or students, including graduates who developed the first local training programs in China.

The book opens with “structures of mutual obligations and responsibilities lying in ruins” since “the dismantling of state collectives and the collapse of socialist morals in the 1990s (that) left young Chinese villagers in an ideological vacuum” (Hanson and Cuiming, p. 3). Researchers and clinicians offer compelling examples of how “the logic of capital” employs and disturbs cultural logics that long shaped family life, gender roles and family relationships in China.

In this “free market” context, young people in China experience social and psychological conflict between “collectivism” and “individualism.” Hanson and Cuiming found that while young men report seeking not marriage but “free love,” the responsibility of caring for elders has increasingly fallen to young women, who long for the “freedom” of lateral economic mobility: the freedom to leave one factory job for another and avoid binding job contracts.

Layers of political history undergird these “modern problems.” Following the birth of the Chinese republic in 1912, decades of devastating internal and international military conflict ensued, starting with the Chinese civil war and the near-simultaneous Sino-Japanese war. Plänkers (p. 33) summarizes:

During [these two wars, from 1927 to 1949], three million soldiers lost their lives, more than nine million civilians died in the crossfire and over eight million civilians died in non-military incidents. Ninety-five million people were exiled or fled the country. When the People’s Republic of China was established after a communist victory [in 1949], a new chain of catastrophes commenced. As Chang and Halliday (2005) state … , ‘Mao Tse-tung … was responsible for well over 70 million deaths in peacetime, more than any other twentieth-century leader.’ The Great Leap Forward from 1958–1962 led to the largest famine in the history of mankind, starving an estimated twenty to forty million people to death … A campaign was waged to annihilate evidence of China’s traditional culture.

Space & Psyche

Vera Camden

Vera Camden

Cleveland
OH
USA

Vera Camden   vcamden@kent.edu

Space & Psyche, a volume commemorating the interdisciplinary ‘Space + Mind’ symposium held at the University of Texas at Austin School of Architecture in 2007, offers “psychoanalytic perspectives on architecture and architectural perspectives on psychoanalysis” (e‐news, 2007). The essays collected here reflect Gaston Bachelard's conception, articulated in his classic text, The Poetics of Space, that inhabited space extends the human body. Such a conception has an affinity with the psychoanalytic premise that human experience and expression emanate from the maternal body. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews which will ultimately help the authors strengthen their contributions. We would also like to thank the section editors for all their hard work: Heinz Weiss (Education Section), Rachel Blass (Psychoanalytic Controversies), Abbot Bronstein (Analyst at Work), Caroline Bainbridge (Film Essays), Maria Cristina Garcia Vasconcellos (Executive Editor, IJP-es), Gustavo Jarast (Associate Editor IJP-es), Anne Patterson (Book Reviews), Bruce Reis (North American Book Reviews), and all our Book Review Associates. Without the dedication of section editors, reviewers, and readers the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys, and we would like to thank them for everything they have done to assist us during the past 12 months. In addition, we would like to thank the board members and editors of the IJP Annuals who produce collections of IJP papers in various languages. We wish to express our appreciation to our translators who carry out essential work for the Journal's aims. responseSet.title: Game of Thrones—A psychoanalytic interpretation including some remarks on the psychosocial function of modern TV series
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Game of Thrones—A psychoanalytic interpretation including some remarks on the psychosocial function of modern TV series

Gerald Poscheschnik

Gerald Poscheschnik

Innsbruck
Austria
Institute of Psychosocial Intervention, Faculty of Educational Sciences

Gerald Poscheschnik   CONTACT Gerald Poscheschnik gerald.poscheschnik@uibk.ac.at Institute of Psychosocial Intervention, Faculty of Educational Sciences, University of Innsbruck, Schoepfstr. 3, Innsbruck 6020, Austria

Applied psychoanalysis, Film, Art

Cultural products such as films or TV series can be understood as articulations of socio-political fantasies that contain conscious and unconscious elements. At first glance, Game of Thrones, a fierce medieval show full of violence and sex, appears to offer hardly any topical references. Psychoanalytic examination, which unveils the series from its unmistakable vesture, reveals essential elements and their relations that bear remarkable parallels to prevailing socio-political fantasies. The circumstances on the fictitious continent Westeros provide a diagnosis of a descending culture whose rulers are completely absorbed by intrigue for power and influence. They refuse to notice anything that does not serve in retaining their power. The North, cut off from Westeros by a tremendous wall, can be considered a repository for the repressed side effects of their own doing. These effects threaten their own existence in the long run. The distant continent Essos stands for the hope that there may be a shift or an alternative world. Psychoanalysis can not only contribute to the understanding of the unconscious content of TV series with its method, but can also theoretically explain how such series are individually received and mentally processed. For this, object relations theory from Winnicott is used. His concept of transitional space offers the possibility to understand mental processes that take place while watching television. The article concludes with general remarks about the meaning of television series as cultural phenomena and the object of research of psychoanalysis.

Psychic movement from revenge to redemption in Götz Spielmann’s Revanche (2008)**

Orit Dudai

Orit Dudai

Tel Aviv
Israel

Jerusalem

Orit Dudai   CONTACT Orit Dudai ordu@bezeqint.net Tel Aviv, Israel

Background

A dictionary definition of revenge describes it as “the action of inflicting hurt or harm on someone for an injury or wrong suffered at their hands.”1 This definition points to revenge as an action not confined to the verbal domain, and, as such, seems to pose a challenge for psychoanalytic talk. This difficulty seems to be reflected in the absence of the term in The New Dictionary of Kleinian Thought (Bott Spillius et al. 2011). One can wonder why such a major phenomenon in human existence, which has been addressed by literature throughout history,2 has been only meagrely theorized in psychoanalysis. This also contrasts with *This manuscript is part of my doctoral dissertation conducted at the Interdisciplinary Studies Unit, Hermeneutics and Cultural Studies, Bar Ilan University, Israel.510

responseSet.title: On De l’exaltation by Paul Denis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On De l’exaltation by Paul Denis

Aline Cohen de Lara

Aline Cohen de Lara

Paris
Paris Psychoanalytic Society, Editorial Board Member of the French Journal of Psychoanalysis

Aline Cohen de Lara   aline.cohendelara@orange.fr

If, as he claims, Paul Denis is not “sure he is one of the leaders of psychoanalysis in France,” (Braconnier, 2007) he is nonetheless one of the key authors in the French psychoanalytic landscape, as evidenced by the degree of interest evoked by his clinical and theoretical contributions in the fields of psychopathology and child and adult psychiatry, always intercalated with subtle evocations of their cultural and artistic connections. A training psychoanalyst in the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, he was director of the Revue Française de Psychanalyse from 1997 to 2004, editorial director of the collections Le Fil Rouge and Psychanalystes d’aujourd’hui at PUF, and the author and co-author of numerous works in which his clinical insights are presented in a clear and evocative style, accessible to beginners and discerning readers alike: Emprise et satisfaction(Mastery and Satisfaction), Le narcissisme [On narcissism], Les phobies (On Phobias), De l’âge bête: la période de latence (On the awkward age : the latency period); Rives et dérives du contretransfert (Countertransference: limits and transgressions).

His most recent work, De l’exaltation [On exaltation], is a compilation of revised texts of thirteen articles, each with its own dynamic, offering access to the author’s favourite themes. At the level of the psychic economy, these themes include the power of the drives and their derivatives; topographically, they include the distinctions between the superego, ego ideal and the ideal self, and between instance and imago. On the dynamic plane, P. Denis emphasizes the importance of understanding the potential of mobilizing psychological function, a process of mobilization and a source of change requiring the analyst’s participation. Faithful to Freudian thought, including terminology, Denis never separates theoretical progress from practice, starting from the clinical care of neurotic patients with those forms of psychopathology clustered under the term “psychological perversions” (40). But what makes these texts so original and interesting is the way the author communicates his joy in the work of association, brilliantly illustrated by multiple literary fragments. Each citation is a veritable jewel, carrying its own evocative power and remarkably illustrating the author’s statements while firing our senses and thoughts.

responseSet.title: Quatre essais sur la vie de l’âme by Jean-Claude Rolland
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quatre essais sur la vie de l’âme by Jean-Claude Rolland

Bertrand Colin

Bertrand Colin

Paris
66 avenue des Gobelins

Bertrand Colin   bcolin002@gmail.com

Quatre essais sur la vie de l’âme is Jean-Claude Rolland’s fourth book; a psychoanalyst of the French Psychoanalytical Association, he is well known for his works on the analogical interpretation and the consideration of the two dimensions of language and the image—that is, for taking up and extending Freud’s opposition between the word-representation and the thing-representation. This book addresses and develops the themes, concepts and intuitions of the three previous ones, along three main lines: a constantly inventive rereading of Freud and of his “readers”; a listening to patients that is attested, in each chapter, by lucid vignettes; and humanity’s religious and artistic achievements from its most ancient testimonies on prehistoric cave walls to the famous but nevertheless unknown work “Hérodiade” by the first great poet of modernity in France, Stéphane Mallarmé.

More than Rolland’s first three books—(Guérir du mal d’aimer [Healing from the ailment of loving], 1998), Avant d’être celui qui parle [before being the one who speaks] (2006), Les yeux de l’âme [the eyes of the soul] (2010))—which the reader will be able to read or will enjoy rereading for at a second stage, Quatre essais sur la vie de l’âme repays the effort of reading and rereading right through from beginning to end—probably much as it will be enjoyable to listen to an entire work by J.S. Bach, such as the Goldberg variations. This is not because the book has a linear construction. It appears instead that each of these four essays, just like its 15 chapters, was composed at the same time. The progression of a new idea is always associated with a variation on a known theme. Taking up a hackneyed concept from the psychoanalytic literature, particularly the case for the “object relationship,” easily side-tracks the reader by the strong tension that will be inflicted on him by the related reading of a concept that French 1485

responseSet.title: Intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis: A model for theory and practice, by Lewis Kirshner
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis: A model for theory and practice, by Lewis Kirshner

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

Ann Arbor and Farmington Hills
MI
USA

Ann Arbor
MI
USA

Ann Arbor
MI
USA

Michael Shulman   mshulmanphd@gmail.com

The vision of an integrative psychoanalytic theory persists, although it often is obscured by a present scene of our field’s division into camps. A fuller theorization of what occurs in the interaction of two human subjects that is the psychoanalytic encounter holds a potential key to the future of analysis as a discipline whose practitioners can reunite, rather than breaking into groups whose members cannot address each other. Thus the importance of this foundational work on understanding intersubjectivity by Lewis Kirshner.

The problem of the intersubjective is an ur-problem for psychoanalysis. Its existence was heralded in Freud’s metaphor of the analyst’s listening like a telephone receiver to the patient’s unconscious. According to Freud a properly-trained analyst was capable of proper reception, of an across-analysts reliable, reproducible, and in this sense, objective decoding of this unconscious. Rather than recognizing intersubjectivity, it might well be argued that Freud only recognized “objective-subjectivity” in the analyst’s relationship to the patient. Behind the scenes however, he seemed to know more: for example, when he urged Jung to “master the countertransference” as Jung’s relationship with Spielrein spun out of control. Concerned for the future of the psychoanalytic field in the public eye, Freud made clear in his letters, though he did not wish to advertise in his published writings, that he understood the difficulties easily, perhaps inevitably, introduced into the process by the analyst. Yet he could not bear publicly to leave aside the aspiration to a scientific objectivity embodied in the notion of a correctly-applied psychoanalytic method.

Among analysts, Lewis Kirshner is especially qualified to pull together a great diversity of matters that serve as component pieces to a potentially adequate understanding of intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis. A fully-invested student of Lacanian analysis in his earlier work, as well as an analyst with strong philosophical interests, Kirshner offers wide-ranging considerations of the concept of intersubjectivity and a critical account of writings on the intersubjective. The breadth of his reading is unprecedented in our literature. His is a full-scale consideration not only of the use of the intersubjective concept in psychoanalytic discourse, but also of the interfaces between intersubjectivity and neuroscience, between intersubjectivity and the study of human affects, and between intersubjectivity and semiotics.

responseSet.title: Comment on Mentalisation et passivité by Michel Fain
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comment on Mentalisation et passivité by Michel Fain

Fernando Riolo

Fernando Riolo

Fernando Riolo   CONTACT Fernando Riolo frnriolo@tin.it

This is not an easy paper to read, above all for those who have no familiarity with the author’s work and that of his closest precursors—in which case, the exhaustive introduction by Marilia Aisenstein and Marina Papageorgiou will be helpful. A second order of difficulty arises from the very dense and rhapsodic style of this writing, which precludes the systematic development of the argument and the full justification of some statements. However, for readers with the patience to work through the terseness of the discussion, this study will offer the opportunity of getting to know an original thinker of unquestionable theoretical depth.

In the brief space of this commentary it will not be possible to discuss the many questions addressed by the author, or to examine the numerous metapsychological concepts that are unconventionally employed in dealing with them. I will emphasise two points of particular interest, but which are also problematic.

The first is the revision of dream-theory through the application of Pierre Marty and Michel de M’Uzan’s concept of operative thought: that is, as Fain writes that a mental activity which negates oneiric hallucination, in that it is characterised by a close link with somatic reality. Hence, the vision of the dream process not only as the hallucinatory realisation of a repressed desire; but, at the same time, as the principal activity of mentalisation: which does not coincide with dream-work as we know it, but is, if anything, closer to its opposite. For Fain, it is in fact derived from the dream’s function as the guardian of sleep, a function directed at restoring the soma, and characterised by the dis-investment of the sensorimotor system, and, hence, of the hallucinatory activity. The two activities are even mutually antagonistic: whereas the first presupposes the existence of a satisfaction which has been experienced and repressed (and which the dream aims to reproduce by means of hallucinatory satisfaction), the second does not aim to reproduce anything and does not suppose any repression; it is a narcissistic reinvestment of somatic reality, from which the dream proceeds as a new inscription, a “new word on a blank screen.” It is, therefore, not an overcoming of the psychic repression, but a mentalisation of the soma.

responseSet.title: The incestual in Xavier Dolan’s cinematographic works
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The incestual in Xavier Dolan’s cinematographic works**

Bella Habip

Bella Habip

Istanbul
Turkey

Bella Habip   CONTACT Bella Habip bellahabip@gmail.com

The concept of “the incestual” was brought out by Racamier in the 1980s. It is relatively new, and an article on the theme of narcissistic perversion by this author was only first presented in the February 2014 issue of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (Racamier 2014). It occupies an important position in his theory since, starting from the realm of the psychoses and bonds with regard to the Oedipus complex, Racamier finally defined a specific psychic form in the psychic life of the individual, the family, and also in society. This psychic form bears the imprint of incest without this being carried out. It is a form of incest that is not phantasized or symbolized, in which the bond with the primary object has to be constantly remade and reconstructed. According to the author, behind every incestual relationship is hidden an evicted, unelaborated mourning. It encompasses a certain number of psychic phenomena that operate to bar access to the Oedipus complex: the rejection of mourning and the stopping of time, a predilection for acting out, an expansion of the unsaid and secrecy and therefore of denial, which blocks generational transmission, and a confusion of beings and generations, as well as uncompleted and interminable narcissistic seduction.

Dolan’s cinematographic work provides an excellent illustration of the clinical form of the incestual revealed through a particular type of mother–son relationship. This ambiguous, violent, passionate relationship that is damaging to both parties is omnipresent in all the director’s films. It is characterized by a mutual discord and the partners’ perpetual and vain quest to establish a mutual equilibrium. According to Racamier, this mutual equilibrium is the narcissistic unity of the earliest beginning, which is restored by the mother and child confronted with the agonizing experience of the separation of childbirth. This natural quest for unity by the mother–child couple is theorized by the author using the term “narcissistic seduction”, which is initially necessary but if it continues beyond a certain time will become the source of many obstacles to psychic growth for both the child and the mother. The Oedipus complex as an organizer of psychic growth is then thwarted and the oedipal conflict, that is the conflict of the origins, cannot be instigated. The Anti-Oedipus is not the opposite of the Oedipus complex any more than it precedes it, as the author explains. The conflict that governs the Anti-Oedipus complex unfolds “between the forces seeking narcissistic union with the primary mother and those instead seeking separation and autonomy” (Racamier 1995, 43 [translated quotations]), whereas the oedipal conflict is directed at both parents in the genital register of sexuality. The Oedipus complex concerns the child’s conflict towards his two parents, therefore between three parties, whereas in the Anti-Oedipus it is the child and mother who are *Translated by Sophie Leighton.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Greening of psychoanalysis. Andre Green’s new paradigm in contemporary theory and praxis, edited by R.J. Perelberg and G. Kohon

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

Cambridge
MA

Howard Levine   hblevine@aol.com

The essays in this book originated in a conference organized by the British Psychoanalytical Society in September of 2015 as a memorial to the late Andre Green. They make a compelling case for the view that in exploring the analytic process at the limits of analysability—and given the patients who so often now appear in our consulting rooms do we have any other choice?—Green has taken up many of Freud's intimations and moved psychoanalysis towards a new paradigm—beyond neurosis—where the act (agieren) replaces the dream as the paradigmatic object of psychoanalytic investigation and theory. In the words of Fernando Urribarri, who worked closely with Green in the last 15–20 years of Green's life, the thrust of Green's oeuvre offers us the promise of a “new paradigm … and … not just the construction of yet another post-Freudian school or discourse” (p. 134).

This new paradigm encompasses two Freudian models for contemporary clinical practice that coexist in a dialectical relationship: the dream and the act (including play). The dream relates to neurosis, psychic organization and

… sexual desire, structured through fantasies and woven into representations … . This corresponds to the psychoneurotic mode of functioning. It assumes that the containing function of the framing structure of primary narcissism [in the patient] is well enough established to allow the analysis to concentrate on the content, in accordance with a mainly intrapsychic conflict axis. (Urribarri, p. 145)

In contrast, the action model, which Green insists lies at the heart of Freud's second topography (structural theory)

… originates in the movement of the drive. The latter can be linked to representation or can be evacuated through action. In other words, representation is a possible result, rather than a starting point. The action can take several forms (realized or not), but it always causes a short circuit in symbolization … . Deadly repetition compulsion takes the place of desire, while the 535

responseSet.title: Overview on the case of a seriously ambiguous patient: Some reflections on ambiguity and good faith
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Overview on the case of a seriously ambiguous patient: Some reflections on ambiguity and good faith**

Marina Montagnini

Marina Montagnini

Italian Psychoanalytical Association

Marina Montagnini   Contact Marina Montagnini m.montagnini@iol.it via Cavallotti 34/C Mestre-Venezia Italy, 30171
ambiguity, bad faith, good faith, “agglutinated core”, Bleger, ambiguous nucleus, primary identification, totalitarian ideology

The clinical case describes a severe ambiguous personality, characterised by the propensity to combine indiscriminate identifications in the Ego in order to make an identity “sui generis,” where contradictions coexist consciously without generating conflicts. The antinomies are accepted in the conscious area of the Ego and they make the best ground for a bad faith that is lived in complete good faith. The subject defends himself against the possibility of losing the ambiguous nucleus that constitutes the foundation of his identity and omnipotence. For this purpose, he mobilises depressive and paranoic pseudo-symptomatology. The ambiguity allows him to never face the aftermath of disastrous existential choices, envy or avidity, the weight of responsibility or guilt. The therapy, on the one hand, commits the patient to recognise ambiguity and to discriminate contradictions and, on the other hand, commits the analyst to recognise the possible appearance of her ambiguous core or its derivates. The study of ambiguity’s psychogenesis in the end is used as an observational peak of the totalitarian ideology where the elusion of the judgement skills facilitates the self-deception and risks eradicating from the individual species-specific characteristics, dehumanising the subject himself and others.

responseSet.title: Thinking in cases, by John Forrester
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking in cases, by John Forrester

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

London
UK
Social Sciences

Steven Groarke   s.groarke@roehampton.ac.uk

John Forrester tells us that he may have gone as a graduate student to Harvard, rather than Princeton, had he not clashed in the interview with Isaiah Berlin over eighteenth-century Newtonianism. I should like to have been a fly on the wall on that occasion. Forrester refers to the encounter as a “brush,” a particularly apt term for the minor but extraordinarily generative fights that tend to crop up in the interstices of his thought. The history of psychoanalysis seems to have impressed upon him, among other things, the value of “controversial discussions.” Forrester, in turn, has helped one see that the history of psychoanalysis is, to a large extent, a history of controversies. Skirmishing as a way of thinking, however, is not equivalent to all-out intellectual warfare, which is something that Forrester, with his rationalist English temper, did not go in for; he was not a zealot (he never joined that club).

How should we understand the skirmishes? I can offer a more prosaic, workaday example of the “brush” in my own case, an example (as we shall see) that keeps us squarely within Forrester’s ken. Many years ago, I was faced with a rather supercilious professor of sociology on an interview panel who wondered how anyone could be positively inclined towards psychoanalysis and Michel Foucault. Are we getting ourselves into a bit of a muddle? The question might have been more challenging had it been less condescending. It was, and is, perfectly obvious to anyone, with more than a passing interest in the matter, that Foucault was never going to do justice to Freud; the professor, however, was not inviting a discussion of the case. Needless to say, I did not get the job.

Meanwhile, although psychoanalysis and Foucault have proved to be decidedly odd bedfellows, my positive inclinations have not exactly condemned me to a life of splendid isolation over the past 40 years. Indeed, John Forrester has long been among the very best and most scrupulous of intellectual company—I might add, with no trace of disdain or dismissiveness in the blunt and abrupt manner that is nonetheless evident in his approach. Forrester did not suffer fools gladly. I recall an occasion when, having judged the address from the 1494

responseSet.title: From the unrepresentable to the intersubjective: the case of a high-functioning autistic adolescent
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the unrepresentable to the intersubjective: the case of a high-functioning autistic adolescent**

Mauro Manica

Mauro Manica

Novara
Italy

Mauro Manica   CONTACT Mauro Manica mamanica@libero.it
autism, field theory, reverie, dreams, mentalization, representability

This paper presents the case of a patient on the autistic spectrum (ASD) and proposes a theoretical and technical model which seeks to illustrate the characteristics of the relational and intersubjective perspective in psychoanalysis. Along a complementary axis this perspective combines the knowledge which psychoanalytic research, starting from Kanner and Asperger, has developed through the studies by among others Tustin and Meltzer. The model is conceived from the vertex of a psychoanalysis which seeks to deal with disorganized and unrepresentable states of mind. Important is the personality of the analyst, who must be prepared to experience nothingness, meaninglessness and the chaos of a contiguous-autistic (CA) position. We can then consider a wider oscillation in the field in addition to PS↔D, namely CA↔PS↔D. Disorganized states of mind exist that result from a cumulative trauma which occured very early on, during the pre-natal or at least pre-verbal and pre-representational stages of psychic development.

Such states then become the effect of a basic deficit that the analytic field can oneirically transform into trauma which, through reciprocity, micro-attunements, the encounter with the analyst's negative capability and rêverie, can then evolve into a traumatic experience which can finally be subjected to symbolic alphabetization.

responseSet.title: Projective identification and “telepathic dreams”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Projective identification and “telepathic dreams”

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina

Bogotá
Colombia
Department of Psychiatry

Alfonso Sánchez-Medina   CONTACT Alfonso Sánchez-Medina alsanmed@hotmail.com Carrera 9 No.116.20, (908), Fundación Santa Fe, de Bogotá, Bogotá, Colombia

Intersubjectivity, contemporary kleinians, countertransference, dreams, phantasy, projection, containment/reverie

Based on the pioneering works by Freud and other authors with regard to telepathic dreams, and specifically those related to Klein’s projective identification and latterly developed by Bion and Grinberg, the author reviews some concepts associated with the theory of intersubjectivity. An example of a telepathic dream that emerged within an analytic process is integrated with these concepts in order to propose several hypotheses about their genesis. The main hypothesis is that projective identification and counter-identification, in their normal and abnormal forms, are the oneiric basis of communication. One member of the analytic pair transmits unconsciously to the other the contents of his real life, and the other one dreams about it; this way the dominant emotions and phantasies are made conscious. An emotionally intense climate, especially with abandonment phantasies, is required to achieve this type of communication. These dreams, infrequent in analysis, underscore intersubjective creativity, the “analytic third” described by Ogden. The author mentions some concepts based on neuroscience and quantum physics as possible explanations for the biological basis of unconscious communication.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the “squiggle game” to “games of reciprocity” towards a creative co-construction of a space for working with adolescents**

Alberto Stefana and Alessio Gamba

Alessio Gamba

Brescia
Italy

Monza
Italy

Alessio Gamba  

Adolescent psychoanalysis, Creativity, Symbolization, Squiggle game, Technique

The “squiggle game” is, above all, a method for relating and encouraging mutual exchange between the analyst and the patient (no matter if child, adolescent, or adult), enabling him to experience holding and freely explore different communication possibilities. After having explored the “technique” as it has been developed by Winnicott, this study also exposes some theoretical considerations, and some variations in the basic technique, brought together by the crucial role played by reciprocity: “Me a little and you a little.” The paper is a clinical case with a Chinese adolescent.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic training—who is afraid of evaluation?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic training—who is afraid of evaluation?

Shmuel Erlich and Mira Erlich-Ginor

Mira Erlich-Ginor

Jerusulem
Israel

Mira Erlich-Ginor  

Supervision, evaluation of candidates, psychoanalytic training, basic assumptions, training analysts, the three models

Many aspects of the institutional life and organizational functioning of psychoanalysis deal with and are affected by evaluation. This paper focuses on one manifestation of this difficulty, namely the evaluation of candidates in psychoanalytic training. Opinions vary regarding its place, necessity, and contribution. Although it is at least nominally practiced in many institutes, it is nonetheless fraught with difficulty. We explore some of the sources and nature of what hampers the function of evaluation of candidates, employing Bion’s Basic Assumptions and their effect on institutional dynamics. Related issues include the ambiguity surrounding the role and authority of the supervisory role and the present theoretical diversity. The role and function of the supervisor is central to this evaluation and focal in our discussion. Our presentation draws on and makes use of the findings of the EPF End of Training Evaluation Project (ETEP).
