responseSet.title: John Rickman behind the scenes: The influence of Lewin's field theory on practice, countertransference, and W.R. Bion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

John Rickman behind the scenes: The influence of Lewin's field theory on practice, countertransference, and W.R. Bion

R.D. Hinshelwood

R.D. Hinshelwood

Colchester
UK
Department of Psychosocial and Psychoanalytic Studies

R.D. Hinshelwood   CONTACT R.D. Hinshelwood bob.hinsh@gmail.com Department of Psychosocial and Psychoanalytic Studies, University of Essex, Colchester, UK

Kurt Lewin, J.F. Brown, John Rickman, Social field theory, Galileo, Life-space, War Office Selection Boards, Northfield experiment: W.R. Bion, Group mentality, Therapeutic community, Countertransference

This article introduces Kurt Lewin's field theory of social groups to show the impact at the time (1930s and 1940s) on the conceptualisation of the clinical setting in psychoanalysis. John Rickman is now little known, but these ideas were brought by him to psychoanalysis. Through his mentoring of his one-time analysand, Wilfred Bion, they influenced psychiatry during World War II, and helped to set the direction of a number of subsequent developments, including the evolution of a form of group therapy, the Tavistock Institute approach to organisations, and not least the new thinking about countertransference in clinical practice after 1950.

Dans cet article, l'auteur présente la théorie du champ des groupes sociaux de Kurt Lewin afin de montrer l'impact que cette théorie a exercé sur la conceptualisation du cadre clinique en psychanalyse à l'époque des années trente et quarante. C'est John Rickman, tombé relativement dans l'oubli aujourd'hui, qui a pourtant introduit ces idées en psychanalyse. Et c'est au travers de leur transmission à son ex-analysant, Wilfred Bion, qu'elles ont influencé la psychiatrie pendant la Seconde guerre mondiale et contribué à fixer une orientation, depuis lors, à toute une série de développements, y compris l'évolution d'une forme de thérapie de groupe, suivant le mode d'approche des organisations caractérisant l'Institut de la Tavistock, et jusqu'aux nouvelles façons d'appréhender le contre-transfert dans la pratique clinique à partir de l'année 1950.

In diesem Beitrag stelle ich Kurt Lewins Feldtheorie sozialer Gruppen vor, um zu zeigen, wie diese damals (in den 1930er und 1940er Jahren) die Konzeptualisierung des klinischen Settings in der Psychoanalyse beeinflusste. John Rickman ist heute kaum noch bekannt, obwohl diese Überlegungen ihn zur Psychoanalyse brachten. Durch seinen einzigen Analysanden wiederum, Wilfred Bion, übten sie Einfluss auf die Psychiatrie im 2. Weltkrieg aus und gaben seither die Richtung einer ganzen Reihe von Entwicklungen vor, u.a. einer neuen Form der Gruppentherapie, dem für das Tavistock Institute charakteristischen Verständnis von und der Arbeit mit Organisationen und nicht zuletzt einer neuen Sichtweise der Gegenübertragung in der klinischen Praxis nach 1950.

In questo articolo introdurrò la teoria del campo dei gruppi sociali di Kurt Lewin per illustrare l’impatto che essa ebbe al tempo in cui venne formulata (gli Anni ’30 e ’40 del Novecento) sulla concettualizzazione psicoanalitica del setting clinico. La figura di John Rickman è oggi poco nota, ma fu lui a introdurre queste idee nell’ambito della psicoanalisi. Insieme al suo ex paziente Wilfred Bion, di cui era divenuto nel frattempo mentore, Rickman influenzò la psichiatria durante la seconda guerra mondiale e contribuì fin da allora a orientare una serie di sviluppi in seno alla psicoanalisi, tra cui l’evoluzione di una forma di terapia di gruppo, l’approccio Tavistock alle organizzazioni e – fatto non meno importante – il nuovo modo in cui a partire dal 1950 si è pensato al controtransfert nella pratica clinica.

En este artículo, el autor presenta la teoría del campo de los grupos sociales de Kurt Lewin, para mostrar su impacto en aquel momento (las décadas de 1930 y 1940) sobre la conceptualización del encuadre clínico en psicoanálisis. John Rickman es poco conocido hoy, aunque estas ideas fueron introducidas por él al psicoanálisis. Y mediante su tutoría a quien otrora fuera su analizado, Wilfred Bion, influyeron en la psiquiatría en la Segunda Guerra Mundial, y ayudaron a establecer la dirección de una serie de desarrollos desde entonces, entre ellos: la evolución de una forma de terapia grupal, el enfoque del Instituto Tavistock a las organizaciones y, no menos importante, la nueva concepción de la contratransferencia en la práctica clínica después de 1950.

responseSet.title: Time and the Garden of Eden Illusion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time and the Garden of Eden Illusion

John Steiner

John Steiner

London
UK
British Psychoanalytical Society

John Steiner   CONTACT John Steiner Johnsteiner28@gmail.com
illusion, time, psychic retreats

This article explores the timelessness of idealised psychic retreats and then describes the traumatic impact of time as the patient emerges from the retreat to face reality. The Garden of Eden Illusion involves a retreat where time stands still. Everything is perfect but nothing changes, there is no development, no frustration, no losses and no passion. When the patient confronts the real world, he is introduced to the facts of life and, for each of these facts, time is significant. If the trauma of disillusion is too abrupt or the pain of waiting too severe, defences are mounted against the impact of reality. These involve misrepresentations of the facts of life in which experiences that involve time are repudiated in favour of instant solutions based on omnipotence. These misrepresentations of reality easily become erotised to create perverse scenarios with an addictive quality that are particularly resistant to change. It is possible to delineate Narcissistic Perversions, Oedipal Perversions and Romantic Perversions as defences against time in relation to each of Money-Kyrle’s facts of life.

responseSet.title: Searching for the analyst’s reveries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Searching for the analyst’s reveries

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

MA
USA

Fred Busch   CONTACT Fred Busch drfredbusch@gmail.com
Reverie, psychoanalytic technique, psychoanalytic theory

Over the last 20 years the post-Bionians have begun nothing less than to spell out the beginning of the metabolizing process (reverie) in the analyst’s mind that takes place with under-represented mental states. This bold attempt leading to new discoveries, and its many possibilities for understanding patients, seems to have obscured differences amongst leading post-Bionians with regard to how they see the forms of reverie, and how they might best be worked with. With Bion’s perspective as a background, this paper explores three approaches, and how they differ with regard to whether one follows the views of early or late Bion. Technical issues associated with these views are raised. A clinical example is offered as one way to use reverie.

responseSet.title: From somatic pain to psychic pain: The body in the psychoanalytic field
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From somatic pain to psychic pain: The body in the psychoanalytic field**

Thomas Hartung and Michael Steinbrecher

Michael Steinbrecher

Düsseldorf
Germany

Köln
Germany
michael.steinbrecher@gmail.com

Michael Steinbrecher  

body, containment/reverie, countertransference, psychic pain, unconscious

The integration of psyche and soma begins with a baby's earliest contact with his or her parents. With the help of maternal empathy and reverie, β-elements are transformed into α-elements. While we understand this to be the case, we would like to enquire what actually happens to those parts of the affect which have not been transformed? For the most part they may be dealt with by evacuation, but they can also remain within the body, subsequently contributing to psychosomatic symptoms.

This paper describes how the body serves as an intermediate store between the psychic (inner) and outer reality. The authors focuses on the unconscious communicative process between the analyst and the analysand, and in particular on how psychosomatic symptoms can spread to the analyst's body. The latter may become sensitive to the analysand's psychosomatic symptoms in order to better understand the psychoanalytical process. Sensory processes (visual and auditory) and psychic mechanisms such as projective identification can serve as a means for this communication.

responseSet.title: The Lara case: A group analysis of initial psychoanalytic interviews using systematic clinical observation and empirical tools
responseSet.authorMast: Andrea Rodríguez Quiroga de Pereira,  Laura Borensztein,  Valeria Corbella and  Juan Carlos Marengo
responseSet.authorCitation: Rodríguez Quiroga de Pereira, A., Borensztein, L., Corbella, V. & Marengo, J. C.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Lara case: A group analysis of initial psychoanalytic interviews using systematic clinical observation and empirical tools

Andrea Rodríguez Quiroga de Pereira , Laura Borensztein , Valeria Corbella and Juan Marengo

Juan Marengo

Director of the IUSAM Department of Research

Secretary of the IUSAM Department of Research

Juan Marengo  

scientific research, psychoanalysis, empirical research, clinical case, training

With the objective of analysing the usefulness of empirical criteria for observing psychoanalytic cases, this article describes an experience of systematic clinical observation carried out by the Psychodynamic Clinical Research Team at the Psychoanalytic Association of Buenos Aires’ University Institute of Mental Health (Instituto Universitario de Salud Mental, IUSAM). A group of four psychoanalytic therapists, including the treating therapist, systematically analysed the first four interviews in the psychoanalytic treatment of a 32-year-old female patient, which was discontinued six months after it started. We used the combined frameworks of the Multilevel Observation and Three-Level Model to systematise the information. We also used a computer-assisted text- analysis system-the Discourse Attribute Analysis Program (DAAP) to measure Referential Activity. The Shedler-Westen Assesment Procedure (SWAP) was included as a diagnostic tool. We describe the application of these resources to our analysis of the four interviews, including examples of the clinical material. We discuss both the hypotheses developed by our clinical observation group and the usefulness of group exchange for understanding the therapeutic process in general. Finally, we discuss the implications of this type of systematic case study for clinical practice, training, and research in psychoanalysis.

Avec l'objectif d'analyser dans quelle mesure les critères empiriques sont utiles à l'observation de cas psychanalytiques, l'auteur de cet article décrit l'expérience d'observation clinique systématique menée par l'équipe de recherche en clinique psychodynamique de l'Institut universitaire de santé mentale (IUSAM) de l'Association psychanalytique de Buenos Aires (ApdeBA). Un groupe de quatre thérapeutes psychanalystes, y compris le thérapeute-traitant, a analysé de façon systématique les quatre premiers entretiens de la cure psychanalytique d'une patiente âgée de 32 ans, qui se sont déroulés à Buenos Aires (Argentine) ; cette cure a été interrompue au bout de six mois. Afin de systématiser l'information, le groupe de thérapeutes a eu recours aux schémas conjugués du Modèle d'observation à multiples niveaux (Thomä y Kächele 1989 y 1997; y Kächele et al., 2009) et du Modèle à trois niveaux de Bernardi (3 L-M Bernardi, 2015a y 2015b). En outre, le groupe a utilisé le système informatique d'analyse de textes DAAP – programme d'analyse des attributs du discours (Maskit, Bucci y Roussos, 2002) – pour mesurer l'activité référentielle. En ce qui concerne l'outil diagnostique, il s'est servi de SWAP – le procédé d'évaluation de Shedler et Westen (Shedler y Westen 2004, 2006). L'auteur de l'article décrit l'application de ces ressources à l'analyse des quatre entretiens et donne des exemples extraits du matériel clinique. Il examine également les hypothèses émises par le groupe d'observation clinique et l'utilité des échanges au sein du groupe quant à la compréhension du processus thérapeutique dans son ensemble. Enfin, il discute des effets de ce type d'étude systématique de cas sur la pratique clinique, la formation et la recherche en psychanalyse.

Mit dem Ziel, die Nützlichkeit empirischer Kriterien für die Beobachtung von Psychoanalysefällen zu analysieren, beschreibt dieser Artikel eine systematische klinische Beobachtung, die von dem Psychodynamic Clinical Research Team at the Psychoanalytic Association des Institute of Mental Health an der Universität Buenos Aires (Instituto Universitario de Salud Mental, IUSAM) durchgeführt wurde. Eine Gruppe von vier psychoanalytischen Therapeuten einschließlich der Behandlerin nahm systematische Analysen der ersten vier Interviews in der psychoanalytischen Behandlung einer 32-jährigen Patientin vor. Die Therapie fand in Buenos Aires, Argentinien, statt und wurde sechs Monate nach Beginn abgebrochen. Wir benutzten kombiniert das Multilevel Observation Model (Thomä und Kächele 1989, 1997; Kächele et al. 2009) und Bernardis Three-Level Model (3-LM) (Bernardi 2015a, 2015b), um die Daten zu systematisieren. Wir arbeiteten zudem mit einem computergestützten Textanalysesystem, dem Discourse Attribute Analysis Program (DAA?) (Maskit et al. 2002), um die Referenzaktivität zu messen. Das Shedler-Westen Evaluation Procedure (SWAP) (Shedler und Westen 2004, 2006) wurde ebenfalls als Diagnoseinstrument herangezogen. Wir beschreiben die Anwendung dieser Hilfsmittel auf unsere Analyse der vier Interviews und illustrieren sie mit klinischem Material. Wir erläutern sowohl die Hypothesen, die unsere Beobachtungsgruppe entwickelte, als auch die Nützlichkeit des Gruppengesprächs für das allgemeine Verständnis des therapeutischen Prozesses. Abschließend diskutieren wir die Implikationen einer solchen systematischen Fallstudie für die klinische Praxis, die Ausbildung und die Forschung in der Psychoanalyse.

Con l’obiettivo di analizzare l’utilità dei criteri empirici per l’osservazione di casi psicoanalitici, il presente articolo descrive un’esperienza di osservazione clinica sistematica effettuata dal Gruppo di Ricerca di Clinica Psicodinamica dell’Istituto Universitario di Salute Mentale (IUSAM), parte dell’Associazione Psicoanalitica di Buenos Aires. Un gruppo di quattro terapeuti di indirizzo psicoanalitico, di cui faceva parte anche la terapeuta che aveva in cura i pazienti, ha condotto un’analisi sistematica dei primi quattro colloqui di una paziente di 32 anni. Il trattamento, svoltosi a Buenos Aires, è stato interrotto a sei mesi dal suo inizio. Per sistematizzare le informazioni raccolte il gruppo ha utilizzato il Modello di Osservazione Multilivello (Thomä e Kächele 1989, 1997; Kächele et al., 2009) e il Modello a Tre Livelli di Bernardi (3 – LM)(Bernardi 2015a, 2015b), combinandoli tra loro. Inoltre, per la misurazione dell’Attività Referenziale il gruppo si è valso di un sistema computerizzato per l’analisi linguistica – Discourse Attribute Analysis Program (DAAP) (Maskit et al., 2002). Come strumento diagnostico è stata usata anche la procedura di valutazione di Shedler-Westen (SWAP) (Shedler and Westen 2004, 2006). L’articolo descrive nel dettaglio il modo in cui questi strumenti sono stati applicati all’analisi dei quattro colloqui, presentando inoltre alcuni esempi di materiale clinico. Vengono quindi vagliate sia le ipotesi formulate dal gruppo di osservazione clinica sia l'utilità degli scambi di vedute all’interno del gruppo ai fini della comprensione del processo terapeutico. Si discutono infine le implicazioni che questo tipo di studi sistematici di singoli casi clinici può avere per la pratica clinica, per la formazione e per la ricerca psicoanalitica.

Con el objetivo de analizar la utilidad de criterios empíricos para la observación de casos psicoanalíticos, este artículo describe una experiencia de observación clínica sistemática realizada por el Equipo de Investigación en Clínica Psicodinámica del Instituto Universitario de Salud Mental (IUSAM) de la Asociación Psicoanalítica de Buenos Aires (APdeBA). Un grupo de cuatro terapeutas psicoanalíticos, incluida la terapeuta tratante, analizaron de manera sistemática las primeras cuatro entrevistas de un tratamiento psicoanalítico de una paciente mujer de 32 años de edad, llevado a cabo en la Ciudad de Buenos Aires, Argentina; el cual fue interrumpido a los seis meses de su inicio. Se utilizaron los esquemas combinados del Modelo de Observación Multinivel (Thomä y Kächele 1989 y 1997; y Kächele et al., 2009) y del Modelo de Tres Niveles de Bernardi (3 L-M Bernardi, 2015a y 2015b) para la sistematización de la información. Además se utilizó el sistema computarizado de análisis de textos DAAP: Programa para el Análisis de Atributos del Discurso (Maskit, Bucci y Roussos, 2002) para medir la Actividad Referencial. Y como herramienta diagnóstica se incluyó el SWAP: Procedimiento de Evaluación de Shedler y Westen (Shedler y Westen 2004, 2006). Se detalla la aplicación de estos recursos al análisis de las cuatro entrevistas estudiadas, brindando ejemplos de material clínico. Se analizan las hipótesis a las que el grupo de observación clínica llegó y la utilidad del intercambio grupal para la comprensión del proceso terapéutico en general. Finalmente, se discuten las implicancias de este tipo de estudios sistemáticos de caso para la práctica clínica, la formación y la investigación en psicoanálisis.

responseSet.title: Freud's ‘transference’: Clinical technique in the ‘Rat Man’ case and theoretical conceptualization compared
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's ‘transference’: Clinical technique in the ‘Rat Man’ case and theoretical conceptualization compared**

Michael Diercks

Michael Diercks

Vienna
Austria
Gyrowetzgasse 11, A‐1140

Michael Diercks   Email: michael.diercks@wpv.at
Clinical theory, comparative methods, Freud's practice, Rat Man, transference, Strachey's translation

A considerable gap exists between clinical psychoanalytic concepts and psychoanalytic practice. It can be traced back to the early beginnings of psychoanalysis and to Freud's own handling of concepts that he had developed himself. Focusing on the concept of ‘transference’ that Freud in several steps coined so precisely from his experiences with hysteric patients and especially from his understanding of the ‘Dora’ case, it can be shown that he – seen from today – could not fully apply the meaning of his own concept in the later treatment of the so‐called ‘Rat Man’. Freud's ‘Original record of the case’ is used to scrutinize his way of understanding and handling the transference with this patient. To a substantial extent transference as well as counter‐transference was rather enacted than understood in this case, partly due to Freud's own personal and scientific interests and to his ambitions to use this case as a demonstration of his therapeutic approach. In order to show this, it is unavoidable to correct several blurry or even misleading passages of Strachey's translation. Findings from numerous workshops using ‘comparative clinical methods’ indicate that up till now we analysts – like Freud – have great difficulties in applying Freud's incredible insight that “a whole series of former psychic experiences comes alive not as the past but as the present relationship to the person of the physician” (Freud, 1905c [1901], p. 279/280, my translation).

responseSet.title: Introduction to “A special section on Lectures on Technique by Melanie Klein”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to “A special section on Lectures on Technique by Melanie Klein”

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass   CONTACT Rachel B. Blass r.blass@ucl.ac.uk

The recently published Lectures on Technique by Melanie Klein edited and introduced by John Steiner (2017) is a small book of great interest. It comprises two previously unpublished texts that centre on issues of technique. One is a series of lectures that Melanie Klein offered candidates at the British Psychoanalytic Society in 1936, and the other a transcription of seminars that she held with young British analysts in 1958. As Klein states at the start of the lectures, they deal “with details of technique … [but] at the same time aim at clarifying and exemplifying the main principles that … underlie psychoanalytic technique and theory, since these two are inseparable” (2017, p. 29). In line with this effort, the lectures include clear descriptions of Klein’s views of the unconscious, the superego, phantasy, the conflictual relationship between love and hate, guilt, anxiety and other basic dimensions of analytic theory, but with a special focus on how these dimensions and their discovery shape technique, and in turn how technical developments that have taken place have shaped our theories (e.g. how Freud’s discovery of unconscious guilt led to more in-depth interpretation and how working at these depths advanced our knowledge of unconscious phantasy). This relationship between theory and practice plays a central role throughout the lectures. In fact, at one point, Klein goes as far as to say that “a contribution to theory is only of value if in the long run it helps towards the development of technique” (p. 38)—emphasizing that psychoanalysis’ true contribution lies in the clinical realm.

The principles that Klein clarifies in the lectures pertain also to the analyst’s frame of mind. As she explains: “Since we come to look at the person’s mind through the medium of our own mind, it is evident that very much, if not everything, depends on our frame of mind in which we set out to work” (p. 29). The desirable analytic frame of mind, the “analytic attitude,” as Klein conceives of it, is largely determined by what she regards as the sole analytic aim, namely, the exploration of the patient’s unconscious mind (“cure” here regarded as a consequence of seeking this aim). What is required for this is an “urge to explore” and an “unfailing desire to ascertain truth” (p. 29), a willingness to put aside diagnostic labels and preconceived plans for the patient, so that we can “learn step by step everything about the patient from himself” (p. 30). This requires imagination, “and a capacity for flexibility and versatility” (p. 52). It is a state of mind that is both eager and patient, “detached from its subject and at the same time fully absorbed in it” (p. 30). Klein comments that in this frame of mind everything aside from our patient’s mind loses importance, “including our own personal feelings” (p. 29). But she goes on to clarify that this should not be misconstrued as a mechanical stance: “The analyst is only capable of approaching and understanding his patient as a human being if his own emotions and 947

responseSet.title: The riddle of anxiety: Between the familiar and the unfamiliar
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The riddle of anxiety: Between the familiar and the unfamiliar

Rosine Perelberg

Rosine Perelberg

London
UK

Rosine Perelberg   CONTACT Rosine Jozef Perelberg rperelberg@perelberg.com 35 Hodford Road, London, NW11 8NL, UK

Anxiety, helplessness, excess, murdered father, dead father, temporality, après-coup, transsexual

It is difficult to say what anxiety is, Freud tells us. This paper suggests various dimensions of anxiety. Anxiety evokes the original experience of helplessness; it is an affective state that is accompanied by physical sensations and bodily symptoms—expression of an excess that it is not possible to process psychically. Anxiety is also linked to the fear of loss of the imaginary integrity of the body, as well as of primary objects. Furthermore, anxiety marks the passage from the world of the narcissistic father and/or mother, in which the individual is alienated from his own history, to the dead father configuration that inserts the individual into his subjective temporality in the après-coup of an analysis. A detailed narrative of an analysis that gave rise to these ideas is presented. In this analysis the transsexual emerges to give shape to something that had not previously reached representation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autism and psychoanalysis in the French context**

Didier Houzel

Didier Houzel

Didier Houzel   CONTACT Didier Houzel didier.houzel@dbmail.com

As is the case in Western countries as a whole, the application of psychoanalysis in France to the treatment of autistic children has been subjected over the last few decades to more or less violent criticism, and even to obstacles of various kinds, which make it increasingly difficult. This takes on, of course, different colourings from one country to another, depending on each country’s history, culture, traditions, and the current organisation of psychiatric and psychotherapeutic care. This article aims to describe these particular features in the French context. With this end in mind, it is necessary to take into account the evolution not only of the notion of infantile autism but also of psychoanalysis, as well as of their interface.

The advent of infantile autism in France

As much as French psychiatry had played a pioneering role in the history of psychiatry, the descriptions of Leo Kanner and Hans Asperger were only heard of relatively late in France. In an authoritative work, the child psychiatrist and psychoanalyst Jacques Hochmann (2009) emphasised the similarity of the descriptions of those who discovered infantile *Translated by Andrew Weller.725

responseSet.title: Microprocesses at the body-mind border in the psychoanalysis of psychosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Microprocesses at the body-mind border in the psychoanalysis of psychosis

Uta Karacaoğlan and Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Germany

Italy

Riccardo Lombardi  

Psychosomatics, psychotic states, technique, body, containment / reverie, countertransference, unconscious

This article presents clinical material involving the treatment of two patients suffering from delusional paranoia and psychosomatic disorders as the basis for investigating the significance of the body–mind border – with particular attention to the working through of body–mind dissociation. In the context of interpersonal transference-countertransference events, the authors focus on the importance of the analysand’s transference onto his own body and the analyst’s use of somatic countertransference. When the analysand is able to develop a relationship to his own body [as a “Concrete Original Object” (COO)], he starts to experience and to register mentally his body at a sensory level, consequently improving his awareness of reality and cognitive capacity. The approach outlined here is decidedly relational in nature – it mandates that the analyst draw from his own perceptions based on particularly intense bodily and emotional participation as the basis for understanding the analysand’s state of mind–body. The technical approach described here is a working through centred on the analysand’s body, strengthening his vertical axis with only minimal interpretative reliance on interpersonal transference.

responseSet.title: Mentalization and passivity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mentalization and passivity

Michel Fain

Michel Fain

75002 Paris

Michel Fain  

The Report presented in 1962 at the Congress of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society (SPP), under the title Aspect fonctionnel de la vie onirique [The functional aspect of oneiric life] by Christian David and myself, included a project aimed at creating surprise. For some time already, Pierre Marty, Michel de M’Uzan, Christian David and myself had been working together on the problems raised by psychosomatics. In this connection, the name of the École psychosomatique de Paris [Paris School of Psychosomatics] was given to us. In other words, the reporters knew that, following their text, the intervention of Michel de M’Uzan and Pierre Marty would surprise the public and pose an unexpected problem: whilst the text of the Report had sought to clarify the mechanisms of the dream-work and their effectiveness as guardians of sleep, the paper presented by Marty and de M’Uzan signalled the existence of a form of mental activity negating the efficacy of oneiric hallucination. However, this mental activity—la pensée opératoire 1 to call it by its name—was characterized by its close relationship with reality and the concrete task that the latter required. The task in question is, moreover, only envisaged in terms of its effectiveness, and not in terms of the personal history of the subject who is interested in the development of his own thought processes.

During this congress, the discussion did not consider the collusion that existed between the Report and the paper by Marty and de M’Uzan, in spite of the fact that everyone knew about the relations that existed between the members of the École de Paris. I think, moreover, that I myself had not fully grasped the meaning that emerged once the two texts were compared. Not a single intervention of the Congress participants attempted to point out that these four discussants, known for their joint studies, were signalling that two types of discourse could be described as representing one thing and its contrary: on one side, the complexities of the dream-work in reaching its aim, namely, sleep, and, on the other, a logical and rational discourse adapted to the task.

responseSet.title: Hypochondria as an actual neurosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypochondria as an actual neurosis**

Bernd Nissen

Bernd Nissen

Berlin
Germany

Bernd Nissen   Email: Bernd.Nissen@gmx.de
hypochondria, actual neurosis, autistoid, breakdown, projective-introjective dynamic, introject

Freud defined hypochondria as an actual neurosis. In this paper the actual neurosis will be interpreted as unbound traumatic elements which threaten the self. In severe hypochondria, breakdowns have occurred, as outlined by Winnicott. The nameless traumatic elements of the breakdown have been encapsulated. The moment these encapsulated elements are liberated, an actual dynamic takes place which threatens the self with annihilation. Projective identification is not possible because no idea of containment exists. The self tries to evacuate these elements projectively, thus triggering a disintegrative regression. However, the object of this projection, which becomes a malign introject, is felt to remove the remaining psychical elements, forcing the worthless residue back into the self. In a final re-introjection, the self is threatened by unintegration. To save the self, these elements are displaced into an organ which becomes hypochondriacal, an autistoid object, protecting itself against unintegration and decomposition. An autistoid dynamic develops between the hypochondriac organ, the ego and the introject. Two short clinical vignettes illustrate the regressive dynamical and metapsychological considerations.

responseSet.title: The feeling of real: On Winnicott’s “Communicating and Not Communicating Leading to a Study of Certain Opposites”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The feeling of real: On Winnicott’s “Communicating and Not Communicating Leading to a Study of Certain Opposites”

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

San Francisco
CA
USA

Thomas Ogden   CONTACT Thomas H. Ogden thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com
isolate, core self, subjective object, cul-de-sac communication

In “Communicating and Not Communicating Leading to a Study of Certain Opposites,” Winnicott introduces the radical idea that communicating with subjective objects is “cul-de-sac” communication (communication not meant for any external or internal object), but which nonetheless endows every aspect of one’s experience with “all the sense of real.” He conceives of the “main point” of his paper to be the idea that “each individual is an isolate, permanently non-communicating, permanently unknown, in fact unfound.” I suggest that the “main point” is more inclusive and might be stated as: each individual needs equally to be found (recognized, but not exposed) and to be unfound (an incommunicado isolate). Winnicott warns that when an analysis has reached the depths of the patient’s personality, interpreting destructively introduces what is “not-me” too close to the core self, so “the analyst had better wait.” An unstated question arises: what does the analyst do while he waits other than remaining silent? I respond to this question by giving clinical examples from my own clinical work. Winnicott ends the paper with the language of poetry in his effort to communicate something of the mystery of being that is “absolutely personal” to each of us. Winnicott at the end of his paper sheds the language of prose and adopts the language of poetry in what I view as his effort to communicate something of the mystery of being.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Critical and reasoned review of the psychosomatic question in French-speaking psychoanalysts**

Valérie Boucherat-Hue , Adeline Hulin and Carla Machado

Carla Machado

Paris
France
Medecine & Society

Carla Machado  

Psychosomatics, history of psychoanalysis, French psychoanalysis (non-Lacanian), Freudian theory, trauma, conceptual research, epistemology, historical research, mentalisation, qualitative research

European psychosomatics, as a branch of psychoanalytic psychopathology, has existed for almost two centuries, but it is a constantly moving body of thought. From epistemology to aetiology, its questions are still evolving and concern the debate between the psyche/soma dualism and the monistic alternative, but also the psychic meaning that is to be attributed to somatic symptoms. In France, the discipline has not had its last word, for it is constantly seeking to position itself as a field of research and clinical practice within psychoanalysis. However, it still needs to affirm itself and open itself more towards other branches of knowledge, such as medicine, neuroscience, or the social sciences. In this context, are the processes of somatisation still to be thought of as boundary objects for psychoanalysis? On what epistemological, theoretical, methodological, and practical conditions is the psychosomatic question still or at last possible? In particular, is it possible to reintroduce the question of the “meaning” of the somatic phenomenon in order to breathe new life into it, without returning, however, to the symbolic interpretation of symptoms, while preserving the subjectivising approach of psychoanalysis and its logic of interpolation? Can we speak in the human sciences of a truly psychosomatic revolution in the image of psychoanalytic advances?

responseSet.title: Can psychoanalysis contribute to the understanding of fundamentalism? An introduction to a vast question
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can psychoanalysis contribute to the understanding of fundamentalism? An introduction to a vast question

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

Croix-de-Rozon
Switzerland

Adela Abella   CONTACT Adela Abella adela.abella@bluewin.ch 217 rte. d’Annecy, 1257 croix-de-Rozon CH, Switzerland

Fundamentalism, identity, power, certainty, applied analysis, interdisciplinary, narcissism, reductionism

During the past 20 years, religious and political fundamentalism has produced a number of barbaric terrorist attacks which have strongly shaken public opinion in the Western world. Can psychoanalysis contribute to the understanding of the unconscious functioning of these fundamentalist groups? The different presentations of fundamentalism and the various hypotheses concerning its origins and aims are discussed. Should fundamentalism be understood mainly as a means for strengthening the sense of identity, as a search for power, or as a refuge in certainty? When trying to understand these phenomena, are there any pitfalls a psychoanalyst should beware of? The slippery ground of applied analysis is discussed, as well as some dangers specific to this issue, such as reductionism, theoretical hyper-saturation, wild speculative over-interpretation, and emotional/ethnocentric biases. Whereas there is general inter-field agreement concerning the absence of obvious psychopathology or of a typical sort of personality in the members of fundamentalist groups, the major contribution of psychoanalysis might be to the understanding of the particular ways in which an individual appropriates a given ideology and the weight of group dynamics. On less slippery ground, detailed clinical material is used in order to illustrate both the defensive value of a fundamentalist position taken by an adolescent and the intervention of fundamentalism in everyday psychoanalytical societies. Particularly relevant issues are suggested to be narcissism and the structuring role of relationships with the others.

responseSet.title: Delusion and reparation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Delusion and reparation

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

United Kingdom

Catalina Bronstein   CONTACT Catalina Bronstein c.bronstein@btinternet.com 7 Mackeson Road, London NW3 2LU, UK
Psychosis, delusions, hallucinations, reparation, breakdown, adolescence

This paper looks at the reparative quality of delusional systems. The Author explores and expands Freud’s notion of delusion as an ‘attempt at reparation’. Even if a delusion is mostly the consequence of hatred of reality and an omnipotent idealized construction to protect the ego from persecutory anxiety stemming from a destructive superego, its content and function show greater complexity. The function of a delusion is not just to protect the ego but also to protect the object (the analyst in the session) from the patient's violence. In order to show the coexistence in delusional systems of manic defensive aspects with proper depressive reparative ones, the Author presents detailed clinical material from the analysis of an adolescent patient who suffered from intense persecuting voices that negated her right to be alive. The paper proposes that understanding the fluctuation and manifestation of reparative unconscious phantasies, the recognition of their depressive aspects and of their specific function as they are lived out in the transference relationship is central to the development of psychic change in psychotic processes.

responseSet.title: Discussion of ‘Ending Analysis: The Case of Karl’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of ‘Ending Analysis: The Case of Karl’

Ricardo Bernardi

Ricardo Bernardi

Montevideo,
Uruguay

Ricardo Bernardi   CONTACT Ricardo Bernardi ric.e.bernardi@gmail.com Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association, Santiago Vázquez 1140, Montevideo, Uruguay 11300, Uruguay

Freud states, in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” (Freud 1937), that the end of an analysis (which is, in his opinion, an “ambiguous phrase”) requires two conditions in order to be fulfilled. Firstly, that the patient shall no longer be suffering from his symptoms, anxieties and inhibitions, and, secondly, that the changes produced prevent their repetition. If these conditions have not been achieved due to external difficulties, Freud suggests talking of an “incomplete analysis” (1937, 219). However, clinical experience also showed that other outcomes are possible. Patients can get worse during the analysis, for example, in the case of a therapeutic negative reaction. The progress of the analytic process can stop in situations of stalemate or impasse. Each analysis has, in fact, its own way of termination, as it has also its own process, history and protagonists.

From the very beginning of his work, Freud was aware that adverse events can occur. In 1900 he wrote to Fliess:

I am beginning to understand that the apparently interminable nature of the treatment is something determined by law and is dependent on the transference … such a prolongation is a compromise between being ill and being well which patients themselves desire and to which for that reason the physician should not consent. (Letter 133, quoted from Strachey, Editor’s Note to “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” [Freud 1937, 256])

When I read Mr K’s case history, I remember Freud’s description of the “compromise between being well and being bad” that compelled the analyst to consider the end of the analysis. In a session two weeks before termination, we learn that Karl’s analyst communicated to his patient his decision to end Karl’s 10-year long analysis: “While Karl argued that he still didn’t feel competent, I told him I felt that continuing the analysis strengthened his position that he remained the still neglected child who had grown up to be incompetent.” The analyst adds: “I had said that it seemed that the continuation of the analysis was in an important way an obstacle to his being able to have a sense of himself as real and his relationships as valuable.” I suggest that we keep in mind these words. They summarize the analyst’s opinion about the core of Karl’s pathology—the disturbance of his sense of self and his sense of self with others. They also call attention to the fact that there is a limit to a prolonged therapeutic effort by the analyst. We can, as analysts, recognize from our own practice these situations and the strong feelings they sometimes provoke in us. Unfortunately, in this case we are not informed about Karl’s reaction to these words, or about the analyst’s countertransferential feelings. These are two significant omissions for understanding this case.

responseSet.title: ‘I do not care to remember’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘I do not care to remember’

Leopoldo Bleger

Leopoldo Bleger

Paris

Leopoldo Bleger   leopoldo.bleger@gmail.com

In a thrilling and passionate text published in 2017 in IJP issue number 3 and presented in a plenary session at the IPA conference in Buenos Aires in July 2017, Harris (2017) reminds us that sexuality and intimacy are shot through with the forces of power and history.

The very site of the IPA conference, the Hilton Hotel in Puerto Madero, was a good example: until the 1990s, this area of the port was under military control—an immense, gated space that could only be entered by passing through an identification checkpoint guarded by armed soldiers.

What is more, a mere 10-minute walk away is the pyramid in the Plaza de Mayo (May Square), which the mothers and grandmothers of the young people tortured, killed, and disappeared by the dictatorship have been marching around every Thursday afternoon since 1977, demanding that their loved ones appear alive. It is an impossible demand, made with unprecedented courage, that has cost many of them their lives. Like in a classical tragedy, demands and courage go together. They were called “the madwomen” of the Plaza de Mayo. You had to be pretty crazy to dare to challenge military power directly. Gómez Mango (1992) has called them the “new Antigones.”

In the Argentina of the dictatorship, tanks in the bedroom were not necessary: military power was a difficult reality to ignore.

In the same spot where political prisoners were once held in Punta Carretas in Montevideo, Uruguay, just across the River Plate, a large and luxurious shopping mall now stands. Similarly, the site of the conference, former military land, is now one of Buenos Aires’ most chic areas.

As we know, victors build temples to their gods atop the temples of the vanquished. “Hilton” is one of the names of the gods of our era.

“Somewhere in La Mancha, in a place whose name I do not care to remember”1 are the first words of the founding text of Spanish literature, Don Quixote (Cervantes 2003). This “not caring to remember” (for Freud, “not wanting to know”) is not exclusive to Latin America or to any place in particular. It would be impossible to find something more human: not wanting to remember a name, a blemish,2 the blemish of a name.

responseSet.title: Unrepresented states and the challenge of historicization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unrepresented states and the challenge of historicization**

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman

Stockbridge
MA
USA
The Austen Riggs Center, 25 Main Street

Jane Tillman   Email: jane.tillman@austenriggs.net
unrepresented states, historicization, hypochondria

Bernd Nissen has been developing a metapsychology of hypochondria and ‘autistoid’ states for 20 years, working within the Kleinian object relations tradition to formulate the earliest events of psychic life. His imaginative work shows careful thinking about early catastrophic developmental experiences which may lead to various forms of psychopathology. In the current paper, Hypochondria as an actual neurosis, Nissen extends his progressive work on hypochondria and joins his evolving theory of autistoid encapsulation to a revival of interest in Freud's concept of actual neurosis, the experience of unrepresentable early trauma, the problems of historicization, and to Winnicott's description of the fear of breakdown. I first summarize Nissen's theoretical work in the area of hypochondria and autistoid encapsulation, and then comment on his current paper including the challenge of historicization in unrepresented states. Finally I consider the question of parsimony in Nissen's metapsychology.

[[RunningHead]]

Origins of a theory of psychic temporality in Freud: The study of eels and the Darwinist influence of Carl Claus

Rodrigo Cornejo

Rodrigo Cornejo

Viña Del Mar
Chile
Facultad de Humanidades y Ciencias Sociales

Rodrigo Cornejo   CONTACT Rodrigo Cornejo rodrigo.cornejo@unab.cl Facultad de Humanidades y Ciencias Sociales, Universidad Andres Bello, Escuela De Psicología, Calle Quillota 980, 5° Piso, Viña Del Mar 2531106, Chile

Psychic temporality, sexuality, eels, heterochrony, Claus

Freud’s research on sexuality was, without doubt, a paradigm shift in the temporary conception of childhood experiences in relation to adult life. From those first experiences arise mnemic material which retroactively constitute the place and function of the unconscious. The theory of a psychic temporality that governs mood process, among other things, brings into question the classic model of time in western societies. Freud’s relation to these issues is already observable in his first scientific work, where he faces issues related to the sexual differentiation and heterochrony of living beings.

responseSet.title: The trauma and disillusionment of Oedipus
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The trauma and disillusionment of Oedipus

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner   CONTACT John Steiner johnsteiner28@gmail.com
Oedipus Complex, trauma, turning a blind eye, literature

In the third day of his life Oedipus suffered a violent murderous assault in which his ankles were pierced so that he could be left to die. In fact, he was not only saved, but brought up as their son by the childless King and Queen of Corinth. My hypothesis is that a severe trauma left a significant physical and psychological scar, which was hidden beneath an illusion of a normal childhood. His background, including the cause of the infirmity of his ankles, remained obscure until it was revealed in the course of Sophocles drama. Facing the truth involved an exposure of the way an illusion had served to protect Oedipus not only from the truth of the murder and incest, but also of the fact that he had been severely traumatised. A phantasy of an ideal family commonly may serve as a defence against trauma and, as a result, facing the truth involves relinquishing the idealisation, and this may be experienced as a further trauma. In this paper, I will pay particular attention to the importance of guilt for the victim of early trauma. Severe trauma may block the acceptance of guilt and, hence, prevent the evolution of a benevolent cycle involving forgiveness and reparation.

[[RunningHead]]

Object relations approaches to autism

Maria Rhode

Maria Rhode

London
UK

Maria Rhode   CONTACT Maria Rhode maria.rhode@gmail.com Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA, UK

Introduction: Kanner’s autism, Asperger’s syndrome and the autistic spectrum

In 1943, Leo Kanner described a case series of children with severe impairments in communication as well as in emotional and social relatedness. They were often without language, did not play symbolically, and screamed if their rituals were interfered with. Kanner saw their most important characteristic as “extreme autistic aloneness.” On follow-up, most of the cohort were in institutions; a few made an unexpectedly satisfactory adjustment, though it was unclear why. Wing and Gould (1979) summarized the essential features of childhood autism as the “Triad of impairments”—impairments in social interaction, in social communication, and in social understanding and imagination, with rituals taking the place of pretend play—and this triad remains central to the description and diagnosis of the condition.

A year later, the Viennese paediatrician Asperger (1944) independently outlined the syndrome that is named after him. His children shared with Kanner’s a greater or lesser incapacity for ordinary reciprocal relationships, particularly for eye contact and empathy. Importantly, unlike children with Kanner’s autism, they had no language delay, though their language use might be atypical. Intense, idiosyncratic interests (“obsessions”) seemed to correspond to the rituals present in Kanner’s syndrome. Asperger stressed his children’s many good capacities, and described them as suffering from a fundamental disorder of the personality rather than from psychosis (Simpson 2004). In 1981, Wing put forward the idea of an “autistic spectrum” comprising both Asperger’s syndrome and autism1. Since 1971 (Kolvin 1971; Rutter and Bartak 1971), psychiatrists have contrasted pervasive developmental disorders including autism, which have their onset in early childhood, with so-called late-onset psychoses such as manic depression and schizophrenia.

The original estimate for the incidence of Kanner’s autism was 4 cases per 10,000, whereas the prevalence in the UK for the broader autistic spectrum is now thought to be about 1% or even higher (Baron-Cohen et al. 2009). Opinions vary as to whether this increase is genuine or a function of more inclusive criteria. Research on the autism spectrum is very active across many domains—genetic, biochemical, behavioural, and developmental, as well as psychoanalytic. According to the emerging consensus, it may be more accurate to speak of “autisms” rather than “autism”: different pathways can lead to similar presentations (e.g. Chawarska, Klin, and Volkmar 2008; Singletary 2015) and 702

responseSet.title: Trans-itory identities: some psychoanalytic reflections on transgender identities
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trans-itory identities: some psychoanalytic reflections on transgender identities

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

UK

UK

Alessandra Lemma   Tavistock and Portman Clinics, 120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA, UK
gender identity, transgender, adolescence

The capacity of transgender to incorporate all gender variance and sexual preferences has become a powerful tool of activism and personal identification. Rather than being an index of marginality “trans” has become a central cultural site. In this paper, I will argue that this identity label encompasses a complex range of internal psychic positions in relation to consciously stated sexual preferences and gender identifications. My aim is to explore what can appear to be in some cases a premature embracement of the empowering potential of the transgender identification through my work with under 18-year-olds who are seeking medical intervention for gender dysphoria. This can undermine the painful psychic work required to establish what transgender means for any given young person. In an external culture where to ask “why transgender” (as opposed to “how transgender”) is felt to be pathologising, working with these young people can prove difficult for the analyst. The challenge is to tread the fine line between a dialogue based on an equidistant curiosity about meaning and function that is core to an analytic approach, and a posture of implicit skepticism.

responseSet.title: Busch, F. 2018. “Searching for the Analyst’s Reveries.” The International Journal of Psychoanalysis 99:569–598
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Busch, F. 2018. “Searching for the Analyst’s Reveries.” The International Journal of Psychoanalysis 99:569–598

Barbara Stimmel

Barbara Stimmel

New York City
NY
USA

Barbara Stimmel   barbara.stimmel@mssm.edu

If Fred Busch had been on my mind during a session with a patient would it have been a reverie, a dream state, hallucination … what to think, what to do?

One of the reasons I gobbled up this paper so quickly is because I've long wanted someone really savvy to explain why “ … it's [astounding] incorporation … over the last two decades” has happened.

Busch has done a masterful job helping strong skeptics like me feel better about this chain of clinical/theoretical events. The paper is thorough, well-reasoned, and in a very subtle manner, counter-revolutionary. It demands re-reading, re-thinking, and re-finding of basic principles. It seems to me that it should be a core paper in theory, technique, and the as-yet-unresolved differences among major schools of thought.

I admire its quiet, careful, indirect, insistence that: the analyst's responsibility to know his own mind (as distinct from an amorphous fusion with that of the patient), free association, and interpretation, not to mention defense against counter resistance and countertransference(!), remain hallmarks of analytic technique.

Busch pays particular attention to Bion and Ogden, but first a word about three other significant contributors (da Rocha Barros (E.M. & E.L. and Ferro) in this area to whom he refers and whose work he admires.

While I know the Barros' work slightly, I was happy to be introduced to it more thoroughly and incisively via Busch's careful presentation of their thinking. While only a short part of this paper, its description of how they put reverie to use is enriching. And, as Busch points out, they remain in the Freudian/Kleinian mindset when it comes to symbolization and the importance of language.

Busch spends more time on Ferro's clinical work, in particular using one of his clinical examples to again make the point that transformation of primitive, “under-metabolized” words remains key to analytic progress. But perhaps more important, certainly more provocative, is Ferro's claim that Freud is clinically “useless”(!). Busch might better have placed this front and centre, rather than relegate it to a footnote. I say this because it is easy to see a disturbing layer of meaning to so much of what seems to be translation from tried and true understanding of the analytic process. Alas, it is incomprehensible to me that Ferro, an admired colleague, would make such an unfortunate comment.

responseSet.title: The ecological war: A reflection on the conflictive dimension of humankind’s relations with its environment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ecological war: A reflection on the conflictive dimension of humankind’s relations with its environment

Maud Devès

Maud Devès

CNRS EA 3522 & Institut de Physique du Globe de Paris
CNRS UMR 7154 – Université de Paris

Maud Devès   CONTACT Maud H. Devès deves@ipgp.fr Centre de Recherche Psychanalyse Médecine et Société, Bâtiment Olympe de Gouges, 8 rue Albert Einstein, Paris, 75013 France

Sigmund Freud, Anthropology, Unconscious, War

In his most recent book, Face à Gaïa, Bruno Latour expresses his concern for the mental state of the inhabitants of planet Earth. He finds it ludicrous that earthlings do not worry more about the damage that their life style causes to the environment. He declares that in the twentieth century, “this classic century of war,” humanity entered a war, a war that was “hidden by the profusion of world wars, colonial wars, and nuclear threats,” but which is also “global and total,” and “equally colonial” … an ecological war that we have already lost in failing to think about it and live through it. In this article, we propose to analyze the content of this “deep alteration of our relation to the world” that Bruno Latour exposes, and which he qualifies as madness. To do so, we will draw on contributions from psychoanalysis and on the Freudian theory of culture in particular. We will show that this can prove to be particularly fertile when it comes to thinking about the conflictual relationships that human beings maintain with their environment.

Dans son ouvrage le plus récent, Face à Gaïa, Bruno Latour exprime son inquiétude quant à l'état mental des habitants de la planète Terre. Il estime insensé que les terriens ne se préoccupent pas plus des dégâts que leur style de vie inflige à l'environnement. Il déclare qu'au vingtième siècle, « ce siècle classique de la guerre », l'humanité est entrée dans une guerre, une guerre qui était « cachée par la profusion des guerres mondiales, des guerres coloniales, et les menaces nucléaires », mais qui était tout aussi « globale et totale », et « également coloniale » … une guerre écologique que nous avons déjà perdue en ne réussissant pas à la penser et à la surmonter. L'auteure de cet article cherche à analyser le contenu de cette « profonde altération de notre relation au monde » que Bruno Latour décrit et qu'il qualifie de folie. A cette fin, l'auteur s'appuie sur les contributions de la psychanalyse et en particulier, sur la théorie freudienne de la culture. Elle montre qu'un tel apport peut s'avérer particulièrement fécond pour penser les relations conflictuelles que les êtres humains entretiennent avec leur environnement.

In seinem jüngsten Buch Kampf um Gaia bringt Bruno Latour seine Sorge um den psychischen Zustand der Bewohner des Planeten Erde zum Ausdruck. Er findet es haarsträubend, dass die Erdlinge sich wenig Gedanken machen über die Schäden, die ihr Lebensstil der Umwelt zufügt. Im 20. Jahrhundert, so Latour, „diesem klassischen Jahrhundert des Krieges“, trat die Menschheit in einen Krieg ein, der sich fast verborgen hinter der großen Anzahl an Weltkriegen, Kolonialkriegen und nuklearen Bedrohungen abspielt, aber „global und total“ und gleichermaßen „kolonial“ ist … ein ökologischer Krieg, den wir bereits verloren haben, weil wir nicht über ihn nachdenken. In diesem Beitrag plädieren wir für eine Analyse des Inhalts dieser tiefgreifenden Veränderung unserer Beziehung zur Welt, die Bruno Latour beschreibt und als einen Wahnsinn bezeichnet. Wir stützen uns dabei insbesondere auf psychoanalytische Arbeiten und die Freud’sche Kulturtheorie. Wir zeigen, dass diese sich als besonders ergiebig erweisen, wenn es darum geht, über die konflikthaften Beziehungen nachzudenken, die Menschen zu ihrer Umwelt pflegen.

Nel suo ultimo libro, intitolato Di fronte a Gaia, Bruno Latour esprime la sua preoccupazione per lo stato mentale degli abitanti del pianeta Terra. Gli pare infatti assurdo che i terrestri non si preoccupino maggiormente del danno che il loro stile di vita sta procurando all’ambiente, e sostiene che nel ventesimo secolo, “questo secolo di guerre per eccellenza”, l’umanità sia entrata in guerra – una guerra “nascosta dalla profusione di guerre mondiali, guerre coloniali e minacce nucleari” eppure al tempo stesso “globale e totale”, e “altrettanto coloniale” … una guerra ecologica che abbiamo già perso nel momento stesso in cui non siamo riusciti a pensarla e a viverla. Nel presente articolo l’Autrice, facendo riferimento a contributi psicoanalitici e in particolare alla teoria freudiana della cultura, si propone di analizzare il contenuto della “profonda alterazione del nostro rapporto con il mondo” descritta da Latour, e da lui interpretata come una forma di follia. In tal modo si mostrerà peraltro come il pensiero psicoanalitico possa rivelarsi particolarmente fertile laddove si intenda esaminare il tema del rapporto conflittuale che gli esseri umani hanno con l’ambiente in cui vivono.

Bruno Latour, en su libro más reciente, Face à Gaïa, expresa preocupación por el estado mental de los habitantes del planeta Tierra. Encuentra absurdo que los terrícolas no se preocupen más del daño que causa su estilo de vida al medio ambiente. Declara que en el siglo veinte, “este siglo clásico de guerra”, la humanidad entró a una guerra, una guerra que estaba “oculta por la profusión de guerras mundiales, guerras coloniales y amenazas nucleares”, pero que también es “global y total”, e “igualmente colonial”, … una guerra ecológica que ya hemos perdido al haber dejado de pensar sobre ella y experimentarla. En este artículo, la autora se propone analizar el contenido de esta “profunda alteración de nuestra relación con el mundo” que Bruno Latour denuncia y a la que califica de locura. Para hacerlo, la autora se basa en los aportes del psicoanálisis y en la teoría freudiana de la cultura, en particular. Se demuestra que esto puede resultar particularmente fértil cuando se trata de pensar acerca de las relaciones conflictivas que los seres humanos mantienen con su medio ambiente.

responseSet.title: The European Psychoanalytical Federation and the conference in Warsaw 2018
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The European Psychoanalytical Federation and the conference in Warsaw 2018

Heribert Blass

Heribert Blass

Heribert Blass   heribert.blass@epf-fep.eu

The European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF) is very grateful to the colleagues of the Polish Psychoanalytical Society for giving the opportunity to conduct the 31st EPF Conference in Warsaw 2018. Warsaw, with its changeful and tragic, as well as heroic, history is a most appropriate place for a cooperative exchange about the conference theme “The Origin of Life.” In the last century, Warsaw and its inhabitants suffered so many painful deaths but they were also able to survive the terrible destruction executed by the German Nazi war criminals. Warsaw had to also overcome a long period of repression by the communist system. We remember and honour the heroic resistance of the Polish Jews and all Polish citizens who fought against the Nazi barbarism and thus finally gave space for the predominance of life over death, even though very many people lost their lives. The defeat and capitulation of the German army had been a starting point for new hope and new life in Europe, even if there followed other political vicissitudes and difficulties in Poland which persist until today. The analytical traveller who arrives in Warsaw from abroad and visits the scars and resurrection of the city’s architecture, can also dare to sense the fight between life and death in the impressive Rising Museum and in the Jewish Museum. He/she will be deeply touched by a vast amount of emotions, oscillating between horror, fear of death, admiration of bravery, and relief by new hope. The traveller may be reminded of Freud’s (1915) statement:

We recall the old saying: Si vis pacem, para bellum. If you want to preserve peace, arm for war. It would be in keeping with the times to alter it: Si vis vitam, para mortem. If you want to endure life, prepare yourself for death. (SE, 14, p. 300)

But the traveller may also remember another remark in which Freud (1930) declares:

And now it is to be expected that the other of the two ‘Heavenly Powers’ [p. 133], eternal Eros, will make an effort to assert himself in the struggle with his equally immortal adversary. But who can foresee with what success and with what result? (SE 21, p 145)

responseSet.title: Freud’s “selected fact”: His journey of mourning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud’s “selected fact”: His journey of mourning

Paul Schimmel

Paul Schimmel

Roseville
Sydney

Paul Schimmel   CONTACT Paul Schimmel pschimmel51@gmail.com

In Mourning and Melancholia, written in 1915, Freud proposed a coherent and convincing theory of the nature and origin of melancholic states and offered startling new insights into the growth of the mind. Freud’s essay represented a new metapsychological formulation and the beginning of a “paradigm shift” in the still young discipline of psychoanalysis; it is recognized as containing the origins of contemporary object relations theory.

The present paper argues that this clarity of understanding and the transformative power of Freud’s essay represented the culmination of a long period of transformation within Freud himself. The evolution of Freud’s thinking and theory are considered in relation to two important periods and experiences of mourning: first, that of his “self-analysis” and mourning the death of his father, and second, his experience of disillusion and mourning in response to World War I.

The metapsychological shifts in Mourning and Melancholia will be identified, along with Freud’s synthesis of clinical observation and theory. The text challenges George Klein’s contention that Freud was never able to achieve integration between his “clinical theory” and his “metapsychological theory”. Wilfred Bion’s differentiation between the integrative thinking of the “selected fact” experience and rational logical thinking will be used to reconsider the clinical theory/metapsychological theory dichotomy.

responseSet.title: Myths of termination: What patients can teach psychoanalysts about endings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Myths of termination: What patients can teach psychoanalysts about endings**

Sara Tucker

Sara Tucker

Ohio
USA

Sara Tucker   Email: satuckermd@gmail.com

Myths of Termination: What Patients Can Teach Psychoanalysts about Endings delivers more than its title promises. In addition to examining long-held beliefs and assumptions about termination, this time from the patients’ points of view, Dr Kantrowitz documents the profound sea change in American psychoanalysis, tracing the shifts from dogmatic authoritarianism and expectations of perfection to a more tolerant and accepting stance.

Dr Kantrowitz starts with a simply but elegantly designed study of responses from 82 analysands who had terminated their treatments from less than six months to more than 50 years ago. All had volunteered to be interviewed over the telephone for one to two hours, to describe their experiences of termination in their own words in an open-ended way. She used the reports to examine certain myths she sees as continuing to influence how analysts conduct analyses and judge their work. “An overarching point I want to make in this book … is that generalizations interfere with our appreciation of both the complexity and specificity of experience” (p. 1).

From her interviews, Kantrowitz fashions an examination of views of termination and how they have changed, or not, over time. Further, she documents the seemingly endless varieties of experiences which, taken together, show the range of diversity and emotions which characterize the process of termination. She meets the challenge of extracting data from 82 interviews by creating a densely layered narrative.

The first chapter, ‘A Short History of Termination’, is a selective review of the literature focused on the variety of theories about terminating. Its thrust is to show how myths around perfection and conformity were repeatedly challenged as psychoanalysis shifted from a one-person to a two-person stance. Kantrowitz presents this history in a lively nuanced way. Interspersed are her observations about myths that persist despite clinical evidence that disputes them. She names four categories she will address in her inquiry: criteria for terminating; mourning; time, transference and the need for an ending; and post-analytic contact.

responseSet.title: Object Relations in a Love Poem. A psychoanalytic reading of Neruda
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations in a Love Poem. A psychoanalytic reading of Neruda

Ricardo Readi

Ricardo Readi

Ricardo Readi   CONTACT Ricardo Readi rreadig@gmail.com
mourning, denial, Neruda, Klein, Bion

This paper is about an interpretation of Neruda’s “Poem no 15”, one of his most important works of poetry. The author reads this poem as about the painful loss of the loved object, and the struggle to accept this. This work emphasizes the complex relation between denial and mourning, specifically between psychotic anxieties and mechanisms, and psychic growth. Object relations, as described by Klein and Bion, are used for this reading of Neruda’s poem. The author makes an effort to link this interpretation of the poem to the effect it has on the reader. The paper suggests that the common interpretation of this poem as a passionate declaration of love for a silent muse could itself express a denial that can be understood by the impact that Neruda’s poem has, in which the emotional experience conveyed in the poem can be “lived” by the reader.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic education at the crossroads, by Otto
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic education at the crossroads, by Otto

David Millar

David Millar

London

David Millar   davidgmillar@hotmail.com

Introduction

This volume of papers by Otto Kernberg covers a wide range of topics. They span 30 years of his work and a number of them voice his criticisms of psychoanalytic education. Kernberg is a highly perceptive observer of the strengths and weaknesses of psychoanalytic training, with worldwide experience, and it is helpful to have these papers gathered in a single volume. One cannot read or re-read them without enormous respect and admiration for the persistence the author has shown in drawing analysts’ attention to his concerns.

Areas of interest include: the identity of the psychoanalyst; studies of group and organizational leadership; institutional problems; authoritarian culture and personality; the pressing need to increase research in and on psychoanalysis; psychoanalytic supervision; previous efforts to modify the education system and the need for new relationships between training institutes and university settings. The lighter tone of “Thirty methods to destroy the creativity of psychoanalytic candidates,” published in the IJPA 20 years ago, offers the reader some “respite,” but this account of ways in which our training can inhibit development and stifle creativity remains a disturbing theme.

A crisis in psychoanalysis

The most recent account in the volume of Kernberg's views is the last chapter: “The Twilight of the Training Analyst System.” He believes psychoanalysis to be in a crisis and “that it is fair to state that, while psychoanalysis has assured its place as a major contribution to the culture in the twentieth century, its future as a profession and a science in the twenty-first century cannot be assumed” (p. 257). He describes a number of serious external and internal challenges to psychoanalysis in America, including the following (pp. 256–257):

that: “there has been a growing critique coming from federal funding sources, and private insurance companies of psychoanalysis as too long-term, too expensive, non-empirically validated treatment, creating a cultural shift away from psychoanalysis as an elitist pursuit for the wealthy.

responseSet.title: The Unpast, by Dominique Scarfone
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unpast, by Dominique Scarfone

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

Cambridge
MA

Howard Levine   hblevine@aol.com

The Unpast by Dominique Scarfone is one of the most important and disturbing books that I have read in recent years. Important, because it is a profound examination of the meaning of “timelessness” in the unconscious—indeed, of the meaning of the unconscious itself—and the place and role of time in psychoanalysis, psychic development and the psychoanalytic cure. Disturbing, because it is a close—and I would say brilliant—reading of Freud that promises to return his thinking to its most innovative, creative and radical frontiers. It unveils the extent to which this discomfiting Freud, the true Copernican genius who challenges our more timid Ptolomeic sensibilities and ways of looking at the world, has been inadvertently put aside as psychoanalysis has drifted away from a head-on confrontation with the ineffable, enigmatic and ultimately unknowable towards a more “rational” and seemingly “knowable” sense of what we can expect to encounter in the patients’ unconscious and what the “familiar” paths to that discovery will be.

Surely, this is a bold, perhaps even presumptuous claim, one that Scarfone, who writes with a scholarly and at times poetic demeanour, does not himself advance directly. However, one cannot follow his carefully crafted description of Freud's metapsychology to its logical conclusions, as he reminds readers of the original meaning and implications of such now ordinary and familiar terms as “remembering,” “repetition,” “action,” and “transference” without realizing the extent to which psychoanalysis has moved away from Freud’s truly revolutionary vision to a still powerful and yet tamer, more reason-based, psychological rather than psychoanalytic perspective.

The starting point for this contemporary “return to Freud,” which in this volume consists of four linked and carefully integrated essays—A Matter of Time; Repetition: Between Presence and Meaning; In the Hollow of the Transference; and The Unpast, the latter given as one of the two main reports of The 74th Congress of Psychoanalysts of the French Language, Montreal, 29 May–1 June 2014—is a comment that Freud (1933) made in his New Introductory Lectures:

responseSet.title: The Skin-Ego, by Didier Anzieu
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Skin-Ego, by Didier Anzieu

Ellen Sparer

Ellen Sparer

Paris
France

Ellen Sparer   easparer@gmail.com

Writing a review about a book written by a French psychoanalyst normally puts the reviewer in the unenviable position of having to explain what Didier Anzieu limpidly describes, very early in this almost perfect translation of “The Skin-Ego”. Anzieu (p. 4) clearly articulates the “internal conflict in psychoanalytic thought” between what is called in France a British or American pragmatic empiricism and the French structural “tendency”. A large majority of French psychoanalysts disdain the “pragmatic” position. Didier Anzieu simply “refuse(s) to take sides in this conflict” (p. 5). Taking a profoundly dialectical approach, Anzieu underlines the value of the complementarity and antagonism between the two. Furthermore, he clarifies that the Skin-ego itself is “an intermediate structure of the psychical apparatus: it mediates chronologically between the mother and the infant and structurally between the mutual inclusion of psyches in the state of original fusion and the differentiation of psychical agencies in Freud's” (p. 5) second topography. Naomi Segal uses the French terminology: the second topographical theory, although the correct translation into English would be the structural theory. The French term, the second topographical theory, maintains the intuitive dialectal differentiation in French, between the first and the second theories. One overlays the other. However, in the interest of clarity, the translator might explain this distinction.

Segal's writing is a pleasure to read, and I salute her capacity to render into readable English this complex and profoundly important French contribution to psychoanalysis. In and of itself, this is not an easy task. Her introduction is particularly useful as she presents Didier Anzieu to the reader, giving some important elements of his personal history, as well as some elements about the evolution of his work in the context of his epoch. It is unfortunate that the excellent introduction by Évelyne Séchaud to the 1985 French edition was not included. Séchaud addresses the different issues and reactions within the French psychoanalytic world to Anzieu’s Skin-ego, which would be, I believe, both fascinating and useful for non-French readers.

responseSet.title: Dire ce qui vient. Association libre et transfert [Saying what comes to mind: free association and transference]
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dire ce qui vient. Association libre et transfert [Saying what comes to mind: free association and transference]

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert   catherine@chabert.org

Jean-Luc Donnet’s latest book, Dire ce qui vient. Association libre et transfert, is a magnificent synthesis of all his works: it draws out the essentials of his metapsychological thought while retracing its dynamics. It is of course based on his rich, intense, and varied clinical practice, while also drawing on his substantial institutional experience both in public services and within the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, where he completed his psychoanalytic training and where he still holds a major position as a training analyst and titular member. Donnet is, without question, a major figure in contemporary psychoanalysis, and he belongs, powerfully and tenaciously, to an intellectual movement in France that seeks a deeper knowledge of Freud’s works, while putting them to the test of the practice and the metapsychology. For Donnet, psychoanalysis can only maintain a rigorous epistemology by taking account of the clinical practice, the method, and the metapsychology, which must be linked if any advances are to be made.

Donnet is a man who is actively engaged, on all fronts, and, since his earliest days, as a young psychiatrist during his military service in Algeria, he treated traumatic war neuroses with cathartic hypnosis and developed his theory of suggestion on the basis of this experience. During his training at the Paris Psychoanalytical Institute, he followed Lacan’s teaching until 1965, adopting above all its ‘return to Freud’, in the form of a rigorous critical reading of Freud’s texts, and the importance accorded to the role of speech and language. He also worked with Pierre Mâle, Conrad Stein, Serge Viderman, Joyce McDougall, and Piera Aulagnier, but it is with André Green in particular that he pursued a long collaboration, most notably in their book L’enfant de ça, which played a key role in opening up psychoanalysis to psychosis in France.

He has had some important institutional attachments: at an early age he was medical director of the Centre Étienne Marcel (1963–1983), which led him to work intensely and publish prolifically in the field of adolescence. He has been very active in institutional movements with the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, closely involved in inter-analytic exchanges and their theorisation, which has constituted a major pole of interest throughout his analytic career. His ties with the Paris Psychoanalytical Society grew even closer when he became medical director of the Centre de Consultations et de Traitements Psychanalytiques Jean Favreau (CTPP). There he developed his ideas on the ‘double setting’ within the institutional context—that of the analytic exchange with the analyst, and that of the consultation considered as an annexe to it.
