[[RunningHead]]

Reality and fiction – Tarantino’s filmography on the couch**

Ana Pandolfo , Carla Brunstein , Cristiano Frank , Jorge Almeida , Laura Meyer and Nyvia Sousa

Nyvia Sousa

Porto Alegre
Brazil

Nyvia Sousa  

Introduction

Working in psychoanalysis keeps us in touch with the most intimate human conflicts. Such conflicts carry with them individual experiences resulting from different stages of life, along with the overload of the intersection with culture. However, the two-way road established by mutual influences between what is pertinent to the individual and what is cultural – which could be a way to access knowledge – is undergoing a transformation process and often takes the opposite role, the one of inner ignorance.

The main theme of the 30th Latin American Congress of Psychoanalysis – realities and fictions – prompted us to think of art as a resource to understand the current culture, about which we tend increasingly not to think. We live in a time without time, a time that demands immediate responses, and in which the moments for reflection are being extinguished. The consequences appear in the form of intolerance to frustration, and not recognizing the other, both icons of our time.

The audience that comes to us is embedded in this culture, and our role as psychoanalysts is to build with them the possibility of maintaining their human condition with a quality of life that satisfies them. It seems essential, therefore, to understand as profoundly as *Paper presented at the Round Table: Power and Violence in the Scene during the I Latin American Meeting on Film and Psychoanalysis during the 30th Latin American Congress on Psychoanalysis - Fepal, Buenos Aires, 2014756

responseSet.title: The collected works of D. W. Winnicott
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The collected works of D. W. Winnicott

Rachel Chaplin

Rachel Chaplin

London
England

Rachel Chaplin   rachel.chaplin@btinternet.com

Winnicott, surprised to be the defender of “the right not to communicate” (Volume 6, 433), did of course have “a great deal to communicate” (Volume 2, 358). Indeed on the evidence of the Collected Works, he seems to have been busy talking, writing, and drawing much of the time. The sheer quantity of this communication poses two obvious challenges to the editors of the Collected Works: the scholarship required to produce an authoritative text, and the question as to what to include and what to exclude.

The scholarship challenge has been met by over 40 years’ work, originating with Clare Winnicott’s plan, after her husband’s death, to gather together some previously unpublished papers and to republish others. The task has grown since in ambition and over 40 psychoanalysts and editors, working in relays, are recognised for their contributions by Christopher Bollas (Foreword). However, there is a cohesiveness to the Collected Works, derived from the clarity of the editorial policy, which seems to have evolved as the project gathered weight and direction. Robert Adès, Assistant Editor, states this policy clearly: to be inclusive and to present the texts chronologically, according to the year in which they were written, delivered, or first published. The guiding principle here is that the Collected Works are “about the analyst, about the analyst’s work” (Volume 12, p. liv, quoting Winnicott CW 2:7:8). This policy supports the reading Scarfone recommends, a reading that captures “the mode, the logic, the very movement of Winnicott’s thinking” (Volume 4, 3).

So here, in a work of prodigious scholarship, we find collected nearly all of Winnicott’s works. Volume 1 opens with teenage Winnicott’s letters home, including an exuberantly enthusiastic letter to his sister about his initial encounters with the “astoundingly controversial axioms” of psychoanalysis (Volume 1, 27). “Works”, generously interpreted, encompasses Winnicott’s own drawings and doodles, heartfelt poems and playful limericks, and numerous photographs. The Collected Works also gathers together (in book form and online) Winnicott’s previously published books, well-known and unfamiliar papers, transcriptions of his broadcasts, book reviews, fragmentary clinical notes, obituaries, letters to colleagues and friends, and a final volume of collated bibliographies and indices. The closing text is Clare Winnicott’s moving “Reflection” on her husband’s life. As such, the Collected Works bears the marks of its origin: it has the feeling of an act of loving commemoration.

responseSet.title: Formless infinity. Clinical explorations of Matte Blanco and Bion. Preface by Owen Renik
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Formless infinity. Clinical explorations of Matte Blanco and Bion. Preface by Owen Renik

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas

Nantes
France

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas   drmsanchezcardenas@wanadoo.fr

Riccardo Lombardi is a practising analyst in Rome. The texts in this book are devoted to difficult patients, including psychotics. He has forged his own style, while drawing on Bion (1897–1975), Matte Blanco (1908–1995), and Ferrari (1924–2006), and had direct contacts with the latter two. Bion describes the protomental beta-elements which are a shapeless mixture of affect and bodily sensations outside thought. It is maternal reverie, by virtue of its alpha function, that will rid them of their excess emotion and that will open up a path for them towards perception, and then symbolization and verbalizable thought. Rather than focusing on conscious/unconscious relations, Matte Blanco prefers to give priority to the relations between asymmetrical/symmetrical modes—relations that are close to the first, but which do not overlap with them completely. To make these terms understandable schematically, let’s say that consciousness operates according to asymmetrical logic: if John is Peter’s father, Peter cannot be John’s father. This is an unequivocal, and therefore asymmetrical relationship. On the other hand, at the unconscious level, it becomes possible: Peter can be the father of John. This is seen, for example, clinically when an abused child becomes an abusive parent: subject and object are then indistinguishable. To the unconscious, let us recall, Freud ascribes the following logical rules: “ … exemption from mutual contradiction, primary process (mobility of cathexes), timelessness, and replacement of external by psychical reality … ” (Freud, 1915a, p. 187).1 Matte Blanco draws all the consequences from this: if the inside and the outside, one place and another (displacement and condensation), one moment and another (timelessness) are indistinguishable, all notion of order disappears and everything merges with everything else in the unconscious/symmetrical mode, and in particular the subject and the object. In the example given, only a Propositional Function persists, abuse, which permeates all the protagonists. Lombardi thus relies on Matte Blanco’s argument to insist on the fact that the unconscious (symmetrical) is formless and limitless (because the limit, precisely, is by definition unequivocal and ensures that everything is not confused with everything else; on the contrary, it ensures that differentiated and thus limited elements appear). And he adds that the more prominent affects and emotions are, the greater is the proportion of symmetry in the mind (an example of the infinitization of affects would be the apprehension of the breast, which, according to Melanie Klein, has extreme positive or negative qualities). Bion and Matte Blanco thus consider that one of the roles of analysis is to be able to establish an alphabetization, an asymmetrization of the infinite and formless unconscious; that of moving from a bodily and emotional protomind to alphabetization, then to a narrative form and defined thoughts. Understanding the patient’s contents and psychological conflicts is no longer the only issue under the spotlight of analysis: it is this very possibility of transformation that is found there. There are many long clinical examples illustrating this here.

responseSet.title: Laplanche, an introduction by Dominique Scarfone
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Laplanche, an introduction by Dominique Scarfone

Avgi Saketopoulou

Avgi Saketopoulou

USA

Avgi Saketopoulou   avgisaketopoulou@gmail.com

In what we have come to know as his emblematic erudite and lucid style, Scarfone has offered us a remarkable introduction to Laplanche’s ideas and, most critically, to his way of thinking. This is no small gift. Scarfone’s text is excellent in its explication of Laplanche’s complicated and radical ideas. Laplanche’s contributions to analytic theory—which have long been known to French audiences but are only more recently becoming more widely available to Anglophone analysts through Jonathan House’s press The Unconscious in Translation—have far-reaching implications for psychoanalytic metapsychology and for clinical technique. But Scarfone’s most important offering to us in this volume is the particular way in which he has written it; it is a text that provides an immersive experience into what it feels like to be working intellectually not only with Laplanche’s ideas but more precisely with his method of using ideas psychoanalytically, which for Scarfone constitutes Laplanche’s most critical bequest. This book, therefore, accomplishes much more than a solid introduction to this major analytic figure. It exposes its reader to a way of reading texts and of applying Laplanche’s method of thinking of psychic movement as following not the linear order of repetition but that of an elliptical turn, a spiral (a point to which I’ll return) in the clinical domain as well as in our very process of theory building.

778

responseSet.title: Language and speech in Melanie Klein’s work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Language and speech in Melanie Klein’s work

Gordana Vulevic

Gordana Vulevic

Gordana Vulevic   CONTACT Gordana Vulevic vulevic@eunet.rs
Melanie Klein, phantasm, iconic, Bakhtin, carnivalization of the soul

Melanie Klein’s writing style was distinctive. Many would concur that her theoretical and clinical writings are characterized by the absence of the fully developed poetic in the sense that literary theory understands this term. Although the visual poetic, as such, cannot be attributed to Melanie Klein’s style, her discours has power to evoke image. Through iconicity of her images, Melanie Klein creates then effect of visualizing the inner world of the paranoid-schizoid and depressive position, which is marked by lack of successiveness and temporality characteristic of the symbolic order. Narrative of a Child Analysis reveals that the speech of Melanie Klein becomes idiosyncratic when she attempts to introduce preverbal contents in the verbal realm. At those points of the analysis her language becomes concrete, nonmetaphoric, ridiculous and misshaped. Grotesque. In an attempt to describe the characteristics of internalized part objects and their mutual relations in Richard’s inner world, Melanie Klein constructs formulas-agglomerations which produce comic effects, such as greedy-octopus-Daddy, salmon-genital, Richard’s bomb-faeces. Thus the image of Richard’s inner world becomes isomorphic with Rabelais’ laughter-provoking, comic-grotesque images of hell. Melanie Klein (Bakhtin would say) carnivalizes Richard’s inner world so it does not seem scary but more like a “cheerful carnivalesque scarecrow.”

responseSet.title: Response to letter from Leo Bleger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to letter from Leo Bleger

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

NY
United States

Adrienne Harris   adrienneeharris@gmail.com

I have felt very grateful to the North American Program Committee (led by Maureen Murphy) of the IPA Congress in Buenos Aires last summer for their invitation to speak about Intimacy. And I am grateful now to read Leo Bleger’s discussion and to have the opportunity to reflect on the questions he is raising, particularly in regard to binaries.

Leo Bleger’s letter expands on his discussion at that meeting. It was important to me then at the meeting, and now in this letter, that I felt and feel his welcoming a psychoanalytic conversation about the impact and insertion of history and power into every avenue of intimate life. This seems more crucial now, in the United States, in regard to racial division, racism, misogyny, and the place of sexuality in public life and in the contested sites of ambition and power for women.

Leo Bleger spoke in Spanish when he discussed my paper, making a serious link to his own early history in Argentina, to what had happened in the 1970s, and to the ongoing trauma of the Disappeared. As he notes, the very spot where the Congress was held is haunted by this history.

In this letter, he is pushing me (rightly) to think more critically and more deeply about binaries, particularly the binary of gender and of sexuality. As he notes, my clinical examples are loaded with intensely claimed and lived binaries. Of course, our theories and models of gender and sexual life will need to include a variety of polarized modes of subjectivity. But I would still argue our theories must expand beyond the binary. In my paper I was very committed to a model of theory making, taken from Deleuze (1994) and Braidotti (2011), from philosophy and feminism, respectively, where there is a strong commitment to what they call “nomadic theory.” That is, theory determined on freedom, on moving across disciplines, on creating sites of intersection and interdisciplinary. The binaries of gender have a place in our understanding, but not an overarching management of what is healthy or pathological.

responseSet.title: Blind cave of eternal night: The work of mourning in Tagore's Play of Four
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blind cave of eternal night: The work of mourning in Tagore's Play of Four

Kamalika Mitra

Kamalika Mitra

Salt Lake
Kolkata
India

Kamalika Mitra   here_is_km@yahoo.co.in
Tagore, Freud, loss, mourning, father‐figure, desire

This paper correlates Sigmund Freud and Rabindranath Tagore's writings on mourning through two specific texts. Despite being contemporaries and profoundly influential, Tagore and Freud's spheres of influence have tended to be separate, so that there have been but few attempts at connecting their philosophies. This essay examines the second chapter of Tagore's novella Play of Four (Chaturanga, 1916) in the light of Freud's essay ‘Mourning and melancholia’ (1917). It explores how mourning may at once demand confirmation and denial; how it affects love and desire. The essay examines the Freudian concept of the unconscious through Tagore's symbolism; it also looks at Tagore and Freud's references to autobiographical elements and Shakespeare in their writing. The paper thus offers a close and juxtaposed reading of texts by two of the most important writers of the past century, who wrote and revolutionized our thinking about human minds and lives. In doing so, it throws new light on Tagore's novella and further proves the universality of Freud's propositions.

responseSet.authorCitation: da Rocha Barros, E. M. & Lima da Rocha Barros, E.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Klein yesterday, today and tomorrow: Reflections on her 1936 lectures in Lectures on Technique

Elias da Rocha Barros and Elizabeth Lima da Rocha Barros

Elizabeth Lima da Rocha Barros

Elizabeth Lima da Rocha Barros  

Given the way that both psychoanalysis itself and Kleinian theory in particular have changed over the past 82 years since Melanie Klein taught these recently found and published lectures, the question arises regarding their contemporary relevance. Can they actually impact psychoanalytic authors and clinicians drenched in contemporary modes of analytic thinking and practice, or are they merely of historical interest?

In our view their value goes well beyond the historical interest. They are not dead specimens only to be rarely revisited for dry academic purposes. Rather these lectures are still very much alive; they are full of relevance and still have things to say to us directly from those bygone years. In fact, they are so alive and actual that our first reaction to reading them was that of surprise. The Lectures shine new light on Melanie Klein’s thinking, lending it an open-endedness that leads us to revisit and rethink the past, present and future of Kleinian psychoanalysis. Moreover, reflection on their narrative structure and style reveals that the lectures embody a special way of thinking and building psychoanalytic arguments—structure and content of thought here being intimately tied. Of special interest also are Klein’s thoughts on the analytic attitude and her understanding and use of the concept of interpretation that are developed in the lectures. We now elaborate.

The past in the present

Julia Kristeva writes that “any text is constructed as a mosaic of quotations; any text is the absorption and transformation of another” (Kristeva 1980, p. 66). If we approach texts with this insight in mind, we could approach the contemporary concept of reverie, for example, as something already dormant in Freud, in his concept of evenly suspended attention (but without the inter-subjective component). Freud’s thinking planted a seed that was absorbed and transformed by later thinkers into something which stretches back to psychoanalysis’ origins, is a hotly debated topic today, and might well undergo future transformations in the works of the analysts of tomorrow.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative listening and the psychoanalytic process: Sensibility, engagement and envisioning, by Fred L. Griffin

Sharone Bergner

Sharone Bergner

New York
NY
USA

Sharone Bergner   E-mail:sbergnerphd@yahoo.com

Psychoanalysis and literature share the medium of language and proceed through the shaping, interrupting and deepening of narrative, but that is not all they share. As Ogden has articulated, psychoanalysis and literature both require of their protagonist couples – analysand‐analyst, and writer‐reader – imagination, which is the basis for a live attunement and receptivity from which spring “spontaneously invented or newly discovered metaphors” (Ogden, 1997). Fred Griffin's creative, interesting book, Creative Listening and the Psychoanalytic Process: Sensibility, Engagement and Envisioning, depicts good psychoanalytic listening as a generative, active process. When analysts are threatened by emotional currents in our lives or in the consulting room, or otherwise find ourselves in a darkness that collapses or obscures analytic space, we may turn, he writes, to more content‐driven models or reductive genetic interpretations, in an effort to regain the footing of certainty. The constructs that arise when working this way translate analysands into two‐dimensional ideas “now inhabiting emotionally sterile environments of theory that hold little vitality or potential for emotional growth” (p. 4). Instead, Griffin works towards a psychoanalytic listening that actively engages imaginatively and creatively. responseSet.title: On analytic endings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On analytic endings

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

San Francisco
USA

Abbot Bronstein   CONTACT Abbot A. Bronstein abbot.bronstein@gmail.com 2473 Washington Street, San Francisco, CA 94115, United States

In the many and varied stories of how analyses come to an end, we have maintained a psychoanalytic mythology about that which has come to be called “Termination.”

However, what to one analyst is a completed analysis, to another is not. Since Freud’s (1937) “Analysis Terminable and Interminable,” psychoanalysts have described many different criteria: the removal of restrictive and pathological defences, evolving into the integration and plasticity of defensive systems (Norman et al. 1976), the internalization of a self-analysing function and the ability to do self-analysis (Busch 2009), or of warded off affects and thoughts (Weiss 1993), the capacity to experience and “think” about oneself (Caper 1999). No one set of ideas or goals has emerged across different analytic schools of thought as the necessary or sufficient accomplishment marking when an analysis can end “successfully.” The idea of the analyst as a real, three-dimensional object largely replacing the transference phantasy (ies) when an analysis is nearing an end has also been suggested (Firestein 1982), also as seen in dreams (Hebbrecht 2013; Meltzer 1983; Quinodoz 1999). This comes with the notion of the resolution of the transference neurosis and its resolution (Calef 1971; Firestein 1982; Loewald 1971; Norman et al. 1976).

Psychoanalysts have described the mourning, weaning and grieving process that occurs at the end of analysis (Blum 1987; Meltzer 1967). A number of psychoanalysts also suggest that there is a recrudescence of the specific symptoms from the earliest part of the analysis when treatment comes to an end.

Many of these ideas are clinically observable and are still part of our lexicon, though with less certainty and they have become less of a requirement to deem an analysis successful.

The Analyst at Work section provides a space for taking a closer look at some actual clinical practices.

On the occasion of her 90th birthday, in London, Betty Joseph presented a brief paper called “Ending Analysis.” The International Journal has been given permission by Joseph’s family to publish this previously unpublished work, which illuminates her thinking on how we must bring an end to both our time in analysis itself and our time as analysts. Joseph reflects on what has become her most enduring clinical contribution: that of analysing transference phantasies in the here and now—observing the moment-to-moment psychic change that signifies the analytic work as it is accomplished over the course of a session, the analytic week, and indeed the entire analysis. This analytic work in the “here and now,” in her view, brings about the sought-after psychic change within the 1425

responseSet.title: Ending analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ending analysis

Betty Joseph

Betty Joseph

(1917–2013)

Betty Joseph  

Today I want to discuss the ending of the analysis of a patient which took place about 18 months ago. She had assumed, correctly, that she was one of my last analytic patients and that this meant that the ending would have particular resonance not only for her but for me as I would be facing the ending of my actual analytic career. In this she was of course right, and I hope to discuss some of the implications later.

Ordinarily we take for granted that as we approach termination, old forms of behaviour, old problems tend to recur, though we hope in a more modified form. What I am particularly interested to discuss in this case is the emergence of basically near-psychotic behaviour, anxiety and defences stirred by the ending. This enabled us to get more understanding of the relationship between the near-psychotic functioning and the rigid character structure that for so long dominated the analysis, as well as to gain more idea about the role played by her actual family experiences in building her personality.

Mrs T, a teacher with a highly responsible position, came to analysis in her late 30s. The treatment lasted for 10 years (during which time she had been gravely ill in a period roughly coinciding with, but extending beyond, one summer holiday break, and later I had been away ill for most of one term).

Throughout the early years of the analysis I was struck by the contrast between her constant lateness and yet her otherwise apparent easy compliance. She talked fairly readily and almost any interpretation was apparently accepted, but the talking seemed superficial, often almost chatting, and my interpretations often seemed to me almost facile and superficial and not to take us much further, though my patient seemed quite satisfied. Although in the analysis Mrs T seemed a pleasant, friendly, easy person, I began to hear about her getting caught up in situations at work and at home where there were rows, difficulties; she seemed to become disliked and seen as troublesome. It became clearer then that she must somehow be behaving in a provocative, quietly paranoid manner. Although the analysis progressed and her life improved altogether, the apparently superficial and compliant behaviour in the analysis would return and persist over quite long periods.

responseSet.title: Discussion of ‘Ending Analysis: The Case of Karl’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of ‘Ending Analysis: The Case of Karl’

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman

London
UK

Michael Feldman   CONTACT Michael Feldman mmf@mmfeldman.co.uk British Psychoanalytical Society (Incorporating the Institute of Psychoanalysis), 32 Southwood Avenue, London N6 5RZ, UK

This is a moving account of an analysis with a man who began his treatment in his early fifties. The article begins with detailed material from a session in 2005, the first year of his analysis, and concludes with a session in the tenth year of his analysis, two weeks before termination.

The material initially focused on the patient’s repeated complaints about his marriage. He was dependent on his wife, and relied on her for advice concerning his work, and he was terrified she would leave him. At the same time, he was fearful that she would use her power to control him.

The patient described how, in his family of origin, anything a child accomplished or enjoyed was put down or mocked. He spoke in a critical and devaluing manner about both his parents, and he complained that he had been deprived of love or attention. His parents divorced in his early teens, and his mother soon married an older man, and seemed to begin to enjoy life more.

The patient had a persistent and “vigorous” claim to being damaged and inadequate, as a lover and in his work. It was as if he had internalised a critical, devaluing and mocking presence, while at the same time revealing a very elevated opinion of himself, his power and his capacities. The analyst describes the pressure he was put under either to side with the patient in relation to his marital quarrels, or to become critical of his treatment of his wife. Indeed, throughout this account, the analyst conveys the various ways in which he was repeatedly invited, or indeed pressured, to criticise and to judge the patient, almost always in a negative fashion.

It became clear how necessary, even gratifying it was for the patient repeatedly to construct this familiar, sadomasochistic relationship with the analyst. On a number of occasions when there was a threat that the patient would come into contact with depression, pain or guilt, he would evoke an (often) unconvincing negative or masochistic version of the situation and invite the analyst to join in with criticism and attack.

responseSet.title: In Writing, by Adam Phillips
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Writing, by Adam Phillips

Susanne Lansman

Susanne Lansman

London
UK

Susanne Lansman   info@susannelansman.co.uk

In writing, by Adam Phillips, a psychotherapist and psychoanalytic writer, is a collection of essays many of which were originally delivered as individual lectures at a number of universities. As is typical of Adam Phillips’ approach to the reader, the book is an invitation to have a conversation and see where it takes you. In a series of interviews with Paul Holdengräber (The Paris Review) over a number of years, Phillips comments that he either has reviewers describing him (Holdengräber 2014) as narcissistic and empty, or his books are found to be interesting (p. 259). He sets himself up as a writer of books that will evoke an extreme response or divide opinion. It is a challenge aimed at anyone reviewing his work that might dismiss him as shallow, but also offers up his books for the reader to find something that resonates.

Adam Philips makes the case that the relationship between psychoanalysis and poetry is one way. Freud may have been open about the inspiration for his method coming from Goethe, and from the philosopher-poet Schiller who observed that hunger and love held the mechanism of the world together, but creative patients come into therapy with great fear. Will analysis kill the creative impulse, or will understanding dry out the creative juices? The phrase: “If they lose their devils they will lose their angels” (p. 8) suggests psychoanalysis is a contagion, a language that gets into creative work and ruins it. He talks about a psychoanalytic understanding of sexuality versus an understanding of economic pressures and anxiety.

For Pound, Freud makes people write about sex when they should be writing about economics; for Eliot, Freud makes us write too definitively about unknown terrors and mysteries (‘the material is so clearly defined’). Psychoanalysis is the wrong language in the wrong place; too prescriptive, too knowing. (p. 3)

Phillips’ focus is on the language that stifles expression. But then he turns on his heel, which he does many times in the book, and unseats the reader who may have gone along with such criticism. He writes that psychoanalysis might be warning us, ‘How languages can take over … ” (p. 7).

The surprising modernity of Klein’s Lectures on Technique and Clinical Seminars: Putting them in context

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Stuttgart,
Germany
Abteilung für Psychosomatische Medizin

Frankfurt a.M.,
Germany

Heinz Weiss   CONTACT Heinz Weiss heinz.weiss@rbk.de Abteilung für Psychosomatische Medizin, Robert-Bosch-Krankenhaus, Auerbachstraße 110, Stuttgart D-70376, Germany

transference-situation, analytic attitude, projective identification, counter-transference

Focusing on the vividness and modernity of Klein's 1936 lectures the author argues that Klein was very aware of the complexity of the transference-situation and the analyst's involvement in it. The Clinical Seminars of 1958 show that the concept of projective identification helped to further clarify her ideas and to anticipate later developments. Nevertheless she remaines sceptical about the counter-transference simply seen as a response to the patient's projections and warned against re-projecting it back into the patient.

responseSet.title: Common sense: Its uses, misuses, and pitfalls
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Common sense: Its uses, misuses, and pitfalls

Richard Zimmer

Richard Zimmer

New York
NY
USA

Richard Zimmer   CONTACT Richard B. Zimmer RBZ1@cumc.columbia.edu 952 Fifth Avenue, Apt. 2D, New York, NY 10075, USA

intersubjectivity, countertransference, enactment, phantasy, theory of thinking, bastion

“Common sense” is ubiquitous in most discourse, including the psychoanalytic process. It is comprised of multiple different forms of thinking which have certain similarities of organization. Its appearance in interpersonal discourse evokes typical affects, fantasies, and intersubjective experiences, which I call “the feeling of common sense”. Because of its underlying conceptual structure and its powerful affective and intersubjective components, as well as the underlying conceptual organization of the various forms of thinking associated with it, common sense can have a variety of different functions in the analytic process; some serve its forward movement, while others function, in various ways, as impediments which need to be understood and unravelled. Such understanding sheds light not only on the patient’s internal dynamics, but the analyst’s collusive participation in enactments at many levels. Such awareness may shape the analyst’s technical decisions as much as the content of his or her interpretations. I offer clinical illustrations of a number of these phenomena.

responseSet.title: Hypochondria as a self-destructive attack on the subject’s vitality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypochondria as a self-destructive attack on the subject’s vitality**

Ute Rupprecht-Schampera

Ute Rupprecht-Schampera

Tübingen
Germany

Ute Rupprecht-Schampera   CONTACT Ute Rupprecht-Schampera ute.rupprecht-schampera@dpv-mail.de Schönbuchstraße 10, 72074 Tübingen, Germany

There is much to be said for Nissen’s (2017) model of “hypochondria as an actual neurosis,” which sees the genesis of hypochondriacal anxieties as the end-product of a long series of defensive operations and a sign of an autistoid dynamic between the self, a malignant introject, and the hypochondriacally cathected organ—a model that seeks to integrate concepts due to Freud (1895, 1896, 1911, 1914), Winnicott (1971, 1974), Bion (1959, 1962, 1970), Rosenfeld (1958, 1964, 1971, 1987), Baranger, Baranger, and Mom (1988), and others. However, Nissen regards his model as primarily applicable to severe forms of hypochondria and hypochondriacal anxieties as a final substitutive formation directed against the unbinding of encapsulated contents resulting from earlier instances of traumatic collapse. In the tradition of Freud (1895), Klein (1932), Bion (1962), and Rosenfeld (1964), Nissen assumes that, in hypochondriacal anxieties concerning the body, a failure of the mechanism of projection drastically impairs the symbolizing function of the individual, so that the hypochondriac can undertake little or no symbolic elaboration of his psychic experience.

As a contribution to a discussion of this model I should like to outline an alternative conception of hypochondria (Rupprecht-Schampera 2003), to present some clinical examples of my own, and to consider some consequent objections.

Let me begin by anticipating some fundamental theoretical objections. First, there is in my view little point in devising a specific genetic or psychodynamic explanation for individual clinical pictures of a pathology—e.g. “a particularly severe form” of hypochondria. Instead, the requirement is for consistent investigation and description of the various forms of a pathology that appear phenomenologically to belong together “with a view to establishing their common denominator.” That is ultimately the only way to delimit and validate the field of a given complex of symptoms or indeed of a nosological category, while also ensuring that the theory can perform its function of reducing the volume of data involved and of revealing higher-order patterns in the observed diversity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Melanie Klein archive: Klein’s further thoughts on loneliness**

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

London
UK

Jane Milton   CONTACT Jane Milton jmilton@waitrose.com British Psychoanalytical Society, 6 Narcissus Road, London NW6 1TH, UK

Melanie Klein, archive, loneliness

The author brings to light previously unpublished material from the part of the Melanie Klein archive that deals with the subject of Klein’s last, posthumously published paper “On the sense of loneliness”. Here are found four differing versions of the loneliness paper, written between 1958 and 1960, and prepared for spoken presentations to different audiences. The author gives evidence from Klein’s copious additional notes that she intended to write a whole book or monograph dealing with the topic of loneliness from a psychoanalytic point of view. At the time of Klein’s death, as well as elaborating her own thinking on the topic of loneliness, she was gathering and incorporating the comments of a number of close colleagues on her work. Previously unpublished letters to Klein from Wilfred Bion and Elliott Jaques are included in this paper, as are extracts from Klein’s own notes, organized under a number of headings. Without attempting to analyse the material in any depth, the author suggests that some of the themes Klein was working on may have had particular relevance for her personally in what turned out to be the very last months of her life.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An introduction to Michel Fain’s thought**

Marilia Aisenstein

Marilia Aisenstein

Marilia Aisenstein   CONTACT Marilia Aisenstein marilia.aisenstein@gmail.com
Michel Fain, Education Section, Significant Figures

Some biographical elements

Michel Fain was born in Paris on 29 November 1917 at 95 rue du Faubourg Saint-Antoine. This address is not without importance because it was the ultimate stage of a long and tumultuous emigration that had led his father from Moscow to the Sentier, a famous textile district. His mother, a worker in haute couture, had herself emigrated to France on account of the economic crisis and unemployment that was rife in Belgium.

Now, as Michel Fain recounted, his parents’ marriage was marked from the outset by a conflict. His mother, a pure Walloon, was not well accepted by his father’s Jewish family, especially as Jewishness is transmitted by women—a Jewishness, moreover, that was denigrated by his mother’s relatives. We can already see in the making something of a position apart, or in-between, which would always be one that Michel Fain held. Indeed, he did not want to belong to any particular school. If he turned towards medical studies, it was due to his father’s firm refusal to accept young Michel’s own choice; he had wanted to be a pharmacist. This profession attracted him because he thought it would allow him a lot of freedom. He imagined getting someone to manage his pharmacy and having plenty of time to “stroll” through the city and life, to follow his own thoughts and look around … His father’s opposition had consisted of a single sentence which Michel Fain said could be understood as an interpretation of the unconscious compromise contained in his vocation: “A shopkeeper, that’s out of the question!” But that’s exactly what his father was in the Sentier district. Years later, having been successively the Director of the Institute of Psychoanalysis and then President of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society (SPP: Société psychanalytique de Paris), Michel Fain was always proud of “defending the shop,” whilst not being (and I will come back to this) very inclined towards ecumenism or any kind of demagogy. He pursued his medical studies from 1937 to 1945, during the dark years of the Second World War which were made even more painful due to Michel Fain’s semi-Jewishness as well to his status as a child of parents who were both foreign. He did not like to dwell on this period, simply saying that during it he had suffered a great deal. Michel Fain liked medicine, was never a psychiatrist, and his insistence on psychoanalytic and psychiatric semiology stemmed from his practice as well as from a certain view of medical thinking to which he was still attached. Of those post-war years during which he practiced general medicine, he liked to say that that they were essentially marked by his friendship with Pierre Marty. From being friends, they would soon become relatives because Pierre Marty married Simone, Michel Fain’s sister. It was *Translated by Andrew Weller.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mentalisation and passivity by Michel Fain

Marilia Aisenstein and Marina Papageorgiou

Marina Papageorgiou

Marina Papageorgiou  

Michel Fain (1917–2007) was an eminent figure of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society (he was both its President and also the Director of the Institute), and had a profound influence on French psychoanalysis. Introducing the work of this great thinker is no simple task. His first contributions date back to 1950. His writing is difficult, because his texts are always very condensed. His work is subtle and complex; never systematic and doctrinaire. It is a way of thinking that is in search of itself, that wanders and rediscovers its internal coherence, without giving the impression that the author is pursuing a project.

In Michel Fain (Aisenstein 2000), I wrote that this author strolls through metapsychology and clinical practice in the manner of Rameau's Nephew,1 of whom Diderot said that he compared his ideas to “beautiful strollers” (“belles promeneuses”), always ready as he was to follow them, whether wise or mad … His work does not claim to be built or organised around a guiding theme. It could be compared to a musical sequence in which the multiplicity of the themes discussed and their diversity allows for an interlacing of encounters and cross-fertilisations that are forever becoming richer. Reading his work is arduous, because Michel Fain was a theoretician who was demanding both on himself and his reader, whose task he refused to make simpler. No concessions are made to style or clarity. Michel Fain prided himself on not writing for disciples, but for his peers. That is why he implied what he considered should be known, rather than saying it; he did not explain, and boasted of being ignorant in the art of developing ideas in writing.

His first texts had a clear orientation towards psychosomatics: “Abords psychosomatiques de l’hypertension arterielle dite essentielle” [Psychosomatic approaches to so-called essential high blood pressure] (Fain 1950); “Le facteur conflictuel dans l’étiologie des ulcères gastroduodénaux” [The conflictual factor in the aetiology of gastroduodenal ulcers] (Fain 1951); and “Notes sur certain aspects psychosomatiques de la tuberculose pulmonaire” [Notes on certain psychosomatic aspects of pulmonary tuberculosis] (Marty and Fain 1954). And yet, as early as 1954, certain articles were oriented towards a more strictly classical approach, for instance, “Contribution à l’étude du voyeurisme” [Contribution to the study of voyeurism] (Fain 1954). In this short text, we see him using his experience as a psychosomatician with a thorough knowledge of psychic functioning in order to enrich the psychoanalytic semiology of the neuroses.

responseSet.title: Winnicott’s theory of playing: a reconsideration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott’s theory of playing: a reconsideration

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand   CONTACT Marie Lenormand marie.lenormand@univ.amu.fr
Play, Child psychoanalysis, Winnicott, Klein

“Playing is itself a therapy,” argues Winnicott, in one of the most famous phrases in the history of psychoanalysis. Despite its seductiveness, this paper suggests that this powerful proposition should be reconsidered. Winnicott’s extraordinary ability to transmit his theory in jargon-free language should not conceal the singularity of his conception of playing. Questioning the triad of play/playing/game may, therefore, be theoretically illuminating and clinically constructive. After examining (1) the stumbling blocks of Winnicott’s concept of playing and the negative part implied by its dialectics, this paper will highlight (2) the markers Winnicott offers us to identify the reasons behind some failures he noted, and (3) their consequences on the reception of play activity in the cure. This will lead us to propose a discontinuity between the play activity phenomenologically speaking (what we call “play”) and what Winnicott defines as the event of discovering the self through creative experience (what he calls “playing”) in order to rethink the question of what is “therapeutic.” Our conclusion that “some types of play without playing may also have a therapeutic function” will show the distance travelled in relation to Winnicott’s initial proposition.

responseSet.title: “Psychoanalysis partagé”: Winnicott, The Piggle, and the set-up of child analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Psychoanalysis partagé”: Winnicott, The Piggle, and the set-up of child analysis

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand

Aix en Provence
France
Laboratoire de rattachement: LPCPP, EA3278 “Laboratoire de Psychologie Clinique, de Psychopathologie et de Psychanalyse,”

Marie Lenormand   CONTACT Marie Lenormand marie.anne.lenormand@gmail.com 29, avenue Robert Schuman, 13621 Aix en Provence Cedex 1, France
Piggle, child analysis, Winnicott, set-up, Lacan

Forty years after its publication in 1977, The Piggle has recently provoked new interest among researchers. Most notably, the real “Gabrielle” has shared her story with D. Luepnitz and the analytical community. Joining in this vibrant discussion, this paper aims at shedding new light on the case by focusing on The Piggle as a “set-up,” namely on the heterogenous ensemble of discursive and non-discursive elements which determined the analytical process. This reconsideration, although grounded in a Lacanian framework, supposes opening a dialogue between the French and English-speaking literature on the case. Three issues are at stake. First the paper intends to uncover the logic at work in the cure, studying it from the perspective of the analyst and the child, but also from that of the parents. Second, this triple focus is key to a reconsideration of the set-up of psychoanalysis with children more generally—in so far as it questions the function of infantile neurosis and what Winnicott calls “psychoanalysis partagé.” Third, it will look at the powerful effects of co-writing and co-publishing with the parents—considered as part of the set-up itself rather than exterior to the process of the cure—on the analytical outcome.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sándor Ferenczi’s multiple confusions of tongues and their influence on psychoanalytical thinking**

Nicolas Evzonas

Nicolas Evzonas

Nicolas Evzonas   CONTACT Nicolas Evzonas nicolas.evzonas@gmail.com
Ferenczi, Freud, Trauma, Sexual Abuse, Analytic Relationship, multilingualism

Using a poststructuralist model, this article explores the lecture given by Ferenczi and published under the title “Confusion of Tongues between Adults and the Child—(The Language of Tenderness and Passion).” By initially focusing on the closed structure of the text, the author identifies two types of confusion of tongues that are closely interlinked: the confusion between adults and the child, and that between the analyst and the analysand. By then placing the manuscript within the corpus of Ferenczi, he connects it to the latter’s multilingualism and pleads in favour of autobiographical determinants for psychoanalytic conceptualizations. This positioning of the text in its historical framework also enables it to be situated in the context of the metapsychological confusion of tongues between Freud and Ferenczi, and to delimit the influence of Ferenczi’s ideas in psychoanalytic posterity.

responseSet.title: Klein’s technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Klein’s technique

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg   CONTACT Jay Greenberg jaygreenberg275@aol.com
Psychoanalytic technique, epistemology, countertransference, projective identification, Melanie Klein

Reading these heretofore unpublished works by Melanie Klein is certain to surprise their readers, regardless of their previous familiarity with her work. Bound though she was to an earlier vision of the psychoanalytic situation and to an epistemology that today may strike many analysts as archaic, Klein’s ideas remain fresh and provocative. In this commentary I sketch out the controversies in which she was involved at the time of the lectures and seminars, and discuss how we might think about them in light of contemporary developments.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A cat is not a battleship: thoughts on the meaning of “neuropsychoanalysis”

Brett Clarke

Brett Clarke

Cincinnati
OH
USA

Brett Clarke   CONTACT Brett H. Clarke brettclarke55@gmail.com 3001 Highland Avenue, Ste D, Cincinnati, OH 45219, USA
Hermeneutic, scientism, epistemology, subjectivity, neuropsychoanalysis, phenomenology

As a conspicuously hybrid entity, neuropsychoanalysis enjoins one to look critically at its assumptions about knowledge and subjectivity as one tries to understand how its un-hyphenated halves relate to one another. The author looks at the differences between mind (which is grounded in subjective experience) and brain (which is an objectively described neurobiological entity), and suggests that neuropsychoanalytic writers are inclined to acknowledge but then disregard the unique, irreducible nature of lived experience, and the fundamental differences between the psychoanalytic mind (which requires an experiencing subject) and the brain (which is a neuronal aggregate). The author offers a philosophical basis for contending that there are potential dangers for psychoanalysis when neuroscience is misrecognized in its fundamental differences and injudiciously employed as a psychoanalytic partner in order to answer questions that properly belong to the language and conceptual architecture of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The debate on frequency: Can low-frequency psychoanalysis be free-floating?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The debate on frequency: Can low-frequency psychoanalysis be free-floating?**

Bernd Nissen

Bernd Nissen

Berlin

Bernd Nissen   Bernd.Nissen@gmx.de

What characterizes understanding in psychoanalytic processes is that it develops in a joint process and then appears directly to both participants. This understanding process in statu nascendi and in vivo distinguishes psychoanalysis from all other therapeutic procedures.

How can a treatment situation come about in which the unconscious manifests in the transference relationship in presentia? Or to put the question another way, what are the necessary preconditions for this kind of situation of direct understanding of psychic structure and dynamics?

Free-floating attention and free association

To answer this question, we must examine more closely both the key psychoanalytic methods discovered by Freud that are valid to this day: free-floating attention and free association.

Free-floating attention is the necessary counterpart to the demand made on the patient that he should communicate everything that occurs to him (1912, p. 112). Freud goes on to write, with dry masterliness: “He should withhold all conscious influences from his capacity to attend, and give himself over completely to his “unconscious memory.” Or, to put it purely in terms of technique: “He should simply listen, and not bother about whether he is keeping anything in mind.”” Therefore: no strained attention, no intentional noticing, but an artificial fading out (Freud in a letter to Andreas Salome, 1916, 1980, p. 327). We must keep everything similarly suspended in free-floating attention while also allowing our intuition to work.

The patient’s freely associated communications are searched in a free-floating way, to identify his unconscious.

Freud writes:

Just as the patient must relate everything that his self-observation can detect, and keep back all the logical and affective objections that seek to induce him to make a selection from among them, so the doctor must put himself in a position to make use of everything he is told for the purposes of interpretation and of recognising the concealed unconscious material without substituting a censorship of his own for the selection that the patient has forgone. To put it in a formula: he must turn his own unconscious like a receptive organ towards the transmitting unconscious of the patient … so the doctor’s unconscious is able, from the derivatives of the unconscious which are communicated to him, to reconstruct that unconscious, which has determined the patient’s free associations. (1912, p. 115)

responseSet.title: Repetition in the service of the erotisation of suffering. When addiction enters into the relationship to work: Theoretical considerations on a clinical case
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition in the service of the erotisation of suffering. When addiction enters into the relationship to work: Theoretical considerations on a clinical case**

E. Pelladeau and J.-B. Marchand

J.-B. Marchand

Poitiers
France

J.-B. Marchand   J.-B. Marchand jean-baptiste.marchand@hotmail.fr Maître de conférences en psychologie clinique et psychopathologie, Université Caen Normandie, LPCN – EA 7452, UFR de Psychologie, Esplanade de la paix, 14032 Caen, France
Addiction, work, erotisation, suffering, mastery

This article tries to reconsider the relationship to work that one can develop to the point when it impacts the intimate sphere. To do so, it shall question the role of the sexualisation of the suffering between subject and object of a work/job that has become the vector of an enjoyable torment. The study will focus on the clinical case of a patient who is being treated in psychoanalytical psychotherapy (she started the therapy 5 years ago). This patient maintains a relationship to her work that could be defined as “addictive.” The approach will be to use the psychodynamics of work (Dejours, 1992. “Pathologie de la communication. Situation de travail et espace public: le cas du nucléaire.” In Pouvoir et légitimité, Raison pratiques, edited by A. Cotterau and P. Ladrière, vol. 3, 177–201. Paris: Éditions de l’EHESS; 2003. L’évaluation du travail à l’épreuve du réel: Critiques des fondements de l’évaluation. Paris: INRAA Editions) in perspective of Paul Denis’s work on satisfaction and influence. Therefore, it will clinically illustrate the process inherent in the subversive and intimate perversion of the relationship to work: between resistance and suffering serving a satisfaction for which the relations of impulsive subordination (between means and goal) have been inverted to the benefit of a repetitive compulsion.

responseSet.title: Thin-skinned or vulnerable narcissism and thick-skinned or grandiose narcissism: similarities and differences
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thin-skinned or vulnerable narcissism and thick-skinned or grandiose narcissism: similarities and differences††

Ricardo Bernardi and Mónica Eidlin

Mónica Eidlin

Montevideo
Uruguay

Mónica Eidlin  

Narcissism, identity, self

This paper explores two clinical forms or aspects of narcissism and their psychopathological implications: thin-skinned, or vulnerable, narcissism (VN) and thick-skinned, or grandiose, narcissism (GN). The different names used and the characteristics with which they have been described are reviewed. Clinical vignettes are examined for both types of narcissism, emphasising their diagnostic characteristics and discussing their similarities and differences, in addition to the factors that may confer additional complexity to the clinical case. The importance of the experiences of shame and humiliation are emphasised. Based on these comparisons, the psychodynamic mechanisms at play are examined in both cases, especially their relation to the levels of mental functioning. The paper concludes that both VN and GN constitute failed ways to face the difficult dialectic articulation between self-affirmation and acknowledgement of the other.

responseSet.title: The construction of sadomasochism: Vicissitudes of attachment and mentalization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The construction of sadomasochism: Vicissitudes of attachment and mentalization

Douglas Chavis

Douglas Chavis

Rockville
MD
USA
Washington-Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis

Douglas Chavis   CONTACT Douglas A. Chavis dougchavis@gmail.com 6420 Danville Court, Rockville, MD, USA

Attachment, Sadomasochism, Infant Research, Mentalization

This paper integrates empirical developmental research with the clinical theory and treatment of sadomasochism. Three strands of argument illustrate the thesis that sadomasochistic relatedness develops from relational procedures originating in the mother–infant dyad. The first strand involves attachment research including data from infancy to adulthood and videotaped microanalyses of infant–mother dyads. The second strand addresses research on mentalization and its relationship to attachment. These strands suggest that sadomasochistic relatedness exists on continua from minor to severe as attachment modes move from secure to insecure to disorganized, and from the capacities for mentalized to prementalized recognition of the self and other. Specific relational procedural models representing insecure and disorganized attachment involve the regulation of contradictory emotions, motives, and messages conveyed through varied sensory modalities. These models suggest dissociative procedural ways of being with the other and managing internal states. They are incorporated into and shaped by later development. The third strand uses a clinical case to deepen these constructs, and illustrate the use of knowledge from infant research in work with adults. The discussion addresses the relationship of mother–infant interactions with sadomasochism and how sadomasochistic procedural relational models may contribute to the formation of sadomasochistic relatedness at different developmental levels.
